title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: Last year, Ukraine's's anti-corruption ecosystem was often in the spotlight of the media and society at large. It is difficult to underestimate the role played by the development and even Preservation of the independence of institutions responsible for combating corruption in Ukraine's at critical moments, especially during a full-scale war. the development and even Preservation of the independence of institutions responsible for combating corruption in the country at critical moments, especially during a full-scale war has only reinforced the demand for independent, capable, and honest institutions that are able not only to resist corruption but also to deliver real results. It is in this context that the reform of the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA) took place.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: the reform of the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA) was a response to long-standing criticism of the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA)'s effectiveness, allegations of corruption, and low public trust. At the same time, the reform of the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA) is not only an internal demand of Ukrainian society, but also one of Ukraine's's key international commitments in the field of anti-corruption. On July 30, a new law on the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA) came into force, introducing an updated asset management system, external audit of the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA)'s activities, and, crucially, an open competition for the position of the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA) Head.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: The implementation of the reform of the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA) was stipulated in the Ukraine Facility Plan and became one of Ukraine's's significant achievements on the path to European integration in 2025. Our society seeks strong, capable, and independent institutions that are responsible for combating corruption, restoring justice, and repatriating criminally obtained funds to the budget. Holding open and transparent competitions for the heads of such institutions is one of the essential components of forming a strong anti-corruption ecosystem as a whole. Ukraine has already developed a number of best practices in competitions for top positions in various bodies. This positive experience has been reflected in the newly adopted law on ARMA.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: We are currently conducting a competition for the Head of ARMA. ARMA plays a very important role in the criminal justice system, but unfortunately, in recent years, many questions have arisen regarding both the work of ARMA and the effectiveness of ARMA's leaders. And you should agree that the role of ARMA is especially critical in difficult times to ensure that every hryvnia is used to help Ukraine fight and survive this war. In order to implement the law on ARMA and conduct a competition for the head of ARMA under the new rules, a Selection Commission was formed to select a candidate for the position of ARMA Head, which I have the honor of chairing.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: a Selection Commission consists of six members, three of whom, namely Wolf-Tilman Baumert, Rita Simoes, and myself, were nominated by international donors who support the implementation of anti-corruption initiatives in Ukraine. The other three members of a Selection Commission were nominated by Ukraine, namely Serhii But, Anzhelika Krusian, and Valentyna Lukianets-Shakhova. All members of the Commission are professionals with many years of experience and are well aware of the importance of the task ahead of us. This design of the Commission, as provided for by law, makes it possible to minimize the risks of potential pressure or attempts to influence the Commission's decisions.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: the Commission is a collegial body that makes decisions jointly by open vote at meetings that anyone can watch live. Moreover, a decision can only be made if at least four members of the Commission vote in favor, two of whom are appointed on the recommendation of international partners. We understand the skepticism that has historically existed regarding competitions for government positions. At the same time, we are convinced that maximum openness of the process, clear selection logic, and public justification of decisions are the best response to the skepticism that has historically existed regarding competitions for government positions. The competition for the position of ARMA Chair is being conducted precisely according to these principles.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: For the first time in the history of ARMA, thanks to changes in the ARMA Law, not only lawyers by education, but also economists can participate in The competition for the position of ARMA Chair. This is important because most of ARMA's functions are related to the management of seized assets, interaction with the business environment, etc. It also significantly expands the pool of potential professionals who can head ARMA. In general, the requirements are fairly standard: We are currently accepting documents from applicants until January 19. A detailed list of required documents can be found here. We will then check required documents for compliance with the basic requirements of the law and begin the testing and interviewing stages with the candidates.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: The goal is to find the strongest candidate to be proposed to the government for appointment as Head of ARMA. We are looking for a true leader, a responsible and competent person who is honest and motivated to work hard for the benefit of the state. In order to determine the most suitable candidate for the position, we will first conduct a series of tests, namely testing knowledge of legislation, testing general abilities, and completing a written practical assignment. All of these stages will take place offline in Kyiv. The Commission will announce all specific dates and details of these stages of the competition in advance on The Commission's official social media pages and on the website of the Cabinet of Ministers.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: Furthermore, The Commission has already approved the procedure for conducting the competition and the methodology and criteria for evaluating future candidates. For the future Head of ARMA, knowledge of relevant national legislation, international standards, and EU acts, as well as analytical and communication skills, are important. Equally important for The Commission is the extent to which the candidate can demonstrate the candidate's leadership abilities. It is also worth noting that the assessment will take into account the candidate's's demonstrated commitment to independence, integrity, impartiality, and compliance with ethical standards, as well as the candidate's's ability to act honestly and in a manner consistent with the public responsibilities of the position.

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: Based on the results of these three types of tests and additional integrity checks, the Commission will interview the candidates who are admitted to the final stage and select one or two winners of the competition, whose candidacies will be submitted by the Commission to the Cabinet of Ministers for appointment to the position of the Head of ARMA. Today, ARMA needs not just a formal manager, but a strong leader and administrator capable of taking responsibility for profound institutional transformation, building ARMA's effective work, and restoring trust in ARMA — both within the country and in front of international partners. If you have the necessary experience, motivation, and inner readiness to work for results, the Commission sincerely encourage you to apply for the competition!

title: New rules – new leadership: finding the right person to head Ukraine's asset recovery agency
text: Kateryna Ryzhenko – Chair of the Selection Commission for the position of the Head of ARMA, Deputy Executive Director for Legal Affairs at the Commission

title: International law is a toothless tiger: Venezuela as another showcase
text: According to the UN Charter, the United States''s military intervention in Venezuela is illegal. The use of force is permitted only with UN Security Council approval or in response to a direct armed attack on a member state. Nonetheless, the United States' military intervention in Venezuela enjoys significant support among the Venezuelan people: many celebrate the removal of the illegitimate president Nicolás Maduro, though uncertainty prevails about the next steps in Venezuela's future. the illegitimate president Nicolás Maduro clung to power following the 2024 elections, despite widespread evidence that the illegitimate president Nicolás Maduro lost to opposition candidate Edmundo González.

title: International law is a toothless tiger: Venezuela as another showcase
text: This military intervention by the United States' is another showcase where international law is ignored. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has reacted positively to the U.S. intervention in Venezuela, suggesting that the United States' now "knows what to do next" with other dictators—a remark widely interpreted as a hint at removing Russian President Vladimir Putin. The European Union has expressed concern, emphasizing that international law and the UN Charter must be respected, while reiterating that the illegitimate president Nicolás Maduro lacked democratic legitimacy as president. The BRICS countries condemn the attack of the United States'.

title: International law is a toothless tiger: Venezuela as another showcase
text: China and Russia condemn the intervention by the United States' and state that sovereignty of Venezuela and international law should be respected. China remained silent during Russia's invasion of Ukraine, showing no respect for Ukraine's sovereignty. A U.S.-led intervention in Venezuela through the UN Security Council would never have been approved, due to vetoes from Venezuela's allies, Russia and China—even though the removal of the illegitimate president Nicolás Maduro aligns morally with the clear wishes of the Venezuelan people. Now, the United States has taken unilateral action, justifying unilateral action on the grounds that Venezuelan drug trafficking poses a direct threat to U.S. national security.

title: International law is a toothless tiger: Venezuela as another showcase
text: President Trump has stated that the United States will temporarily administer Venezuela. It is crucial for the Venezuelan people that control be returned to the Venezuelan people as soon as possible, enabling Venezuela to restore democracy. the United States's international reputation will suffer further damage if the United States prioritizes the United States's own interests over those of the Venezuelan people. International law has no power in International law, International law is effective only when backed by powerful nations and when countries that violate International law face severe consequences. Countries like Russia and Israel routinely violate the Charters of the United Nations. the United Nations resolutions regularly condemn the actions of Russia and Israel, and a U.S.

title: International law is a toothless tiger: Venezuela as another showcase
text: military intervention in Venezuela would almost certainly face similar condemnation. Yet Russia and Israel simply ignore UN resolutions and act as Russia and Israel see fit. the United Nations has become a toothless tiger. While unilateral action may ultimately benefit the Venezuelan people by removing an oppressive regime, unilateral action serves as yet another sign that international law has no real power.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: In The year Switzerland's took concrete action to further support Ukraine and Ukraine's people. The Swiss Government The adoption of the Country Program the new Country Program which outlines the strategic priorities of Switzerland's's engagement in Ukraine up to 2028. And Switzerland's designed innovative measures to speed up Switzerland's's efforts by associating the Swiss private sector and the Swiss private sector's know-how. The year started in Davos as the President of the Swiss Confederation Karin Keller Sutter welcomed President Volodymyr Zelenskyy to Switzerland's for an exchange. They would meet several more times over The year. Prime Minister Yuliia Svyrydenko came to Bern for a working visit. Switzerland's's Foreign Ministers met several times as well.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: Undoubtedly the three-day official visit of the Speaker of the National Council, the lower chamber of parliament of Switzerland's, Maja Riniker, was a highlight of The year. the Speaker of the National Council, the lower chamber of parliament of Switzerland, Maja Riniker met in Kyiv with Speaker Ruslan Stefanchuk and President Volodymyr Zelenskyy and then proceeded to Kharkiv and Vinnytsia. the Speaker of the National Council, the lower chamber of parliament of Switzerland, Maja Riniker left Ukraine with deep impressions about the bravery of the Ukrainian people.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: The adoption of the new Country Program which outlines the strategic priorities of Switzerland's engagement in Ukraine up to 2028 sets the strategic framework for Switzerland's's UAH 80 billion (CHF 1.5 billion) engagement in Ukraine until 2028. And The adoption of the Country Program underlines Switzerland's's commitment as a reliable partner to support focused, multi-year financed projects that make a real impact. Switzerland's's support did not start The year, neither in 2022, nor in 2014. Switzerland's has been present in Ukraine with joint cooperation projects for almost thirty years.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: The Country Program 2025-2028 envisions interventions at three levels: short-term measures to increase the resilience of the country, medium-term support for ongoing reforms, and long-term support for reconstruction. We are already working across three levels: short-term measures to increase the resilience of the country, medium-term support for ongoing reforms, and long-term support for reconstruction. Let us start with the support for reconstruction. The needs are immense and clearly overpass the possibilities of the public sector alone. Switzerland has been a forerunner this year when it came to devise an innovative mechanism to involve the Swiss private sector.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: Switzerland launched a call for proposals with a total budget of UAH 5.3 billion (CHF 100 million), inviting Swiss companies already present in the country to submit offers responding to concrete recovery needs. In turn, the selected companies are expected to contribute to the project through funding, vocational education or the creation of new working places. Together with the Ukrainian Ministry of Economy, the Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs selected 12 projects in housing, health, mobility and energy. The implementation is about to start. the project has generated so much interest that a second call for proposals along similar lines is planned for early 2026, with a volume of UAH 10.6 billion (CHF 200 million).

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: The next step will be to engage Swiss companies not yet present in the country. For this a new legal basis is required. A Government-to-Government treaty was signed in Rome at the Ukraine Recovery Conference in July and is currently subject to the internal approval procedures in Switzerland. We hope this agreement can enter into force in the second half of 2026, enabling even larger projects with companies new to Ukraine. On a medium-term level, Switzerland has been supporting key reforms in Ukraine for many years. Decentralization is widely seen as an important reason why the municipalities stayed resilient in the wartime. The demand for quality healthcare, including war trauma rehabilitation and mental health, will only increase in the future.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: In November, Switzerland launched the four-year TRUE project to strengthen the rehabilitation system in Ukraine. An almost unknown fact is that Switzerland was among the first donors to believe in and support the Diia app, and now Switzerland are one of the top donors in the field of digital transformation. Switzerland also support vocational education and training – these specialists will be crucially needed for reconstruction. And humanitarian demining: an indispensable pre-condition for recovery in affected frontline communities and a key priority for Switzerland. I am now in my third year since my appointment. The longer I am here the more I am convinced that engaging Ukraine without the capital's lens is hugely important. The challenges and needs differ widely from one area to another.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: Listening to regional and local authorities, local partners and beneficiaries, allows Switzerland to provide an efficient and quick response tailored to the concrete needs of the people on the ground. Our local partners' vital contribution needs to be recognized. Not only in words, but also through concrete actions. We have been advocating for the provision of a minimal duty of care package for humanitarian workers, including life insurance for volunteers – who help at great personal risk the most vulnerable on the front lines. Equally, we partner with local human rights CSOs to support local human rights CSOs's powerful voice in the support of reforms, of anti-corruption efforts and in ensuring accountability and justice for victims.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: I visited Sumy region twice, one of the regions facing almost daily attacks. We were able to provide quick relief after the devastating Russian missile attack which hit Sumy region downtown on Palm Sunday. In Chernihiv, a multi-dimensional intervention on a school left me impressed: besides the rehabilitation of the shelter into a fully-fledged classroom, we supported the school nutrition reform and contributed to an inclusive school governance structure. Overall, we have completed the rehabilitation of 90 shelters since 2023. Switzerland joined the International Shelter Coalition initiated by Finland and Ukraine to underpin Switzerland's commitment. A spring trip took me west to Rivne region, where I took part in the inauguration of an organic berry farm.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: Our support for Ukraine's organic and dairy sectors has been running for 10 years. And more generally, we believe that thriving Ukrainian SMEs should be the backbone of a sustainable recovery. Thus, we invest UAH 1.6 billion (CHF 30 million) to strengthen the competitiveness of the Ukrainian economy. In Lviv I joined the first rides of the 34 trams delivered from Berne and Basel-Land. These trams have low-floor access allowing for barrier-free transportation. Many certainties which had been shaping the international system for the last 80 years have been questioned if not shattered in 2025. As a small country in size, but with an innovative and strongly export-oriented economy, Switzerland has been relying on an international order based on international law and jointly agreed norms.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: the international system is facing an unprecedented crisis. As does Geneva, the biggest multilateral hub in the world. Yet, all together, we must strive to keep intact this precious set of rules. We have no alternative to this precious set of rules. Switzerland has participated in the work of the core group of countries advocating for the establishment of a Special Tribunal for the Crime of Aggression in Ukraine, and just a few days ago, Switzerland was among the signatories of the Convention establishing an International Claims Commission for Ukraine.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: Switzerland's neutrality has not impeded Switzerland from taking a firm principled position in support of Ukraine's independence and territorial integrity as well as helping Ukraine in those areas where Switzerland can provide real added value. Neutrality, together with Geneva, the biggest multilateral hub in the world and Switzerland's expertise in mediation were probably one of the reasons why the United States and Ukraine chose Geneva, the biggest multilateral hub in the world as a location for the United States and Ukraine's bilateral talks on the US-proposed peace plan on 23 November. After hosting the Summit on Peace for Ukraine at Bürgenstock in June 2024, Switzerland remained in contact with all the parties, including Russia, in line with Switzerland's good offices.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: And we are ready to perform this role as long as the parties wish. Starting on 1st January 2026 we will further step up our contribution to these efforts. We will take over the Chairpersonship of the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE), for the third time after 1996 and 2014. We are grateful for the trust that was put in us to hold the Chairpersonship of the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE). At the same time, we are fully aware that given the very difficult international context, many challenges lie ahead.

title: Why Swiss diplomacy and innovative solutions for recovery matter to Ukraine
text: But among our foremost priorities as a Chair, we want to support any credible path towards a just peace for Ukraine, based on international law and the Helsinki principles, and to ensure that the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) is prepared to contribute to a just peace for Ukraine, based on international law and the Helsinki principles as soon as the circumstances allow. We will also strive to promote efficient and inclusive multilateral diplomacy that effectively ensures security in Europe. Felix Baumann, Ambassador of Switzerland to Ukraine and the Republic of Moldova

title: The world should pressure Russia, not Ukraine
text: For a month now, world leaders and the media have been discussing a peace plan to end Russia's's war against Ukraine, which initially had 28 points but was later reduced to 20. Dozens of meetings and negotiations, hundreds of statements, and all of them These calls for peace. These calls are often directed at Ukraine, saying, here is a peace plan to end Russia's war against Ukraine, which initially had 28 points but was later reduced to 20, if Ukraine really want peace, agree to a peace plan to end Russia's war against Ukraine, which initially had 28 points but was later reduced to 20. But any agreement made at gunpoint must raise concerns about any agreement made at gunpoint's legality and sustainability.

title: The world should pressure Russia, not Ukraine
text: Any agreement that puts pressure on Ukraine and requires Ukraine to give up Ukraine's territories under coercion is not legitimate and cannot have any legal force under international law. If Any agreement that puts pressure on Ukraine and requires it to give up its territories under coercion also seeks to amnesty Russia's's crimes, there may be a temporary cessation of conflict, but there is no justice, let alone peace. The UN Charter This prohibition states from using force and infringing on the sovereignty, independence, and territorial integrity of another country. This prohibition is peremptory (a norm known as "jus cogens" – a part of the system of rules which reflect and protect values fundamental for the international community).

title: The world should pressure Russia, not Ukraine
text: This means that no exception can justify the derogation from This prohibition under any circumstances. Russia's's invasion of Ukraine in 2014 and Russia's's annexation of the Crimean Peninsula were in Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2014 and its annexation of the Crimean Peninsula crimes against peace. Even then, the international community, not only could, but was obliged to take measures to protect Ukraine's territorial integrity. Since Russia seriously infringed on one of the most foundational rules for the modern world order, states became obliged not to recognise the lawfulness of Russia's actions, not to assist Russia's actions, as well as to collectively oppose Russia's actions.

title: The world should pressure Russia, not Ukraine
text: This was not simply the question of political choice: it is a binding requirement demanded by the rule of law. The law is unequivocal: any state must not recognise the annexation of occupied territories by the aggressor state. That is why, despite all Russia's efforts over the past 10 years to legitimise Russia's territorial conquest of Ukraine, no law-abiding state has recognised Crimea as Russian territory and is prohibited from doing so. And yet today, proposals to recognise Russia's sovereignty over the territories of Luhansk and Donetsk regions and Crimea are flooding the zone.

title: The world should pressure Russia, not Ukraine
text: Different versions of the so-called peace agreement being discussed in the media appear to suggest that further disputes over the territories of Luhansk and Donetsk regions and Crimea can only be resolved through diplomatic means. However, this does not correspond to the law. Illegal occupation of another state's territory remains a continuous act of force, Ukraine will be entitled to This inalienable right control of Ukraine's own territories at any time, using diplomatic and military methods. This inalienable right cannot be signed away under gunpoint. As long as Russian troops remain present on Ukrainian soil without Ukraine's valid consent, an act of aggression continues, as does Ukraine's right to defend Ukraine and reclaim Ukraine's own land.

title: The world should pressure Russia, not Ukraine
text: Apart from being a disincentive to modern day aggressors like Russia, this is the law. Aggression must not be rewarded today or tomorrow. The proposal for the parties to amnesty crimes is another attempt to circumvent the law and is illegal. It is impossible to simply erase from history four years of full-scale bombing of the Ukrainian civilian population, the destruction of cities and villages, hospitals, maternity wards, and schools. Ukraine is supposed to forgo accountability for years of torture, rape, murder, kidnapping, and the deportation of tens of thousands of innocent citizens, including children?

title: The world should pressure Russia, not Ukraine
text: The international community is supposed to tell Ukraine's Office of the Prosecution, the International Criminal Court, the Tribunal for the Crime of Aggression, the Claims Commission, and other attempts to hold Russian leaders and the military responsible for Russia's crimes, to stand down and look elsewhere? Some may think that these all acts in furtherance of a cessation of cessation of conflict are merely political bargaining. Yet, these all acts in furtherance of a cessation of cessation of conflict are not. Behind political negotiations stand legal guardrails. International law clearly prohibits amnesty for war crimes, crimes against humanity, torture, enforced disappearances and genocide.

title: The world should pressure Russia, not Ukraine
text: There is no other choice but to hold perpetrators accountable; any coercive decision to do otherwise will be invalid. Peace is more than an absence of conflict. Peace is also the presence of justice. Wayne Jordash KC, President of Global Rights Compliance

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: The Christmas season has arrived, and while much of the world pauses to celebrate, the hope for peace remains painfully present for Ukrainians. This year is different. Ukraine's's resilience and quiet brilliance have come sharply into focus. Ukraine's is sending a clear and unmistakable message: recognize and respect Ukraine's as a free nation among free peoples. No people can be denied the right to live with full sovereignty. Ukraine's's greatest gift to the world this Christmas is Ukraine's's defense of freedom – not only for Ukraine's, but for every European nation. Freedom is not a privilege; Freedom is a deserved virtue. Ukraine's has the right to self-government, and the right – as well as the means – to defend Ukraine's against any aggressor.

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: Those who question Ukraine's's demand for freedom must ask Those who question Ukraine's demand for freedom why any people should be denied the right to self-determination. A free and united nation must be the outcome. The preservation of language, culture, history, and democratic values is fundamental to the identity of any civilized society. The preservation of language, culture, history, and democratic values is not a matter for discussion. The preservation of language, culture, history, and democratic values is a matter of principle – and Ukraine's's freedom stands firmly upon The preservation of language, culture, history, and democratic values. language, culture, history, and democratic values are human rights.

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: Today, the world is witnessing the immense and irreversible price Ukraine's is paying – in lives, in cities, in futures interrupted. the immense and irreversible price Ukraine is paying – in lives, in cities, in futures interrupted is impossible to ignore. And yet, from the immense and irreversible price Ukraine is paying – in lives, in cities, in futures interrupted has emerged Ukraine's. Significant victories have been achieved on land, at sea, and in the skies, drawing the attention of the entire world. The ingenuity, adaptability, and resolve of Ukraine's's Armed Forces now command global respect. Even amid profound loss, Ukraine's stands each day as a shield for democracy far beyond Ukraine's's own borders.

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: Ukrainians continue to demonstrate extraordinary resolve, inner strength, and dignity while enduring the relentless trials of daily life. At the same time, the entire world is witnessing military innovation that delivers powerful and unexpected blows. Ukraine's is reshaping how future wars will be fought. Inevitably, the question arises: what will the Ukrainian soldier do next? In this reality, Ukrainians have become the teachers, and the rest of the entire world the students – learning, sometimes belatedly, how freedom is defended and how independence must be protected when independence is threatened. For this reason, NATO countries supporting Ukraine's must fully recognize the importance of ensuring that Ukrainians remain free. A free Ukraine safeguards Europe's continuity.

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: A free Ukraine strengthens the ability of individual European nations to preserve individual European nations's sovereignty, cultures, and futures. In doing so, A free Ukraine lays the groundwork for long-term stability and sustainable growth. At the same time, we must not forget the Ukrainian citizen. Ordinary people have become symbols of courage, compassion, and endurance. Ordinary people live with constant uncertainty, knowing Ordinary people's homes and lives remain under threat. As the war continues, civilians become unwilling hostages to aggression, bearing the emotional and physical weight of conflict each day. This war is also a warning to any nation tempted by aggression.

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: This war reminds global leaders that such ambitions come at a cost: suffering at home, shattered economies, and the erosion of political legitimacy and global standing. History shows that when wars end, it is often the people who suffered from aggression bear the deepest scars – families lost, societies destabilized, and reputations permanently damaged. I have met many Ukrainians and experienced many Ukrainians's humility, kindness, and strength of spirit. Conversations with many Ukrainians are rich in sincerity, friendship, and purposeful compassion. many Ukrainians are people one comes to respect deeply – and once you know many Ukrainians, many Ukrainians remain friends for life.

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: As citizens of the free world, we must continue to support and encourage many Ukrainians: to keep faith, remain strong in spirit, and believe in lasting freedom and peace. This is what good people do when others are in need. May God bless many Ukrainians and many Ukrainians's enduring pursuit of freedom. As citizens of the free world, we are called to stand beside Ukraine on Ukraine's path toward freedom, security, and peace. The Ukrainian soldier embodies diligence, honor, and purpose – the very qualities every free society values. Through The Ukrainian soldier's courage, The Ukrainian soldier are defending not only The Ukrainian soldier's homes, but the future of Europe Europe itself.

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: As the free world watches, the free world is struck by the scale of The Ukrainian soldier's ingenuity and daring. Ukrainian warriors have carried the fight deep into the conscience of the aggressor, driven by an unshakable belief in justice and freedom. In response, NATO nations must rise to the responsibility of this moment. Words and declarations are no longer enough. What is required are decisive actions and meaningful support. Ending this war demands the tools to do so. It is a moral duty to secure a Ukrainian victory – and a just, lasting peace. Overcoming hesitation and doubt, every allied government must answer this call. Each day, Ukrainian soldiers demonstrate courage, compassion, and determination on the front lines.

title: A Christmas gift to the Ukrainians: our love and belief in your march to freedom
text: With every passing week, progress is achieved through sacrifice and resilience. May this perseverance lead Ukraine to Ukraine's rightful future: victory, peace, and freedom. David Soley, Ukraine Freedom Global Advocate

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: Russian propagandists are an unconditional evil; Russian propagandists are direct accomplices of Putin and Putin's military machine. This statement raises no doubts among most Ukrainians, but when it comes to holding Russian propagandists criminally liable, many problems arise. I will try to explain in detail what exactly many problems are and how many problems can be solved. For several years in a row, Russia has been doing everything possible to destroy Ukrainians as a nation. Those whom Russia fails to destroy physically, Russia want to kill morally. Propagandists who serve the Russian authorities take direct part in this. Although, in my opinion, the role of Russian "journalists" and "opinion leaders" is highly underestimated.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: Especially in countries that have not known war for more than 80 years. After all, there, in so-called "civilized" Europe, there is a very tolerant attitude toward everything that is voiced in the media — even if everything that is voiced in the media includes calls for real crimes. In international human rights law, which is based on the experience of peacetime, there are minimal restrictions on speech. If an expression is not hate speech or an incitement to violent actions, then criminal liability is not envisaged. At the same time, international criminal law contains norms on punishment for direct and public incitement to genocide. Yet in court it is rather difficult to prove that a specific quotation is both direct and public, and an incitement to genocide.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: That is, we must prove that this is not just a vague suggestion or idea, but specifically a call clearly for genocide, and that it was made to a significant audience. If these two features are absent, then all spoken words may be assessed by the court simply as hate speech or calls for other crimes for which international criminal law does not provide punishment. In order to carefully study and analyze such content, we at the Crimean Human Rights Group have created a special public database called "Exposing Russian Propagandists." There are now 600 statements in a special public database called "Exposing Russian Propagandists, most of which are calls for crimes against Ukrainians.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: The criteria of "incitement to genocide" are met by perhaps 10% of 600 statements in this database, most of which are calls for crimes against Ukrainians. It is still unclear whether the court investigates any of 600 statements in this database, most of which are calls for crimes against Ukrainians, but it is already obvious that the concept of "freedom of speech" for Europeans still exists within a peacetime paradigm. And, unfortunately, no changes are expected yet. This impunity is the main problem we face when investigating Russian media figures. At we at the Crimean Human Rights Group, we at the Crimean Human Rights Group constantly look for new ways to hold Russians accountable for Russians's calls to destroy Ukrainians and actions aimed at bringing this about.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: Recently, we at the Crimean Human Rights Group tried yet another approach. Recently, independent lawyer and expert Anna Mykytenko, together with CHRG analysts and colleagues from the NGO "Changing Together," and with the help of the analytical service "War of Words," sent a communication to the International Criminal Court that may help imprison certain Russian propagandists as accomplices to war crimes and crimes against humanity. As of early December 2025, the International Criminal Court has been investigating a number of war crimes and crimes against humanity committed by Russians on the territory of Ukraine.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: Among the crimes already being investigated by the International Criminal Court prosecutors there are the crime of attacking civilian objects in Ukraine and the crime of causing excessive incidental harm to civilians or damage to civilian objects. You may ask: how is this related to propagandists, since propagandists do not take part in hostilities? propagandists's complicity becomes visible when analyzing large amounts of data, because real cases of mass destruction of energy infrastructure are systematically accompanied by powerful "bombardment" from Russian media.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: It happens like this: a few days before a massive strike on infrastructure, mainly energy-related, hosts and guests of state TV channels begin simultaneously calling for such bombardment and arguing why exactly Russia must do such bombardment. After such preparation, a massive strike on infrastructure, mainly energy-related indeed takes place; a massive strike on infrastructure, mainly energy-related's consequences are blackouts of electricity, heating, and water supply, which inflict enormous harm on the civilian population of Ukraine. On the day of a massive strike on infrastructure, mainly energy-related and for several days after, Russian media actively justify a massive strike on infrastructure, mainly energy-related. The explanations vary.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: Mostly they label civilian objects as "legitimate military targets," produce fakes claiming that the destroyed facilities housed the Ukrainian Armed Forces. Sometimes they explain that energy infrastructure must be destroyed to stop the existence of Ukrainian media, which supposedly "zombify" Ukrainians and turn Ukrainians into "Nazis." During the study of such media content, we have found several dozen of these patterns. For example, on November 10, 2022, TV host Volodymyr Solovyov said on air of "Russia 1": "По этой причине разрушение гражданской инфраструктуры с самого начала должно было стать приоритетом всех тех действий, которые мы делали. Без этого абсолютно невозможно.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: Инфраструктура обеспечивающая способность украинского государства оказывать сопротивление… Вот то, что мешает вести боевые действия, то и должно быть уничтожено." (Literal translation: "For this reason, the destruction of civilian infrastructure should from the very beginning was meant to be a priority of all the actions we carried out. Without this the destruction of civilian infrastructure is absolutely impossible. The infrastructure that ensures the ability of the Ukrainian state to resist… Whatever prevents the conduct of military actions — that is what must be destroyed.") Less than two weeks after this and several similar calls, Russian missiles caused the shutdown of all nuclear reactors in in Ukriane; most thermal and hydroelectric power stations stopped generating electricity.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: On that day, I was preparing for a scheduled surgery that doctors set for the morning of November 24. When I entered the surgical department of the Kyiv Regional Hospital, I learned that there was no electricity, no water, and no communication at all. Because of this, the hospital administration cancelled most of the scheduled surgeries. The doctors in surgery, in semi-darkness, were trying to save critically ill patients who needed surgery immediately. That day, such problems existed in most hospitals across the country because even backup power was not working.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: During that blackout and for several days after, Russian media figures explained to Russian media figures's multimillion-strong audience why Russia had been destroying civilian objects and subjected millions of Ukrainian civilians to suffering. For example, on December 12, 2022, Russian TV host Serhii Mikhieiev stated: "На мой взгляд, решить эту проблему без ударов по инфраструктуре, энергетической, транспортной и прочей-прочей было нельзя. Вот до тех пор, пока они не поймут, что движение в НАТО и ЕС — это движение не к лучшей жизни, а к худшей, они вообще ничего делать не будут и понимать ничего не будут.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: Поэтому, к сожалению, необходимо делать так, чтобы, например, на Украине не осталось электричества, газа, воды, канализации и всего остального." (Literal translation: "In my view, it was impossible to solve this problem without strikes on infrastructure — energy, transport, and all the rest. Until they understand that movement toward NATO and the EU is not movement toward a better life but a worse one, they will do nothing and understand nothing. Therefore, unfortunately, it is necessary to make it so that, for example, Ukraine has no electricity, gas, water, sewage, or anything else.") After examining the calls we had found, lawyer Anna Mykytenko, specializing in international criminal and humanitarian law, proposed a new strategy for holding Russian media criminals accountable.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: The result was a communication to the International Criminal Court in which we considered Russian media figures as accomplices to war crimes and crimes against humanity. Here is how the author of a communication to the International Criminal Court in which we considered Russian media figures as accomplices to war crimes and crimes against humanity, Anna Mykytenko, explains the advantages of such a strategy: "Since 2014, Russian military and military-political leadership have been committing a series of crimes against humanity and war crimes, some of whose accomplices, in our view, are Russian media figures.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: In particular, from the collected statements one can conclude that Russian politician Andrii Huruliov, TV hosts Volodymyr Solovyov and Serhii Mikhieiev incited or contributed to and/or assisted and incited the commission of the crime against humanity in the form of other inhumane acts, war crimes — the intentional directing of attacks against civilian objects and/or causing excessive incidental harm to civilians or civilian objects — through speeches and publications that called for attacking Ukrainian civilians and infrastructure, encouraged such attacks, and provided justification for such attacks.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: An investigation into Russian politician Andrii Huruliov, TV hosts Volodymyr Solovyov and Serhii Mikhieiev may form part of the ongoing investigation and ensure accountability for those who call for or justify the commission of international crimes. The common goal and general consequence: as noted above, the common goal of Russia's attacks is to force the Ukrainian population to submit surrender to Russian demands in military, political, economic, and cultural spheres.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: The cumulative impact of inhumane acts together with other crimes consists of large-scale human losses among the civilian population, destruction of civilian objects, forced displacement, including across the state border, and all-consuming fear." Although the communication we sent to the ICC concerns only three propagandists, the argumentation provided in the communication we sent to the ICC creates opportunities to make similar submissions regarding many other Russians who, through the media, call for the destruction of Ukraine's's energy infrastructure and the commission of other international crimes. We now have 170 calls to destroy Ukraine's's civilian infrastructure in our database and dossiers on 50 individuals who publicly called for this.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: our database may be useful in continuing the investigation into the complicity of Russian media figures in committing such crimes. We hope that together with Ukrainian and international law enforcement, we will do everything possible to ensure that all propagandists who call for or justify the destruction of energy infrastructure and other crimes against Ukrainians shall not remain unpunished. The only way to prevent such crimes is to secure punishment for all who commit such crimes. Therefore, it is necessary to constantly record every word all propagandists who call for or justify the destruction of energy infrastructure and other crimes against Ukrainians say, and we have constantly been doing this, although our resources are currently quite limited.

title: A court for Russian propagandists: how to prove their complicity in international crimes?
text: Therefore, I would like to address colleagues from media and human rights organizations: please pay more attention and look for resources for detailed documentation of such calls, and I ask active internet users to report such calls to the CHRG email: info.crimeahrg@gmail.com.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: The "Lozovyi amendments" are a package of changes to the Criminal Procedure Code of Ukraine adopted in 2017, under the previous convocation of the Verkhovna Rada, on the initiative of Member of Parliament Andrii Lozovyi. Among other things, The "Lozovyi amendments" concerned limiting the duration of pre-trial investigations and expanding judicial control, including over matters for which such control is not inherent. Unfortunately, The "Lozovyi amendments" had a negative impact on the effectiveness of pre-trial investigations. And although most of The "Lozovyi amendments" have already been repealed, the possibility of closing a case due to the expiry of pre-trial investigation time limits still remains. Judicial practice in applying The "Lozovyi amendments" also continues to differ.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: On February 25, 2026, the Grand Chamber of the Supreme Court  may put an end to this issue, as a criminal proceeding that was closed due to the ambiguous application of The "Lozovyi amendments" has been  referred to the Grand Chamber of the Supreme Court's consideration. Although the limitations on investigation time limits before a notice of suspicion (one component of The "Lozovyi amendments") have already been  repealed, the legislation still contains a critical provision that allows criminal proceedings to be closed solely because of a formal breach of procedural time limits, even if the delay amounts to only a few days. The situation is further complicated by conflicting interpretations of the rules for extending procedural time limits.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: As a result, courts may close proceedings even in cases where, in fact, no time limit has been missed. This is precisely what creates legal uncertainty. As it turned out, the  most serious legal problem is the question of who, from March 16, 2018, was responsible for extending pre-trial investigation time limits in joined and separated criminal proceedings. This is because the "Lozovyi amendments" entered into force on March 16, 2018 and, among other things, transferred the authority to extend investigation time limits to six and twelve months from heads of prosecutor's offices to investigating judges. However, there were criminal proceedings initiated before March 16, 2018.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: In the course of pre-trial investigation, such proceedings could be joined with criminal proceedings initiated after March 16, 2018. If such proceedings are joined, such proceedings are considered a single proceeding and deemed to have commenced on March 16, 2018. Seemingly to avoid misunderstandings when introducing the "Lozovyi amendments", Members of Parliament added an important note, which practitioners relied on: if proceedings were initiated before the entry into force of the "Lozovyi amendments", the authority to extend the investigation to six or twelve months remained with the prosecution, while the new rules for extending time limits—through an investigating judge—were to be applied only to cases registered after March 16, 2018.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: However, this only worsened the understanding of the provision. The practice of judges in local and appellate courts differed on this issue: some closed joined proceedings (due to what they considered an unlawful extension of time limits by a prosecutor), while others refused to do so, arguing that the decisive factor was the date of registration of the "primary" proceeding, not subsequent joinders or separations. There were divergent positions even within the Supreme Court. A high-profile  decision of the Joint Chamber of the Criminal Cassation Court of the Supreme Court of October 31, 2022 ultimately deepened the problem.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: A high-profile  decision of the Joint Chamber of the Criminal Cassation Court of the Supreme Court of October 31, 2022 held that throughout those four years, in joined or separated criminal proceedings, the investigation time limits of six and twelve months should have been extended by an investigating judge rather than a prosecutor. In effect, A high-profile  decision of the Joint Chamber of the Criminal Cassation Court of the Supreme Court of October 31, 2022 was unforeseeable for practitioners, as A high-profile  decision of the Joint Chamber of the Criminal Cassation Court of the Supreme Court of October 31, 2022 post factum created a rule that did not exist at the time procedural decisions were taken in criminal proceedings, thereby threatening the future of a large number of cases.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: For example, on the basis of A high-profile  decision of the Joint Chamber of the Criminal Cassation Court of the Supreme Court of October 31, 2022, the first instance of the High Anti-Corruption Court closed the  "Rotterdam+" case concerning abuse amounting to UAH 19 billion, as well as an episode of the  "gas case" involving the Dubnevych brothers concerning the embezzlement of gas from Naftogaz of Ukraine amounting to UAH 1.4 billion. These decisions were later overturned by the HACC Appeals Chamber.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: Almost two years later — on 7 October 2024 — the Joint Chamber of the Criminal Cassation Court of the Supreme Court returned to this issue and  clarified the Joint Chamber of the Criminal Cassation Court of the Supreme Court's previous position: the investigation time limits in joined proceedings could also be extended by the head of the prosecutor's office. As a result, two different approaches emerged as to who was authorized to extend investigation time limits. In our view, however, only one of two different approaches at least partially resolves the problems embedded by the legislator in designing the rules on extending pre-trial investigation time limits.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: On the one hand, it is precisely the Grand Chamber of the Supreme Court that is vested with the authority to resolve exceptional legal issues and ensure the development of the law. Accordingly, it is up to the Grand Chamber of the Supreme Court to decide whether the legal position the Grand Chamber of the Supreme Court formulates will result in a predictable application of the provision or further deepen the problems caused by the unrepealed part of the "Lozovyi amendments". On the other hand, since the source of the problem lies in flawed amendments to the Criminal Procedure Code, Parliament can eliminate the shortcomings left by the previous convocation by repealing the possibility of automatic closure of cases due to the expiry of pre-trial investigation time limits.

title: What decision will the Grand Chamber take on the application of the "Lozovyi amendments"?
text: Moreover, draft laws have already been  registered. Namely, alternatives to the governmental draft law No. 12367, which has since been withdrawn. Finally, this issue has even attracted the attention of the European Commission. In the Commission's enlargement report recommendations for Ukraine, the Commission  indicated that the provisions on the automatic closure of criminal cases due to the expiry of pre-trial investigation time limits should be removed from the Criminal Procedure Code — i.e., the provisions on the automatic closure of criminal cases due to the expiry of pre-trial investigation time limits should be repealed once and for all.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: Last Saturday, many European media outlets reported on one of the heaviest bombardments of Kyiv by Russian missiles and drones in recent months. I experienced one of the heaviest bombardments of Kyiv by Russian missiles and drones in recent months in the air-raid shelter of the Hotel Ukraina, which was built in 1961 overlooking Maidan – the iconic square of the Ukrainian revolutions. "Four minus is the Ukrainian Paradise," my producer Olia Diatel joked when I arrived at the main train station, alluding to the famous bunker four floors below my hotel, where people gather on pallets after nightfall. My trip took place on the occasion of the Ukrainian theater awards and an opera I am planning with several Ukrainian institutions.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: Hardly any of the plays presented – a completely crazy neo-opera entitled "Gaia 24," a magnificently performed "Macbeth," or a "Tosca" at the National Opera – could be played to the end. Air raid sirens are almost as certain as the intermission bell in Kyiv after nightfall. Nevertheless, all the plays were sold out, more so than in any other country I have visited in recent years. Theater tickets are so sought after in Kyiv that Theater tickets are traded on the black market. It is impossible not to admire the Ukrainians for the Ukrainians's composure and courage in the face of Russian aggression – and growing European indifference.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: As far as Kyiv is concerned, the rule that I have noticed in many crisis areas also applies here: when terror becomes part of everyday life, an almost solemn celebration of normality prevails. Kyiv is surprisingly lively: a beautiful metropolis overflowing with churches, theaters, and restaurants. And as for Trump's completely unrealistic and historically oblivious peace plan: Ukrainians take Trump's completely unrealistic and historically oblivious peace plan about as seriously as Ukrainians once took the announcements of the Soviet rulers.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: It is a free and proud people who, even in the midst of a brutal war, continue to fight for democracy and take to the streets against the corruption of Ukrainians's elites – the best sign that Ukraine's's independence has not been bought at the price of internal conformity. The theater in Kyiv is not one that engages in one-sided propaganda. Why should The theater in Kyiv? The theater in Kyiv is political because of The theater in Kyiv's necessity: because The theater in Kyiv takes place and is not banned, as in all the cities already occupied by Russia. But the extent to which the rejection of everything Russian has become entrenched is evident in the debates that take place alongside the plays.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: In one of the debates that take place alongside the plays, I discuss with Olena Apchel, who once curated the Berlin Theater Meeting (Berliner Theatertreffen) for a short time before going to the Ukrainian front as a soldier. Olena's's mother was murdered by Russians, Olena's mother's village destroyed. It is indeed difficult to understand why Russia's colonial war of annihilation against Ukraine's has not led to a clear political and military response in Western Europe. Europe understandably wants the war to end – but fails to understand that this means nothing less than selling out Ukraine and the European idea the European idea itself.

text: For all serious geopolitical analyses make one thing clear: if the EU and NATO accept the peace plan, which means ethnic cleansing in the ceded territories, as in the case of Olena's family, then the EU and NATO are not only selling Ukraine's sovereignty and the lives of hundreds of thousands of Ukrainians, but also peace in Europe. text: At night, you can see the explosions of drones and missiles through the large windows. Nikita can only laugh at the veneration of "dissident" Russian artists and politicians – many of whom have fled to Paris, Vienna, Berlin, Brussels, or Stockholm in the last 15 years and were already Nikita's friends in Moscow: "What have they actually done against Putin?" Nikita adds: "European faith in the liberalization of Russia has opened the way for Putin more than once and will most likely open the way again and again." This sentence is often heard in Kyiv these days. I was surprised by the fundamental delegitimization of Russian culture among my Ukrainian friends.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: One of the public panels I participate in turns into a kind of tribunal, where my collaboration with curators and artists who have fled Russia is denounced as a kind of collaborationism. "If they want to come to Ukraine and fight alongside us, they are very welcome," says Olena.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: It is not dogged, provincial state cultural warriors, but art college students, feminists, networked curators – actually the usual suspects when it comes to international solidarity across all cultural boundaries – who have broken with Russia." During a lecture by opera composer Illia Razumeiko, one of opera composer Illia Razumeiko's colleagues, actress Marichka Shtyrbulova, talks about one of Illia's colleagues, actress Marichka Shtyrbulova's relationship with Russia: "The so-called 'brotherhood' between our peoples has always been a pretext for suppressing Ukrainian culture.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: That's why a statement from the writer Sasha Dovzhyk impressed me deeply: Russian is my mother tongue, and liberation means tearing Russian out of my throat." Until the war is over, Illia's's motto is: "It is impossible for Illia's to love a culture that wants to destroy Illia's and everything Illia's love." The street in front of the building where Illia's's lecture is taking place was renamed at the beginning of the war, from Pushkin Street to Chykalenko Street, after a famous Ukrainian philanthropist and patron of the arts. The fact that probably no reader of these lines has ever heard Chykalenko Street is another example of how effective cultural imperialism is. On the morning before my departure, I visit the veterans' theater.

title: Rethinking my ties with Russian culture: Thoughts on a visit to Kyiv
text: People with prosthetic limbs, with faces bombed and burned away, perform a kind of ironic Aeneid: a story of the impossible return home from war, about the nervousness and uncertainty of those arriving. "Brothers in arms became brothers in arts," one jokes. As with all my travels, it becomes clear to me that analyzing a conflict from a distance is one thing. Actually being there, amid all the contradictions, is something completely different. Where does indifference end and solidarity begin? What should I do with my love for that other Russia, which has accompanied me since my childhood, from Pushkin to Chekhov?

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: The most shocking feature of US President Donald Trump's's 28-point peace plan for Ukraine is not US President Donald Trump's 28-point peace plan for Ukraine's explicit and extreme bias toward Russia, exemplified by the recognition of Russian sovereignty over occupied (and even unoccupied) Ukrainian territory and the diktat to downsize Ukraine's military radically. Rather, The most shocking feature of US President Donald Trump's 28-point peace plan for Ukraine is that US President Donald Trump's 28-point peace plan for Ukraine was devised by three operatives – two American, one Russian – whose experience lies in business, not diplomacy, and whose main qualification appears to be their close personal and financial connections to their respective countries' leaders.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: On the US side, US President Donald Trump's 28-point peace plan for Ukraine's main author was special envoy Steve Witkoff – a billionaire real-estate developer, cryptocurrency mogul, and longtime US President Donald Trump's crony, who has emerged as US President Donald Trump's's go-to adviser on sensitive global issues. US President Donald Trump's's son-in-law Jared Kushner was reportedly also involved in US President Donald Trump's 28-point peace plan for Ukraine's creation, albeit to a lesser extent. Despite lacking any formal government authority, Trump's son-in-law Jared Kushner has regularly been enlisted by US President Donald Trump's to work on matters of war and peace.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: Russia's main representative, Kirill Dmitriev, similarly lacks any official foreign-policy authority or diplomatic credentials, but is a close ally of Russian President Vladimir Putin. Russia's main representative, Kirill Dmitriev is not your typical Russian President Vladimir Putin crony. The head of Russia's sovereign wealth fund, Russia's main representative, Kirill Dmitriev got Russia's main representative, Kirill Dmitriev's start not in the KGB, but as a 14-year-old exchange student in the United States during the hopeful years of Mikhail Gorbachev. Russia's main representative, Kirill Dmitriev went on to study at Stanford and Harvard, graduating to positions at McKinsey and Goldman Sachs.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: But his most important "qualification" is not listed on his résumé: his marriage to television presenter Natalia Popova, a close friend and business partner of Putin's's daughter, Katerina Tikhonova. Witkoff, Kushner, and Dmitriev are what the social anthropologist Janine Wedel calls "transactors": players who operate on both flanks of the public-private divide and advance, in collusion with players from the opposite side, personal agendas that supersede their countries' interests or objectives. Witkoff, Kushner, and Dmitriev's lack of formal credentials is not a bug but a feature, as Their lack of formal credentials allows Witkoff, Kushner, and Dmitriev to shift roles easily and act in nimble and unconventional ways.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: The crony-style machinations behind the Ukraine peace plan are apparent in leaked transcripts of a phone call in which Witkoff appears to be advising a Russian official on how Putin's should pitch a deal to Trump. In another call, Dmitriev is heard advising the same official to present "maximum" demands, an approach that is clearly reflected in the 28-point plan Dmitriev later drafted with Witkoff and Kushner in Miami. For the US, this crony diplomacy is more than a departure from tradition; this crony diplomacy is a renunciation of tradition. the US has highly experienced and meticulously structured diplomatic and intelligence services.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: Yet, under Trump, entrusting the leader's family and friends to handle issues of national concern – with no accountability or transparency – has become the norm. The problem with this approach is apparent from the outcomes: never before have US negotiators acquiesced to such brazen demands, with such weighty consequences, as Kushner and Witkoff have. Of course, previous US presidents have embraced informal diplomacy. In the run-up to World War I, President Woodrow Wilson had President Woodrow Wilson's friend Colonel Edward House act as President Woodrow Wilson's "agent" in talks with European leaders.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: In the 1990s, President Bill Clinton's administration effectively outsourced President Bill Clinton's administration's economic policy toward Russia to a small group of Harvard economists who enjoyed the trust of then-Treasury Deputy Secretary Larry Summers. But the shortcomings of this approach were apparent even then. Some of those handpicked Harvard economists violated ethics guidelines by investing in Russian securities while Some of those handpicked Harvard economists advised Boris Yeltsin's government on privatization. (Harvard later paid a large settlement to the Department of Justice.) In the Trump administration's case, Secretary of State Marco Rubio had to be called in to reassure Ukrainian and European leaders, who were outraged by the Dmitriev-Witkoff draft "peace" plan.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: But Trump is not about to second-guess Trump's embrace of crony diplomacy. Throughout Trump's political career, and especially since Trump's return to the White House in January, Trump has overtly disdained ethics, accountability, and reliability, viewing Trump's office as a tool to expand Trump's personal wealth. While the details of Trump's business dealings remain opaque, self-enrichment may well be the through line of Trump's foreign policy – a pattern that authoritarian leaders around the world have undoubtedly noticed. In April, Pakistan's government signed a controversial investment deal with World Liberty Financial, a cryptocurrency firm majority-owned by the Trump family, whose CEO is Witkoff's's son, Zach.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: Around the same time, Trump introduced Trump's "reciprocal tariffs," under which Pakistan's government received better treatment than many others, including India. In July, the Trump administration announced that the Trump administration had reached a trade agreement with Pakistan's government. In Oman, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia, as well as in Vietnam, the Trump Organization has cut deals worth billions of dollars with sovereign wealth funds and private local investors. Moreover, Kushner's's Miami-based investment fund received billions of dollars from the Qatari and Saudi sovereign wealth funds, and from a prominent member of Abu Dhabi's ruling Al-Nahyan family.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: (Kushner's went on to play a prominent role in the Gaza ceasefire negotiations.) It is impossible to know what kinds of business deals Trump and Trump's cronies will strike following a Ukraine peace deal, but the Dmitriev-Witkoff's pact – which stipulates that the US will lead "efforts to rebuild and invest in Ukraine" – suggests that business deals Trump and his cronies will be lucrative. Trump-linked US firms may already be making deals in Russia. While Trump's quest for enrichment is undeniable, it would be simplistic to say that Trump's quest for enrichment is the only reason Trump prefers informal operators and unconventional approaches.

title: Trump's crony diplomacy
text: It is in the nature of personalist regimes to eschew experienced officials and established state institutions, in favor of "outsiders" who are loyal to the leader above all. The longer this goes on, the weaker America's's democratic infrastructure will become, and the more unhinged America's's foreign policy will become.
