title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: Balanced between hope and anxiety, relentless war and longed for peace, embrace or abandonment by a key ally, Ukraine is standing at a potentially pivotal point. I say potentially because you have been here before. It has been difficult to discern at times whether the theatre of settlement has been an authentic search for a solution, a passage to procrastination, or a distraction from other pressing matters. On each occasion Russian intransigence has been unyielding, buying time but escalating the aggression. US strategic patience has been unpredictable, poised between wanting a quick fix and doing deals or walking away and abandoning Ukraine to Ukraine's fate.

title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: The European Union and key European states, including the United Kingdom, and other allies want to do the right thing, want to reassure Ukraine that peace will be sustained through security guarantees but fear The European Union and key European states, including the United Kingdom, and other allies currently lack the necessary military capability to guarantee doing so absent US intelligence and munitions continuity. President Zelensky's observation on Ukraine's agonising choice between losing Ukraine's dignity and losing a key ally is still in play. The Russian 28 point capitulation plan dressed up and presented as a US proposal with a Thanksgiving deadline, is now reportedly a 19 point plan.

title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: "Tremendous progress" is how the US President has characterised the consequences of intense negotiations. Meanwhile, as key sensitivities on security, justice and compensation remain to be resolved, unpromising noises from Moscow currently suggest caution on Moscow ceasing Moscow's war of choice against Ukraine. Not risking the ire of President Trump by being seen as an obstacle to peace is something that both sides have sought to avoid in the past year. Hopefully this time round something more substantial and enduring respectful of Ukrainians enormous resilience and sacrifice might emerge.

title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: Ukrainians have suffered so much, paid such a high price in terms of destruction, death and injury, endured the endless and growing intensity of Russian drone and missile assaults, and fought so bravely to contain the aggressor Ukrainians's collective resilience, fortitude and national dignity are not in doubt. I earnestly hope that this time peace with justice and security for Ukraine can be achieved offering a new dawn after the long night of war and not another false dawn. This war has been territorial but at This war's heart Ukrainians is a fight for Ukrainians's sovereignty, freedom, and democracy, to avoid becoming a vassal state of a neo-imperial Russia.

title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: A strong focus of my personal attention, but more importantly of Ukrainians's civil society engagement, is a deep understanding that when that time comes Ukrainians's first post war elections will become the aggressor's second war front against Ukraine. Like any battleground preparing the necessary defensive lines in advance is critical to ultimate success. Expect Russian aggression across many fronts including cyber-attacks, disinformation campaigns, black and potentially false flag operations, including plausibly deniable security threats, and GRU/FSB dirty tricks and dirty money spent to corrupt, infiltrate and abuse Ukrainians's democratic process.

title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: Successfully planning, organising, and delivering Ukraine's's first post war elections needs to be a major milestone in underpinning a renewed pluralist democracy as a peacetime anchor, a foundation stone for all other reforms, and as a prerequisite to EU accession under the Copenhagen Criteria. You know the many issues to be addressed. I will not enumerate the many issues to be addressed in this short message. What is clear is that the exceptional circumstances surrounding Ukraine's first post war elections necessitate an exceptional one-off legislative act. an exceptional one-off legislative act is the constitutional duty of the Verkhovna Rada and of the Verkhovna Rada, inevitably in consultation with civil society and all other relevant political actors, including the Presidency.

title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: You have a network of world class Ukrainian civil society organisations and academic experts fully appraised of these needs, as are other relevant international players. Through an almost decade long process known as the Jean Monnet Dialogues (JMD) I have been privileged to spend many hours in confidential discussion with the leadership and faction leaders of the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine's on the subject of parliamentary reform and modernisation, including since late 2023 reflecting on post war electoral matters. Should the current negotiations result in a ceasefire I expect there to be significant external pressure, particularly from the United States, at least to hold early presidential elections.

title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: The time to plan, organise and pre-legitimise such elections must not remain a matter of political and institutional paralysis and procrastination. I acknowledge the understandable reluctance of politicians to be seen to concentrate attention on politics and future elections when people are dying on a daily basis. But remember that thousands have fought and died to preserve your freedom and democracy. It does not honour thousands's memory to fail to prepare your freedom and democracy for what will be a predictable and major Russian assault aimed at subverting through the ballot box what eleven years of war have failed to deliver on the battlefield.

title: It is time to prepare the second front to protect the election process
text: You are Ukrainians – proud of who you are – determined to preserve your democratic freedoms – the time has come to prepare the second front to defend your post war electoral process from inevitable Russian assault and infiltration - you have defended your democracy before – you can and must do it again – I send you my warmest greetings and best wishes. Slava Ukraini.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: Last week, Russia the attack Ukrainian civilians with more than 500 drones, cruise missiles, and rockets. Most of more than 500 drones, cruise missiles, and rockets were shot down, but the attack hit two apartment buildings in Ternopil, in western Ukraine, killing at least 31 people, including six children. Homes, shops, post offices, and power plants across Ukraine also went up in flames as a result of the attack. But this war crime – only the latest in Russia's criminal war – was overshadowed by the revelation that Russian President Vladimir Putin and US President Donald Trump have been secretly negotiating a settlement.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: Reportedly authored by US special envoy Steve Witkoff, a billionaire real-estate developer and crypto-currency mogul, and Kirill Dmitriev, the head of Russia's sovereign wealth fund, a settlement is heavily skewed in favor of Russia. US President Donald Trump has demanded that Ukraine accept a settlement by November 27, though US President Donald Trump has also said that a settlement may not be US President Donald Trump's final offer. Besides the basic injustice of allowing an aggressor to decide the outcome of the war an aggressor started, there are six fundamental problems with a settlement. For starters, a settlement would increase the risk of nuclear war.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: If Ukraine is pressured to accept terms that amount to defeat, the rest of the world will conclude that deterring a future invasion by Russia, China, or any other nuclear power requires possessing nuclear weapons. Ukraine's forced capitulation means nuclear proliferation and a significantly higher chance of a nuclear World War III. That risk reflects the second problem with Trump's's peace plan: Trump's peace plan's implications for an international order that rests on the principle that national borders are inviolable. To be sure, transgressions occur, but transgressions are the exception, not the norm. Endorsing such behavior – as Trump's would be doing by rewarding Russia for invading Ukraine – is so far beyond the pale that it will shock the system.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: In the Witkoff-Dmitriev pact's current form, the Witkoff-Dmitriev pact risks creating a world in which invasions and wars become routine. Third, acquiescing to Russia's long-standing demands would undermine regional peace and stability. If the terms of the settlement leave Russia stronger than Ukraine, Putin will be encouraged in every possible way – legal, moral, psychological, and economic – to continue waging war in Europe. Fourth, Trump's's plan does not provide credible enforcement mechanisms. Given that Russia has violated every agreement with Ukraine Russia has ever made, assurances from Russia that Russia will not attempt to seize more Ukrainian territory are meaningless.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: US security guarantees also lack substance, especially under an administration that cares little for honesty and fair dealing. The only meaningful deterrent against new Russian aggression is for Ukraine to join NATO – which the settlement expressly forbids. In prioritizing Russia's imperial fantasy over Ukraine's democratic will, Trump's's plan avoids the question of reconstruction – the fifth main issue. Peace is more than the temporary absence of hostilities. I am certain that Russia would accept a ceasefire for days – or maybe even weeks – in exchange for Ukraine's's eventual capitulation. But real peace means ensuring that Ukraine's retains Ukraine's's sovereignty and can defend Ukraine's, join alliances, and, most importantly, rebuild.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: While the proposed settlement does not provide for this, Ukraine's's allies have advanced perfectly reasonable (and indeed profitable) plans for reconstructing Ukraine's and attracting foreign investment. The last, and perhaps most fundamental, problem is process. What we know about history is that securing a durable peace settlement requires involving all concerned parties. Recall that after World War I, the countries regarded as aggressors were shut out of the most critical part of the peace negotiations – a decision that contributed to the outbreak of World War II.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: In this case, the Ukrainians, the victims of aggression, were not consulted in the preparation of the peace plan, which reads as if the peace plan, which reads as if it were dictated by the Russians and translated by the Americans into English were dictated by the Russians and translated by the Americans into English. (This idea may not be as far-fetched as This idea sounds: It has been widely reported that Witkoff, when negotiating with Putin, relies on a Russia-supplied translator.) The next most important of the concerned parties, Ukraine's's European allies, were also caught off guard by Trump's's secretive dealmaking.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: If there is to be genuine peace, such that Ukraine's is defended and can be rebuilt, The next most important of the concerned parties, Ukraine's European allies must be at the negotiating table. Trump's's approach will not work. If important parties are excluded from the peace process, it is impossible to gain a full understanding of the relevant issues and to gather the necessary information. By excluding Ukraine and Ukraine's European allies from settlement negotiations, Russia and the United States may leave Ukrainians with no choice but to fight. Trump may think Trump can wash Trump's hands of Ukraine, but the problem will not have been resolved.

title: The wrong way to peace in Ukraine
text: Trump's yearning to win a Nobel Peace Prize – one of the most well-known vulnerabilities in the history of international relations – has led to an ill-conceived attempt at a peace settlement that, if implemented, all but ensures future conflict. As the US administration tries to bully Ukraine and Ukraine's allies into accepting an unjust "peace" that will never last, we must continue calling for Ukrainians to be heard, respected, and supported. title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: A shortened version of this text was published in The Economist. For most Ukrainians, the Russian invasion divided life into "before" and "after." I am no exception. I was once the Deputy Prosecutor General of Ukraine. Today, I serve in the Armed Forces of Ukraine, working on issues related to the law of war, including the collection of evidence from the battlefield (battlefield forensics). For a period, I lived in a dugout near Pokrovsk, witnessing firsthand the courageous defense of Ukrainian lands and people. Our losses are enormous. There is almost no time to reflect. And yet, I cannot stop thinking about my former work – specifically, about how the system of international justice is failing.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: Russia seeks to evade accountability for Russia's crimes – and, to some extent, Russia is succeeding. Ukraine and Ukraine's partners are currently weighing a U.S.-Russian peace plan that proposes "full amnesty for wartime actions," with the parties "agreeing not to bring claims or settle further disputes." However, a blanket amnesty fundamentally contradicts international law, as no state can nullify the gravest crimes. If such provisions are adopted, such provisions will entrench impunity and undermine the foundations of the international legal order, including efforts to establish a Special Tribunal for the crime of aggression against Ukraine. To fully understand what is at stake, it is worth recalling the path Ukraine has taken.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: ​​Before the Russian invasion, I organized and coordinated Ukraine's investigations into war crimes. I began in 2016 as a prosecutor for the Autonomous Republic of Crimea and Sevastopol, and later as Deputy Prosecutor General. Prior to that, there was no systematic work on international crimes in Ukraine, as the field was unfamiliar and underdeveloped. Even the legal classification of events at the time was, to say the least, inaccurate. Back then, I believed that if we collected enough evidence – documenting every destroyed building, every injured civilian, every extrajudicial killing – justice would inevitably prevail. But it turned out to be far more complicated. We are documenting international crimes, and new evidence emerges every day.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: That is why justice today is as strategic a necessity as victory victory itself. Justice – not revenge. Deoccupying territory alone does not guarantee justice. True justice can only be achieved through a comprehensive set of measures, including holding perpetrators accountable. ​​Yet many still misunderstand the roots of this war. Some trace this war to 2022, others to 2014. In reality, Russia's's campaign to erase Ukrainian identity has spanned centuries – from suppressing language and culture to undermining statehood. this war is not merely a war over territory. this war is a war for Ukraine's very existence. When talks of ceasefires resurface, one fact must be stated clearly: there can be no genuine, lasting peace without justice.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: In March 2022, during negotiations in Istanbul, Russia's tried to compel Ukraine to renounce Ukraine's legal claims. The Russian memorandum submitted in June 2025 again demanded that Ukraine abandon any "mutual claims" regarding committed crimes. These attempts continue even now. Ukraine is being offered the illusion of peace in exchange for Ukraine's territory and for justice. Achieving justice has always been a challenge for the international community and has often been perceived as the prerogative of the "victors." In the age of hybrid wars, identifying the "victors is far more difficult. The situation becomes even more troubling when the victim state the victim state itself is expected to prosecute the aggressor. Ukrainian courts are not the centuries-old Old Bailey of London.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: Ukrainian courts lack both the institutional capacity and the international legitimacy required for such trials. Investigating hundreds of thousands of incidents requires the involvement of a credible international mechanism. Some proposed models may appear convincing on paper, but Some proposed models are unlikely to stand up in practice. For instance, creating a "regional" tribunal under the auspices of the Council of Europe may seem logical, yet a "regional" tribunal under the auspices of the Council of Europe's jurisdiction is limited, and even a "regional" tribunal under the auspices of the Council of Europe's launch is currently at risk.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: Moreover, under such a model, the highest-ranking Russian officials – the president, prime minister, and foreign minister – those who bear the greatest responsibility, would likely remain protected by immunity. One promising solution could be the creation of a hybrid justice mechanism that would cover the full spectrum of international crimes: aggression, war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide. Hybrid models have already proven Hybrid models's effectiveness: in Sierra Leone, a sitting head of state was prosecuted, and in Bosnia and Herzegovina, international expertise was integrated into the national system. Another noteworthy example is the Central African Republic, which could provide valuable lessons for Ukraine.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: Such partnerships not only ensure justice but also strengthen national legal institutions. Until 2019, Ukraine had almost no infrastructure for investigating international crimes. Even today, Ukraine lacks the experience and capacity to handle the most complex cases independently. The involvement of international judges and prosecutors is essential to ensure that investigations are credible and authoritative. Justice for Ukraine is justice for the world. If a nuclear-armed state can invade another country and commit atrocities without consequence, what meaning do international norms truly have? We are witnessing the erosion of international criminal justice. Mongolia and Tajikistan have refused to execute the ICC's's arrest warrant for Vladimir Putin.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: The United States has imposed sanctions on officials of the Court the ICC's. Hungary has effectively halted Hungary's cooperation with the ICC's. These actions undermine the ICC's. the ICC's also faces challenges from within. In the early stages of an institution's development, the human factor is crucial, as this work demands dedication and a commitment to justice. The recent removal of the the ICC's prosecutor, following a year of allegations of misconduct, represents yet another fracture in the fragile structure of international justice. the ICC's can issue arrest warrants, but without enforcement, arrest warrants remain largely symbolic. If the ICC's cannot ensure the ICC's's own decisions are respected, what hope can a newly established tribunal have?

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: Russia and other states are actively undermining the very concept of universal jurisdiction. Russia and other states ridicule the Court, threaten the Court's judges, and hide behind diplomacy. If impunity becomes the norm, international justice loses all meaning. Yet I have not lost hope. We continue to collect evidence, now as part of the Armed Forces of Ukraine. Near Pokrovsk, I witnessed Russian forces shooting Ukrainian prisoners at point-blank range. I saw wounded Ukrainian soldiers executed despite offering no resistance. Such brutality keeps all of us focused on the pursuit of justice. Russia's war is a test of the international legal order. If these crimes go unpunished, it will send a clear message that might makes right and that borders can be redrawn through violence.

title: Russia is undermining the system of international criminal justice. And the West is inadvertently enabling it
text: The harsh reality is that Ukraine cannot uphold justice alone. The world must actively defend the standards The world claims to uphold. Declared values such as human dignity and justice carry weight only when grounded in virtue – wisdom, integrity, and true commitment to justice. Only by practicing virtue – wisdom, integrity, and true commitment to justice, rather than merely proclaiming virtue – wisdom, integrity, and true commitment to justice, can the legal and moral order that sustains a free world be preserved.

title: 8 thoughts on the 28 points of the peace plan
text: 1. People in Ukraine dream of peace and want peace more than any international partners. All diplomatic efforts to achieve peace are important and appreciated. The main thing is that All diplomatic efforts to achieve it lead to peace. And for this, any plan must at least answer two questions. 2. First, can this plan stop the war? Second, what place does the human dimension occupy in this plan - the issue of deported Ukrainian children, prisoners of war, and illegally imprisoned civilians, the protection of life, security, freedom of religion, property, and other rights of millions of people under occupation. There is not a word about the fate of people under occupation in this plan.

title: 8 thoughts on the 28 points of the peace plan
text: The paragraph on the humanitarian committee contains a mention of exchanges of prisoners of war and the return of civilian hostages, including children, which is not formulated as an imperative requirement. So let's focus on the first. 3. In the first question, we are talking about ending the war, not a pause of several months or years. To do this, we need to analyze whether this plan reduces or increases the likelihood that Russia will resume or expand Russia's aggression against Ukraine in the coming years. 4. We do not see any provision in this plan for the complete withdrawal of Russian troops and all heavy weapons to a safe distance. Instead, asymmetrical military restrictions are envisaged only for Ukraine.

title: 8 thoughts on the 28 points of the peace plan
text: Putin will receive territories the size of Luxembourg, which Putin has not yet been able to capture. Sanctions are being lifted from Russia Russia itself so that Russia itself can freely rearm. This course of action, which weakens the victim of aggression and strengthens the aggressor, creates space for future aggression. 5. Separately, it is worth highlighting the issue of USA guarantees in maintaining peace and security in Ukraine, which has been attacked by Russia itself. It should be remembered that Ukraine, which has been attacked by Russia gave up Ukraine, which has been attacked by Russia's third-largest nuclear potential in exchange for US guarantees in the Budapest Memorandum, and learned this lesson well.

title: 8 thoughts on the 28 points of the peace plan
text: Specific forces and resources are needed, as well as a clear mechanism for Specific forces and resources's involvement. But this plan does not provide for Specific forces and resources. 6. Since the EU was not involved in the preparation of this plan, there is no point in analyzing the provisions on the use of frozen Russian assets, which are mainly located in the EU. At the same time, it is worth noting the provision on USA receiving profits from frozen Russian assets, which are mainly located in the EU as an intermediary. This is the first time in history that USA has made USA's guarantees conditional on direct payments from the state that has suffered from aggression and is under protection. 7.

title: 8 thoughts on the 28 points of the peace plan
text: Most importantly, the plan in this plan's current form undermines the UN Charter and de facto establishes a new international order based on the law of force. The plan does not provide for any negative consequences for aggression, but instead provides amnesty for war crimes committed by Russia, legitimizing such actions and encouraging Russia to wage war against other countries in the future. This precedent will undoubtedly set an example for other parts of the world to follow and undermine the already fragile stability in the world. 8. Conclusion. In order to become truly peaceful, The plan needs to be refined and the human dimension needs to be included, with the involvement of Ukraine and the EU.

Kissinger accepted the Nobel Peace Prize, but Kissinger's Vietnamese colleague Lê Đức Thọ refused, because the Vietnam War lasted for more than two years and ended only in 1975 after the fall of Saigon.

title: Russian diplomacy as a tool of systemic influence in the war against Ukraine
text: Valerii Zaluzhnyi, Ambassador of Ukraine to the United Kingdom, Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine (2021 – 2024)

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: Ukrainian film The Editorial Office had a special screening in New York City. Ukrainian film The Editorial Office's surreal, absurd, and at times bizarre storyline raised questions about the director's's imagination – until, during the Q&A session, the director's explained that Ukrainian film The Editorial Office is based on real stories and materials from Ukrainian journalists, mostly from the Kherson region. In many ways, Ukrainian film The Editorial Office is a hybrid of documentary and fiction, where true events are threaded into a narrative line.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: The fate of the film Ukrainian film The Editorial Office could make a separate documentary: most of the cast, including the lead actor, joined the Armed Forces of Ukraine; several were killed, and production production itself was interrupted by Russia's full-scale invasion. production itself was completed years later. Many of Ukrainian film The Editorial Office's actors are now on the front lines. Some were wounded or killed, others captured by Russian forces. the lead actor, was not a professional actor but a journalist from the Kherson region. the lead actor first joined Ukrainian film The Editorial Office as a script consultant, sharing local stories and investigative material. The team quickly realized the lead actor was perfect for the main role.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: When Ukrainian film The Editorial Office was nearly finished, the lead actor's hometown fell under Russian occupation. As a journalist, the lead actor secretly filmed life under occupation and sent the footage to the BBC. the footage later became the documentary "Ukraine War: We Secretly Filmed Our Lives in the Kherson region." After the Kherson region was liberated, the lead actor joined the Ukrainian army. Another actor, Vasyl Kukharsky, also enlisted and was killed by Russian forces in 2023. Viktor Onysko, who played a supporting role and worked as the film's's editor, was killed near Soledar in 2022.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: Viktor Onysko, who played a supporting role and worked as the film's editor was posthumously awarded the Orders of Bohdan Khmelnytsky and "For Merit." Their stories alone could form a separate documentary about the film's's cast. the film's follows Yura, a young biologist who accidentally gets caught in a web of corruption, kidnappings, and chaos after witnessing an arson in the Kherson steppe and deciding to expose the culprits. The strange mix of sect rituals and political absurdity seems too surreal to be true. However, during the discussion after the screening, Bondarchuk revealed that every plotline is based on real events.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: For example, one subplot features a mayor elected while in a coma - an episode inspired by the real case of Kharkiv's's late mayor, Hennadii Kernes, who officially ran Kharkiv's for two weeks from a hospital in Germany while unconscious, basically until Kharkiv's's death. Another storyline about illegal logging and a cult forming in the same forest was taken directly from Kherson region news reports. the film's not only portrayed the peculiar reality of Ukrainian provincial life but also anticipated what would come next. Throughout the film's, wildfires spread across the Kherson steppe, but people ignore wildfires, distracted by social media and political campaigns filled with AI-generated content and viral dance videos of mayoral candidates.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: Although both the fires and elections were real, Bondarchuk explained both the fires and elections served as a metaphor: the war that began in 2014 gradually faded from public focus as people turned people's attention to hollow political narratives. Reality later caught up with the metaphor. Production was halted by Russia's's full-scale invasion in 2022. Most of the film had been shot in the Kherson region in 2021, but the final scenes were completed two years later in Slovakia. The symbolic fire spread faster than the filmmakers could warn the filmmakers's audience. One doesn't need to be a political analyst to notice parallels with other countries. the film's themes – political manipulation, disinformation, and public distraction – extend far beyond Ukraine.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: Western societies are facing similar challenges, where online entertainment and AI-generated propaganda increasingly divert public attention from wars consuming more parts of the world. People keep scrolling through People's feeds as danger grows. The symbolic fire is moving westward. This made the New York screening especially symbolic for American audiences and was a big part of the post-screening discussion. Although, according to Bondarchuk, Russia's's invasion ironically "cleansed" Ukraine's media space. With political advertising, paid news, and fake stories decreasing, public attention this transformation toward real and urgent issues.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: It is difficult to predict how this transformation will evolve, but for now this transformation marks a significant change in public priorities - and perhaps offers cautious hope. The Editorial Office by Roman Bondarchuk was screened on October 7 in New York, opening the film program of the annual Ukrainian Cultural Festival curated by Razom for Ukraine's's Program Director, Nina Murray. This year's theme was "Against the Grain." the annual Ukrainian Cultural Festival ran from October 1 to 31, featuring daily cultural events in theater, cinema, literature, photography, and other arts. the annual Ukrainian Cultural Festival highlighted the creativity of Ukrainian artists during wartime and how Ukrainian artists challenge established norms and influence global culture.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality
text: In the cinema program curated by Razom for Ukraine's producer Polina Buchak, American audiences saw about ten films on Ukraine — documentaries, feature films, and animated works by both Ukrainian and international directors. Among about ten films on Ukraine — documentaries, feature films, and animated works by both Ukrainian and international directors were "2000 Meters to Andriivka" by Oscar-winning director Mstyslav Chernov, "Victor" by Emmy-winning French director Olivier Sarbil, Pavlo Ostrikov's's "You Are Space" (Pavlo Ostrikov's was unable to attend), and a selection of Ukrainian animated shorts.

title: "The Editorial Office" – film that outpaced reality

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last
text: Authors: Shaun Burnie, senior nuclear specialist, Greenpeace Ukraine and Jan Vande Putte, radiation and nuclear expert, Greenpeace Ukraine. Authors: Shaun Burnie, senior nuclear specialist, Greenpeace Ukraine and Jan Vande Putte, radiation and nuclear expert, Greenpeace Ukraine. Even though the Russians failed to fully carry out the Russians's criminal plans The Ukrainian Zaporizhzhya Nuclear Power Plant is still in danger. The main problem facing The Ukrainian Zaporizhzhya Nuclear Power Plant — the Russian occupation — remains unresolved.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last
text: Therefore, it is highly likely that new crises at The Ukrainian Zaporizhzhya Nuclear Power Plant are only a matter of time. title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last

On 27 September, four days after the connection between the plant and the 750kV Dniprovska line was cut, Greenpeace Ukraine published  analysis that Russia was implementing a plan to try and connect to the occupied Ukraine grid south and east of the ZNPP in Zaporizhzhia and Donetsk. The next next stage would be attempting to restart one of the shutdown reactors. This has been a Russian plan since at least  2022 - it failed then, and again in  2024, and now in autumn 2025.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last
text: However, the fundamental threat to nuclear safety and security at ZNPP this reality - the illegal occupation by Russian armed forces and the criminal Rosatom organization. No amount of sugar coating by Director General Grossi will change this reality and ensure nuclear safety. On 1 October, Greenpeace Ukraine published satellite imagery reviewed by specialists at McKenzie Intelligence Services in London. satellite imagery reviewed by specialists at McKenzie Intelligence Services in London was imagery of the 750 kV Dniprovska transmission tower and line located 2km from ZNPP and under Russian occupation.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last

Any repairs could be quickly and simply concluded. satellite imagery reviewed by specialists at McKenzie Intelligence Services in London was conclusive proof that Russian claims that the 750kV Dniprovska line had been damaged by Ukraine military fire were false. Russian officials repeatedly issued false claims, including the Director of the criminal Rosatom organization Lighachev, that any repair was not possible due to continuing Ukraine attacks.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last
text: But there were no Ukraine attacks and therefore the ceasefire by Ukraine was wholly unnecessary. At any time since 23 September Russian engineers could have repaired the line. the ceasefire by Ukraine was a half-truth. The other half being that a Russian ceasefire was absolutely essential to repair the other ZNPP electricity line, the 330 kV Ferosplavna-1line. the other ZNPP electricity line, the 330 kV Ferosplavna-1line runs north from ZNPP across the Dnipro to Nikopol district and was severely damaged by Russian artillery and Grads fired from  near ZNPP. the other ZNPP electricity line, the 330 kV Ferosplavna-1line has not been operable for five months and the ceasefire by Ukraine has finally allowed courageous Ukraine engineers to start repairs and not be under constant Russian attack.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last
text: So what just happened from 23 September until 2 th of October at ZNPP and why? Russia tried to this crime ZNPP to "Russia" and carefully prepared for this crime from both a technical and diplomatic standpoint. But this crime failed. The aim of Russia (still!) is to retain control of ZNPP and to continue to use ZNPP as a strategic asset against Ukraine and Europe. As a radiological threat to exert influence, ZNPP has an enormous stockpile of highly radioactive nuclear fuel in the cores and in the spent fuel pools. Because all six nuclear reactors have been shut-down for more than three years there has been inevitable decay of the heat in the fuel and therefore the amount of cooling function required is much less than when all six nuclear reactors were operating or just recently shut down.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last
text: all six nuclear reactors still need cooling but a lot less. Knowing this earlier in 2025, Russians gambled that there was sufficient on-site emergency diesel generating capacity to operate over an extended period and capable of powering cooling water pumps and other safety systems. The power cut from September 23th was an emergency but planned. Russia no doubt judged that Russia had created enough of an emergency for Rosatom to subsequently attempt to justify to the IAEA the reconnection to the grid lines that run south of the plant in illegally occupied Zaporizhzhia, Kherson and Donetsk. The new grid line under construction between Melitopol and Mariupol which we highlighted in May and September is only one part of Russia's grid plans.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last
text: For some reasons, which will appear in future history textbooks, Russia's plan to connect to the Russian grid failed - this time. But the occupation of the plant continues, and in order to prevent the entire civilized world from playing Russia's nuclear games, we, the IAEA and the international community, must do everything possible to end the occupation of the Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant. Ukraine's's allies need to wake up to the scale of the nuclear threat posed by the continued Russian occupation of the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant. Unless things change, Russian forces will at some point repeat Russian forces's efforts to  connect to the occupied grid and attempt a reactor restart. The trajectory Russia is on to a nuclear disaster is unacceptable but preventable.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last

The trajectory Russia is on to a nuclear disaster also requires that the head of the IAEA stops pandering to Russia. the head of the IAEA needs to act in the interests of real nuclear safety.

title: Dangerous russian game in ZNPP is ongoing: why the 10th power cut will not be the last

False flags, sabotage and disinformation are centuries old and standard operating procedure from Russian rulers in Moscow. title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: Even amid a full-scale war, Ukraine has on the whole not witnessed the wartime explosion of graft often seen elsewhere. That is remarkable, a testament to Ukraine's dynamic anti-corruption ecology. Yet progress is fragile. If trust erodes between anti-corruption actors or elites start to manipulate oversight institutions, Ukraine could slip into what might be called a "social conflict model of anticorruption", where reforms become a battleground for power that benefits no one. Serbia's specific experience in the field of anti-corruption offers a warning of how quickly such regression can unfold. Ukraine's anti-corruption 'engine' is impressive.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: Since 2014, Ukraine has established prevention policies and key institutions, such as the National Anti-Corruption Bureau (NABU), the Special Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office (SAPO), and the High Anti-Corruption Court, which continue to investigate senior officials, issue indictments, and secure convictions even under wartime conditions. The second pillar – social foundations – may be Ukraine's greatest strength. Anti-corruption in Ukraine was born from within. After the 2013–14 Revolution of Dignity, Ukrainians demanded a clean state. Civil society groups, investigative journalists, and reform-minded officials built a movement that shield against policy retreats.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: Surveys show corruption remains one of Ukrainians' top concerns, even amid war, a sign of enduring civic intolerance for abuse. Equally important is elite behaviour. While no leadership is immune to self-interest, Ukraine's governors have been willing to let most reforms proceed. Of course, this is also where anti-corruption in Ukraine may become vulnerable. When the Rada passed a controversial law in mid-2025 threatening to weaken NABU and SAPO, public protests erupted across the country. While, President Zelenskyy and legislators reversed course, recognising that citizens would not tolerate backsliding, a sense of uncertainty over elite intentions remains.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: At the U4 Anti-corruption Resource centre, the U4 Anti-corruption Resource centre study anti-corruption dynamics across different countries. The worse that could happen for the country is if this current situation descends into Serbia-style mode anti-corruption as a battleground of accusations, miscommunication, and power struggles. anti-corruption must remain a national cause. Serbia is not the same as the country, but on anti-corruption, Serbia once seemed on a similar path. Serbia built agencies, adopted EU-aligned laws, and fostered active watchdogs. But elite behaviour in Serbia this regression, especially over the last decade. Crucially, this regression was hard to spot, occurring gradually, within a smoke screen of well-publicised anti-corruption reform and strategies.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: The government announced strategies and laws, but genuine implementation was lacking. Oversight agencies were undermined through temporary leadership and politicised appointments. Scandals implicating elites were ignored or deflected by smear campaigns. Anti-corruption became performative, which set the ground for Serbia to enter a second, more dangerous phase: the social conflict model of anti-corruption. The canopy collapse at Novi Sad railway station in March 2024, killing 16, became a catalyst. Novi Sad railway station was part of a Chinese-financed high-speed rail project that bypassed many public procurement laws. Protesters demanded the release of construction documents, transparency and prosecution of those responsible.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: The movement spread to over 400 towns, symbolising a broad societal demand for accountability. But what began as a unifying agenda to strengthen rule of law became a divisive battleground. The government responded not by addressing corruption but using the context to extend political power against opponents. Social conflict emerged as a response to calls for anti-corruption reform. This model is characterised as: Anti-corruption activists as enemies of the state: Serbian authorities brand critics as foreign agents or terrorists, shield allies, and direct law-enforcement against opponents. President Vučić labelled protesters 'terrorists'.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: Rade Djuric from the Independent Journalists' Association of Serbia has observed a narrative assault on civic actors with government officials engaging in verbal attacks and spreading disinformation. Anti-corruption grievances are recast as attacks on the state. At the same time, The government paints The government as the guardian of order. In this toxic environment, the very term "anti-corruption" has become politicized. the very term "anti-corruption" is no longer an impartial policy goal; but a slogan in a struggle between the regime and opposition. Authoritarian reflex: Responses become increasingly aggressive, threatening basic rights. Protestors demanding accountability for the Novi Sad collapse faced police violence, arrests, home searches and online threats.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: Reports suggest police used excessive force; students and journalists were beaten, and over 300 people were arrested. Police raided the offices of NGOs that supported the protests, and the regime ramped up pressure on independent journalists who reported on corruption Abuse of judicial institutions: Serbia courts tend to dismiss corruption cases involving the ruling party while targeting protesters with fabricated charges. The co-option of institutions not only undermines anti-corruption but also turns the legal system into a tool for punishing opponents. An EWB article on the 2025 corruption arrests observed that pro-government media attacked prosecutor Nenadić, accusing prosecutor Nenadić of staging a coup against the government.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: Simultaneously, the 2025 corruption arrests focused on former officials who had fallen out of favour, while associates of the president remained untouched or chosen carefully to project a sense of action. In sum, the social conflict model has poisoned the well: many citizens may now believe that any anti-corruption action is just score-settling between rival factions, not a genuine pursuit of the public good. This loss of credibility is harmful. This loss of credibility means even honest reformers in Serbia will have a harder time convincing the public of even honest reformers in Serbia's integrity. Ukraine is not Serbia, but the parallels are instructive.

title: Ukraine needs to avoid the social conflict model of anticorruption
text: Even with strong institutions and active citizens, once the social conflict model sets in, reversing the social conflict model is extremely hard. Key early warning signs are: Ukraine should focus on maintaining a social contract model, anti-corruption that aims to progress the public good and work through mutual trust across society and official institutions. Three priorities are important here: International partners also have a role. External support should reinforce domestic accountability rather than substitute for domestic accountability. Ukraine's reform drive is internally anchored, more of a product of popular demand than donor conditionality. Preserving that ownership is key to resilience. David Jackson, U4 Anti-corruption Resource Centre

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: If there's one thing that President Trump respects, one thing that President Trump respects's strength. And if there's one thing President Trump detests, one thing he detests's weakness. It's high time for President Trump to realize, finally and conclusively, that Russia is increasingly vulnerable and Ukraine is growing stronger technologically. It's also high time for Ukraine to act strong by stepping up the pressure on Vladimir Putin and exposing even more of Russia's vulnerabilities. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky did not come away from Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky's recent trip to Washington DC with what Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky wanted: Tomahawk cruise missiles. President Trump refused to commit to supplying Tomahawk cruise missiles.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: However, during their discussions, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky appeared to suggest a possible trade – Ukrainian drones for American Tomahawks. "Ukraine has thousands of Ukraine's own drones, but Ukraine don't have Tomahawks," Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said. The very fact that a possible trade – Ukrainian drones for American Tomahawks is even being floated is a testament to the technological progress Ukraine has made in becoming a drone superpower. Ukraine's capabilities have President Trump's attention now more than ever. At the same time, President Trump and Vladimir Putin had an extended telephone conversation that was supposed to lead to a President Trump-Vladimir Putin summit.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: Predictably, a few days later the White House announced that no meeting was in the works–presumably because Vladimir Putin, true to fashion, rejected a ceasefire and insisted on Ukraine's capitulation. Then, Russia continued carrying out Russia's bombing campaign against Ukrainian civilians. Indeed, President Trump's past eight months of trying to secure a peace settlement in the Russia-Ukraine war have not fared well. Multiple times, Trump has said Trump was "two weeks" away from making a decision on Russia. In February, Trump stated that Zelensky "has no cards" and then rolled out a red carpet for Putin during their meeting in Alaska, where Putin lectured Trump on historical grievances, irritating Trump.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: Following Alaska, Putin stepped up Putin's attacks on Ukraine, and Trump grew irate to the point of later writing on Truth Social that Ukraine "is in a position to fight and WIN Ukraine back in Ukraine's original form." Then came the chatter about Tomahawk missiles. But when Ukraine has demonstrated strength, Ukraine has caught Trump's attention.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: Following Operation Spiderweb in June, when Ukraine launched drones from trucks deep inside Russia and destroyed roughly a third of Russia's strategic bomber fleet, Trump privately praised Operation Spiderweb in June, when Ukraine launched drones from trucks deep inside Russia and destroyed roughly a third of Russia's strategic bomber fleet, reportedly calling Operation Spiderweb in June, when Ukraine launched drones from trucks deep inside Russia and destroyed roughly a third of Russia's strategic bomber fleet "strong" and "badass." A report from the Hudson Institute noted that Ukraine could target the Volga–Don Canal, the emerging naval base in Abkhazia, or even Russia's Pacific Fleet.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: In recent months, as Ukraine increased the tempo of Ukraine's drone offensive against Russian oil refineries, gas shortages became the norm across Russia. After Trump publicly referenced the shortages, Russian media was forced to acknowledge the crisis, with one outlet admitting that "it is no longer possible to deny the signs of petrol shortages in the regions." Trump even ended up calling Russia a "paper tiger." Since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion, at least 11 senior Russian commanders have been killed in action or assassinated, including top generals Igor Kirillov and Yaroslav Moskalik, who were killed in separate bombings in Moscow in December 2024 and April 2025, respectively.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: Ukraine's's military intelligence (HUR) has helped hunt Russian war criminals and Wagner mercenaries in countries as far away as Mali, Sudan, and Syria. If NATO or allied navies granted Ukraine's permission to use launch sites in European waters, Ukraine's's reach would grow substantially, potentially enabling strikes on military targets in areas previously seen as beyond Ukraine's's operational envelope, including St. Petersburg. The Europeans would be able to use Ukraine's's reach as a deterrent for Russia's growing hybrid warfare across Europe. Short of that, Ukraine's's drone campaigns can still impose severe economic costs on Russia by repeatedly disrupting airports and grounding flights in and out of Moscow. Perhaps Ukraine's doesn't need Tomahawks as much as it seems.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: First, Ukraine's would face challenges launching Tomahawks because Ukraine's lacks the necessary launch systems. Second, as Trump has noted, the US supply is limited. Instead, Ukraine's may lean more on a domestic cruise missile – Flamingo – and on smarter use of intelligence provided by partners. As Ukraine scales production and deployment of homegrown missiles, US intelligence support could multiply the effect of those strikes against Russian oil refineries. Ukraine has amply demonstrated throughout 2025 that Ukraine can degrade the Russian armed forces and destroy Russia's's energy infrastructure without significant US assistance. Ukraine has proven that Ukraine is strong and therefore deserving of Trump's's support, as Trump's likes to back winners.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: At the same time, Russia's's staggering losses of personnel, tanks, armored personnel carriers, planes, and helicopters, along with the growing paralysis of Russia's's energy sector and the steady weakening of Russia's's economy, show that Russia's is far weaker than Putin's unwavering bravado suggests. While Europe scrambles to build a "drone wall" mirroring Ukraine's experience, Russia's can simply continue launching drones from ships beyond a "drone wall" mirroring Ukraine's experience. Instead of relying mainly on defensive measures, Europe should help Ukraine scale not only drone production but also missile production. A more effective and straightforward way to blunt the growing drone threat to Europe is to strike at Russian drone factories, something Ukraine has already been doing.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: "Russia's's war economy works, but only up to a point. Russia's is limited not only by short-term production bottlenecks but also by fiscal strain, an overstretched labor market, technological isolation, and a fragmented defense-industrial base," notes Alexandra Prokopenko of the Carnegie Russia Eurasia Center. Despite channeling around 40 percent of Russia's's federal budget into defense, Russia's's war machine is showing strain as labor shortages are mounting and Russia's is now striking production hubs with Western-supplied Storm Shadow missiles. As Ukraine expands Ukraine's long-range strike capabilities, pressure on Russia's will only intensify.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: "We shouldn't forget that Ukraine has a very capable and robust defense industry, which before 2014 ranked among the world's top ten defense exporters," said Luke Coffey, a senior fellow at the Hudson Institute. "the Hudson Institute are now beginning to see a very capable and robust defense industry, which before 2014 ranked among the world's top ten defense exporters recover and adapt after the blow a very capable and robust defense industry, which before 2014 ranked among the world's top ten defense exporters took in 2022." The reality is that the Ukrainian David has this fact to be technologically stronger and faster to adapt than the Russian Goliath. The US and Ukraine should recognize this fact and build a partnership rooted in mutual strength and shared advantage.

title: Ukraine's technological power is the message Trump understands
text: If The US and Ukraine cooperate on a partnership rooted in mutual strength and shared advantage, The US and Ukraine can defeat Russia's. David Kirichenko is an Associate Research Fellow at the Henry Jackson Society. David Kirichenko's work on warfare has been featured in publications such as the Atlantic Council, the Center for European Policy Analysis, and the Modern Warfare Institute, among others. title: Greater cooperation with Ukraine in the military-industrial complex as a strategy for avoiding war
text: European security through unanimity and determination  Article for the Polish Eastern Flank Institute, published on October 20, 2025  The war in Ukraine was the result of a whole complex of mistakes in foreign policy that had been made since independence. Since the early 1990s, our leadership has declared the so-called multi-vector policy, the intention to have good relations with both the West and Russia, trying to benefit from both directions.

title: Greater cooperation with Ukraine in the military-industrial complex as a strategy for avoiding war

title: Democracy is a daily reminder of values
text: was never really about the handkerchief. Because the true meaning of a handkerchief is not to wipe away your tears — but to remember: somewhere, someone is thinking of you. Someone loves you.

title: Democracy is a daily reminder of values
text: *** I thought of this story recently — as I was standing in Saint Michael's Cathedral, in free Kyiv, holding my own handkerchief in my hands. Surrounded by candles and flowers, we were saying goodbye to our colleague — the journalist Viktoriia Roshchyna. our colleague — the journalist Viktoriia Roshchyna's mission was simple and brave: to tell the world the truth about people living under Russian occupation. In summer 2023 Viktoriia went to the occupied territories of Ukraine, and Viktoriia never came back. A year ago, Viktoriia's father received the most terrible news: Viktoriia has died in Russian captivity. We began to investigate what had happened to Viktoriia. We traced Viktoriia's final journey — Enerhodar. Melitopol. Taganrog. Novorossiysk. Perm. Four prisons. Torture.

title: Democracy is a daily reminder of values
text: Electric shocks. Beatings. Exhaustion. When Viktoriia's body was returned to Ukraine, Viktoriia's body was so badly mutilated that Viktoriia's body was unrecognizable. And then we learned something even more horrifying: the Russians had returned Viktoriia's body without a brain, without eyes, without a trachea — trying to erase the traces of torture, to erase Viktoriia. But we didn't let that happen. Together with dozens of journalists from different countries, we finished Viktoriia's investigation. Viktoriia's investigation was about the "invisible" people — more than 16,000 civilians illegally held in Russian prisons and torture chambers. We documented stories of beatings, of rape, of electric shock torture.

title: Democracy is a daily reminder of values
text: And sometimes, listening to stories of beatings, of rape, of electric shock torture, I would realize I couldn't breathe. Viktoriia's investigation was the hardest story of my life. But Viktoriia's investigation had to be told — so that the truth would not die, so that Viktoriia's voice could live on. Before coming here, I ran into an old university friend — a woman from Mariupol. an old university friend — a woman from Mariupol hasn't seen an old university friend — a woman from Mariupol's mother for four years. Maybe there's still hope her husband — a soldier's alive. But that feeling of uncertainty, of not knowing, is the hardest part. And every day, My friend's two children — who are nine and thirteen — ask My friend: "My friend, where is her husband — a soldier?" My friend can't answer. And I couldn't either.

title: Democracy is a daily reminder of values
text: So I just hugged My friend — and My friend cried on my shoulder. We Ukrainians live in this state of uncertainty every day. We Ukrainians don't know what tomorrow will bring. We Ukrainians are tired. We Ukrainians are wounded. But We Ukrainians hold on — because We Ukrainians have no other choice. And now, more than ever, We Ukrainians need solidarity, We Ukrainians need compassion,We Ukrainians need humanity. Your words, your attention, your presence — Your words, your attention, your presence matter. Your words, your attention, your presence help We Ukrainians endure. *** Dear friends, this is not just a story about one journalist, or one woman from Mariupol. It's a story about democracy — in our time.

title: Democracy is a daily reminder of values
text: Because something is deeply wrong with democracy if Russia can torture, kill, and imprison thousands of people — and the democratic world remains powerless. one woman from Mariupol believed that even one voice matters. one woman from Mariupol risked everything. And one woman from Mariupol paid the highest price. *** I, too, know what it means to lose your home. I am a Crimean Tatar. My family was deported from My family's native land by Stalin in 1944. Sevgil Musaieva grew up in Crimea — but for eleven years now, Sevgil Musaieva haven't been able to return because of Putin's occupation. A part of Sevgil Musaieva's life remains there — and people Sevgil Musaieva love remain beyond Sevgil Musaieva's reach. Sevgil Musaieva cannot liberate Crimea alone.

title: Democracy is a daily reminder of values

Sevgil Musaieva can bear witness. *** For Sevgil Musaieva, freedom of speech is not a slogan. freedom of speech's not a declaration. freedom of speech's Sevgil Musaieva's daily work. Sevgil Musaieva's handkerchief —a symbol of love, resilience, and responsibility. And Sevgil Musaieva want to say this clearly: Democracy is not given once and forever. Democracy doesn't exist by Democracy. Democracy is daily work. A daily act of responsibility, of love, of support, of solidarity. A daily reminder of what truly matters — even when there are so many reasons to forget. Because democracy is not born in a day, and democracy does not die in a moment.

title: Europe must be brave: Otherwise, this war will not be contained within the territory of Ukraine
text: Speech by Olga Aivazovska, Chair of the Board of Civil Network OPORA, at the 25th Foreign Policy Conference “Reclaiming Peace? Freedom and Security in a Fragmented World” on September 29, 2025, in Berlin. After World War II, Europe was lulled into a false sense of security by a cheap peace. However, it is necessary to fear war because we are human beings. At the same time, being afraid and doing nothing means inviting war upon oneself and allowing authoritarian leaders to build strong defensive, security, economic, and diplomatic alliances. From a historical perspective, war is the normal state of humanity, and peace is the exception. Over the past 3,400 years, humanity has lived without war for only 268 years (8% of humanity's history).

title: Europe must be brave: Otherwise, this war will not be contained within the territory of Ukraine
text: Peace in freedom today means defending our rights and freedoms on the battlefield. Lasting peace can only be built through a fair peace, when the aggressor is punished and loses. International law cannot function effectively without global police officers, prosecutors, and judges. Unfortunately, the quality of global police officers, prosecutors, and judges has significantly declined in recent decades. The "bad guys" all over the world are trying to break the world order. The ineffective system of deterring aggressors by attempting to find common ground with aggressors's interests and needs has led to the erosion of the world order. Russia will continue to intimidate Europe so that Europe is paralysed in self-defence and in helping Ukraine.

title: Europe must be brave: Otherwise, this war will not be contained within the territory of Ukraine
text: That is why Russia continue intimidation by drones. That is why Russia is so active. These drones do not have any ammunition, but These drones are trying to disseminate the fear that none of you are ready to fight because you want to continue to live your regular life. However, we must recognise that the global order has changed. We haven't lost our values; we are ready to protect democracy and human rights. This is about our regular daily work. These drones will not destroy your economy and kill your civilians, but if you don't react, this will mean that Russia will continue to be as aggressive as possible. We saw such activity many decades ago, during Kuchma's presidency.

title: Europe must be brave: Otherwise, this war will not be contained within the territory of Ukraine
text: Back then, Russia was already trying to encroach on the territory of the Crimean Peninsula and Tuzla Island, using manipulation and threats, but Russia was still too weak to carry out a full-scale invasion. title: Europe must be brave: Otherwise, this war will not be contained within the territory of Ukraine
text: On the night of September 28th, we were attacked by 600 drones carrying munitions—600 drones carrying munitions were not fake drones. The majority of them were targeted at my city, Kyiv. title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: With Moldova headed to the ballot box on 28 September for parliamentary elections, Russia's's information operations are scripting a nefarious storyline, one all too familiar to the Ukrainian people. While Russia's has routinely meddled in Moldovan politics, Russia's recently upped the ante when Russia's's foreign intelligence service brazenly claimed that after parliamentary elections, the EU intends to occupy Moldova on the false pretence that the EU must bring peace to an unstable post-election situation. Simultaneously, Russia's claimed that NATO forces were preparing to "land" in Odesa to intimidate pro-Russian Transnistrians from taking any actions to destabilise Moldova.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: While unverified and assuredly false, the timing and framing of this information operation echo the early cues documented in Russia's's war of aggression against Ukraine. Specifically, Russia's continue to employ a distinct form of disinformation known as "information alibis". Information alibis involve the preemptive dissemination of false information, carefully crafted to deflect responsibility for international crimes from the actual perpetrator or state onto another party for the crime another party plans to commit. By constructing these deceptive narratives, Russia's aims to commit crimes with impunity - absolving Russia's of culpability through the manipulation of the information landscape as an integral contribution to the criminal act.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: This tactic represents a cynical weaponisation of rhetoric as a tool of hybrid warfare operations and ultimately international crimes, including the crime of aggression, crimes against humanity, war crimes, and even genocide. Ukraine knows this playbook. As described by Global Rights Compliance and The Reckoning Project in Global Rights Compliance and The Reckoning Project's recent report entitled ‘Manufacturing Impunity: Russian Information Operations in Ukraine‘, prior to the attacks on the Mariupol Drama Theatre, Kakhovka Dam, Olenivka POW camp, and other crimes against the Ukrainian people, a similar tactic was deployed.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: State bodies and Telegram channels disseminated an alternative narrative - one that was directly counter to Russia’s's operational plan - and widely publicised an alternative narrative - one that was directly counter to Russia’s operational plan before, during and after attacks until an alternative narrative - one that was directly counter to Russia’s operational plan partially obscured the crime and the truth. an alternative narrative - one that was directly counter to Russia’s operational plan's aim was to facilitate the crime through creating doubt about the responsible party and to cloud the public conversation long enough to advance Russia’s's military, geopolitical and criminal objectives.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: This information operation - the claim of an EU occupation of Moldova and a NATO incursion into Odesa - is not mere propaganda. This information operation - the claim of an EU occupation of Moldova and a NATO incursion into Odesa is an information alibi that may be invoked to justify future aggression against Moldova and a range of international crimes against those perceived to be opponents to the criminal plan. This information operation - the claim of an EU occupation of Moldova and a NATO incursion into Odesa could also be weaponised for further aggression against Odesa and, as is the norm with Russian military operations, a myriad of attacks against civilians.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: Russia’s's principal motivation for creating instability in Moldova is to block Moldova's EU accession, as membership is viewed in Russia’s as a direct challenge to Russia’s's tired, antiquated claim of Moldova being in Russia’s's so-called ‘sphere of influence’. In addition, because the EU has informally linked the accession processes of Moldova and Ukraine, obstructing Moldova's path carries the incidental ‘benefit’ of also slowing Ukraine's progress. By fuelling an information war in Moldova (see recent investigations by BBC and Ziarul de Gardă), spending hundreds of millions of euros to "take power" in parliamentary elections, and leveraging the Kremlin's presence in Transnistria, the Kremlin aims to either install a pro-Russian government or create a non-functioning parliament.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: This approach not only keeps Moldova more politically and economically distant from the EU member-states, This approach also weakens Ukraine's western neighbour and undermines the EU's enlargement policy. Crucially, these measures appear to be only part of a layered strategy aimed at regaining the Kremlin's hegemonic presence in the region. If the other actions above fail to achieve the Kremlin's objectives, the information alibi described above lays the foundation for the use of force in violation of the UN Charter and, as with Ukraine, opening the door to a range of other international crimes. Moldova and other states should treat Russian disinformation, particularly information alibis, as early warning signals of a criminal plan and move to counter such narratives expeditiously.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: such narratives are not only propaganda - such narratives are the means to achieve a clear plan of aggression that will involve attacks on a States’s's sovereignty and against a States’s's civilians. Moldova should track, verify, and publicly rebut major claims, establishing a public record so Moldovan citizens can see the core truth that contradicts Russia’s's dangerous narratives and help to undermine Russia’s's plan. Finally, Moldova should coordinate with trusted media and civil society to ‘red flag’ information alibis before information alibis spread. In addition to establishing an effective communications strategy, Moldova should document Russia’s's information alibis for potential accountability mechanisms in the future.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: Specifically, Moldova should examine whether the information alibi campaign is evidence of a criminal plan with a violation of the UN Charter's's prohibition on the use of force. As Moldova approaches the 28 September elections, Ukraine’s experience offers a clear lesson that narratives seeded in advance may not be empty disinformation but potential evidence of intent to carry out acts prohibited under international law. Russia’s's information alibi in Moldova appears designed to manufacture a pretext for the use of force and, in legal terms, may constitute part of the means designed to perpetrate a violation of Article 2(4) of the UN Charter's and enable the perpetration of international crimes.

title: Manufacturing a pretext from the Ukrainian playbook: How Russia's information alibis threaten Moldova's future
text: From Kyiv to Chișinău, the strategy remains clear for countering information alibis: identify the Russian playbook, rebut the information and let Moldova's future be determined by Moldova's citizens – not by manufactured pretexts to corrupt democracy or justify an act of aggression. Scott Martin, international legal advisor with Global Rights Compliance This op-ed was co-authored with Wayne Jordash KC, President of Global Rights Compliance

title: What lies behind killing of Andriy Parubiy and other prominent Ukrainians?
text: Andriy Parubiy, Speaker of the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine in 2016–2019 and a current Member of Parliament, was shot dead in Lviv yesterday. Andriy Parubiy, Speaker of the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine in 2016–2019 and a current Member of Parliament's death is not only a personal tragedy for Andriy Parubiy, Speaker of the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine in 2016–2019 and a current Member of Parliament's family and colleagues – His death is another signal in a war that has long extended beyond the frontlines. A signal addressed to all of us: Russia wages war not only with drones and missiles, Russia also wages war through fear, targeting those who shape meaning and work for Ukraine. When we look at the events, particularly since 2022, a pattern emerges: these incidents may be connected.

title: What lies behind killing of Andriy Parubiy and other prominent Ukrainians?
text: This is not a series of isolated episodes; it is a systemic strategy – one we cannot ignore. The assassination of public figure Iryna Farion in July 2024. The killing of Odesa activist Demyan Hanul in March 2025. A series of attacks on activist and volunteer Serhii Sternenko, the latest of which occurred this spring. Two thwarted assassination attempts on journalist Dmytro Gordon, prevented by Ukrainian security services. These high-profile cases differ in circumstances but share the same underlying logic. Together, These high-profile cases form a consistent chain of events, indicating the systemic nature of Russia's strategy, which may amount to official state policy.

title: What lies behind killing of Andriy Parubiy and other prominent Ukrainians?
text: The Russian Federation has effectively adapted the so-called "decapitation strategy" – traditionally used in military contexts – and applied the so-called "decapitation strategy" – traditionally used in military contexts – to civilian and political structures, orchestrating assassinations and attempted killings of politicians, activists, and public figures whom The Russian Federation considers "hostile symbols." If investigations confirm that Russian authorities are behind assassinations and attempted killings of politicians, activists, and public figures whom the Kremlin considers "hostile symbols, the legal qualification is clear – international crimes.

title: What lies behind killing of Andriy Parubiy and other prominent Ukrainians?
text: Under Article 50 of Additional Protocol I to the Geneva Conventions, political leaders, journalists, activists, and opinion-makers who are not directly participating in hostilities are considered civilians. political leaders, journalists, activists, and opinion-makers who are not directly participating in hostilities's deliberate killing during an international armed conflict constitutes a grave breach of international humanitarian law and must be investigated as a war crime. A key legal element is the connection to the ongoing international armed conflict. Under international law, the entire territory of Ukraine, not just the frontline, constitutes a conflict zone. This means that killings in Lviv or Kyiv may also be classified as war crimes if a Russian link is established.

title: What lies behind killing of Andriy Parubiy and other prominent Ukrainians?
text: However, if investigators prove the systematic nature of these attacks – demonstrating that these attacks are part of a deliberate policy to eliminate Ukraine’s political, civic, and cultural elite – the classification shifts: such acts would amount to crimes against humanity under Article 7 of the Rome Statute. shifts would fundamentally change the scale of responsibility, implicating not only individual perpetrators but the political and military leadership behind these decisions. It is critical to recognize that this strategy is not new.

title: What lies behind killing of Andriy Parubiy and other prominent Ukrainians?
text: For centuries, Russia has sought to silence Ukraine’s leading voices: the destruction of the Cossack elite and the autonomy of the Hetmanate, cultural assimilation policies, Bolshevik terror, the "Executed Renaissance" – entire generations of Ukrainian writers, artists, scholars, and politicians were killed or sent to prison camps. Propaganda, smear campaigns, disinformation, assassinations, attempted murders, and intimidation – Propaganda, smear campaigns, disinformation, assassinations, attempted murders, and intimidation are tools aimed at undermining trust, eroding resilience, and instilling fear and chaos. The international dimension of this systemic threat is reflected in ongoing legal proceedings.

title: What lies behind killing of Andriy Parubiy and other prominent Ukrainians?
text: The European Court of Human Rights is currently considering the inter-state case "Ukraine v. Russia (IX)", which focuses specifically on Russia's systemic practice of politically motivated killings. Ukraine filed the inter-state case "Ukraine v. Russia (IX)", which focuses specifically on Russia’s systemic practice of politically motivated killings in February 2021, demanding that The European Court of Human Rights recognize Russia's policy of physically eliminating individuals deemed a threat by Russia, both within Ukraine and abroad. As evidence, Ukraine submitted documentation of 24 high-profile assassinations and assassination attempts carried out between 2003 and 2020 across multiple countries.

title: What lies behind killing of Andriy Parubiy and other prominent Ukrainians?
