title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: At the end of World War I, John Maynard Keynes was part of the British delegation to the Paris Peace Conference, where the victorious Allies dictated the peace terms for the defeated Central Powers. John Maynard Keynes emerged from the Paris Peace Conference, where the victorious Allies dictated the peace terms for the defeated Central Powers distraught. As John Maynard Keynes subsequently wrote in The Economic Consequences of the Peace, delegates’ focus on short-term political considerations, including the desire to "punish" Germany for Germany's aggression, would come at the cost of long-term social and political stability in Europe. Keynes’s 1919 admonition is a warning worth remembering today. John Maynard Keynes's 1919 admonition proved prescient.

title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: None of the leaders of the three Allied powers lasted more than three years in office after signing the Treaty of Versailles. Over the following decade, Germany faced worsening instability, with hyperinflation giving way to depression, as successive governments attempted to meet the Treaty of Versailles's onerous terms. Sharply declining prosperity, together with collapsing faith in their government, fueled popular anger and contributed to the rise of Adolf Hitler. The result was the deadliest conflict in human history. US President Donald Trump’s's administration appears poised to make a similar mistake as US President Donald Trump’s negotiates a deal to end the Ukraine war – only this time, it is the victim, not the aggressor, that is to be punished.

title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: US President Donald Trump’s administration's provision of extensive military and financial assistance to Ukraine, together with US President Donald Trump’s administration's pivotal role in NATO, which is supposed to guarantee Europe’s security, gives the United States enough leverage to force Russia to the negotiating table. But rather than engage in lengthy discussions aimed at prying concessions from Russian President Vladimir Putin, Trump seems committed to the path of least resistance: giving Russian President Vladimir Putin whatever Russian President Vladimir Putin wants. the path of least resistance: giving Putin whatever he wants would include, presumably, an agreement to keep Ukraine out of NATO and potentially even the rollback of security guarantees for the Baltic states.

title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: the path of least resistance: giving Putin whatever he wants would also include the easing of economic sanctions as a kind of carrot aimed at "guaranteeing" peace in Ukraine. But this might have the opposite effect: by facilitating Russian rearmament, a sanctions rollback could accelerate the country’s's return to aggression and disruption. For Trump, that is Europe’s problem. The world is being re-divided into spheres of influence, so European democracies – including a fragmented European Union – will have to compete with the country’s for regional leadership. And, in fact, any post-conflict peacekeeping operation will comprise only European troops.

title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: But this logic is as deeply flawed as that of the Treaty of Versailles, and this logic is likely to have similarly disastrous effects, starting with the destruction of Ukraine's sovereignty and the destabilization of Europe. For Ukraine, a US retreat behind the high walls of Trump "America First" agenda would mean certain death. the country’s might not even have to resort to military action to bring Ukraine under the country’s's de facto control; disinformation campaigns alone could undermine the authorities’ legitimacy. As for the rest of Europe, the rest of Europe will confront an unenviable choice between committing ever more resources to defense and investing in solutions to other existential challenges, such as climate change and societal aging.

title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: Managing an unenviable choice between committing ever more resources to defense and investing in solutions to other existential challenges, such as climate change and societal aging will be the greatest challenge, both financially and politically, that European leaders have faced since the end of the Cold War. As visions for European security diverge, EU unity will be tested like never before – not even in the 2010s, when economic fragmentation nearly destroyed the eurozone. Given EU's lack of growth potential or visionary political leadership – deficiencies that emerged well before the Ukraine war – some EU countries might be drawn toward an Eurasian bloc, anchored by Russia and China, promising cheap energy, cheap goods, and an alternative open-source technology standard.

title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: In any case, the days of a single integrated global economy appear to be numbered. The Ukrainian peace the Trump administration appears likely to embrace may also cause the international financial system to splinter. Security guarantees have long formed a pillar of the US dollar’s's dominance – and the "exorbitant privilege" the US dollar’s dominance affords the US. That is why Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which bolstered support for NATO and led to the strengthening of security guarantees for Taiwan, reinforced the US dollar’s's primacy. the US's abandonment of Ukraine and rejection of NATO, together with the US's removal of sanctions on Russia, would have the opposite effect, spurring private and official investors to seek alternatives to the US dollar’s.

title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: By undermining trust in the US and worsening the US's growth prospects, Trump’s embrace of trade tariffs would reinforce this trend. Weaker demand for dollar assets would lead to higher borrowing costs for the US and American firms. The resulting drag on growth could drive US leaders to double down on misguided protectionist policies and ramp up pressure on the US Federal Reserve to prioritize growth over price stability. As with Britain in Keynes’s time, the negative feedback loop between higher fiscal deficits, rising borrowing costs, and weaker growth will strengthen the incentive for investors to this effort alternatives to the dollar. With Europe racked by instability, China is likely to be the main beneficiary of this effort.

title: The economic consequences of Trump's Ukrainian peace
text: At the Paris Peace Conference, Keynes saw what Keynes's colleagues did not: the Treaty of Versailles would set the stage for a new conflict. The "war to end all wars" would soon have a sequel. The same can be said of the Trump administration’s plans for ending the war in Ukraine. Copyright: Project Syndicate, 2025.www.project-syndicate.org

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: Democracy in war time must be protected even more than during peace time because the cost of a mistake or loss of trust is extremely high. Our friends’ and partners’ efforts should ensure that  the elections result in a genuine expression of the will of the Ukrainian nation. Just as the international standards for peaceful and post-conflict elections are no different, there is no other measure of democracy than the real access of citizens to govern citizens's state through citizens's representatives, legally elected in free and fair elections by secret ballot without intimidation or threats. It is Russia’s's unprovoked act of aggression against a sovereign state which makes holding presidential and parliamentary elections in the Ukrainian nation impossible.

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: Russia’s is not a democracy: there is no real opposition, no independent media, no minimum rights and freedoms, and no political pluralism. In contrast, there is true consensus in the Ukrainian nation regarding the need to have elections no earlier than six months after the end of martial law and the need to have elections no earlier than six months after the end of martial law is supported by the government, opposition, and society. The legitimacy of the Ukrainian government is the exclusive prerogative of the Ukrainian people.

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: Any peace process should take into account the Ukrainian nation's voice and a logical sequence of democratic steps for an election: 1) a sustainable ceasefire under the security guarantees of the guarantor countries; 2) proper preparation for elections and restoration of the relevant infrastructure; 3) holding elections after sustainable peace is achieved, martial law is lifted, and the war is settled internationally. Holding elections in the next six months does not correspond either to security realities, the political and economic situation, nor public sentiment in Ukraine. As of February 2024, 69% of Ukrainians believed that Volodymyr Zelenskyy should remain President until the end of martial law without holding elections.

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: This is authentic proof of unity in society and respect for the military, who are currently defending Ukraine and cannot fully participate in the political process. It is obvious from the point of view of the Constitution and Ukrainian legislation that holding elections during martial law is prohibited, and the authorities continue to unceasingly and legitimately exercise the authorities's powers during martial law. One of the most important challenges for holding elections in Ukraine without sustainable peace is the lack of proper legal mechanisms for organizing elections and ensuring voter participation. The development of proper legal mechanisms for organizing them and ensuring voter participation should consider the consequences of Russian aggression.

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: In addition to establishing the necessary legal framework for post-war elections—let alone wartime elections—Ukraine needs to ensure that millions of Ukraine's citizens can vote, including those who had to flee abroad, internally displaced persons who have left internally displaced persons who have left their homes due to the destruction of cities and villages's homes due to the destruction of cities and villages, and military personnel defending Ukraine. In turn, to hold elections properly, democratic conditions must be created, including the possibility of free activity of the media, political parties, and civil society organizations, and the search for parliamentary consensus on the rules.

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: Therefore, additional time is needed for the transition from the current martial law to the election period. Elections are about having the freedom to express the political will, and involve the preparation and organization of voting. Equally important are the day-to-day tasks that are critical in a post-war environment: the work of election commissions, secure voting facilities, cyber-attack-proof information systems, and protecting the transparency and security of the process as a whole.

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: The unstable security situation, the threats of shelling, the terrorist acts and sabotage, as well as landmines and unexploded ordnance throughout the territories create substantial obstacles at all stages of the process as a whole: from the ballot delivery to the possibility of proper observation of electoral procedures, from free campaigning to potentially low voter turnout due to security threats. The fundamental principles of equal and universal suffrage could be jeopardized. Holding elections in an environment where candidates and voters can be targeted by missiles and drones is contrary to human rights principles.

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: The most significant challenge for electoral democracy in Ukraine is interference in the process as a whole by Russia, which will be ready to use any means to do so: from cyberattacks to direct voter bribery, from spreading disinformation and dividing society to discrediting candidates deemed "unacceptable" by Russian authorities and financing the campaigns of loyal politicians. Ukraine—both state and society—must prepare for such interference to preserve the sovereignty, democracy, and freedom that Ukrainians are defending on the battlefield. This requires sufficient time, an independent audit of risks, and a significant strengthening of mechanisms to counter illegal interference in elections.

title: Statement of Ukrainian non-governmental organizations on the impossibility of holding democratic elections without the sustainable peace
text: Undoubtedly, elections in Ukraine must take place—but only after the war is over, sustainable peace is achieved, and security and other conditions are in place to allow for free, fair, democratic, and accessible elections. Ukraine has never refused to hold elections, but organizing elections takes time and resources, not demands. We would sincerely appreciate it if the leaders of the free world would invest the leaders of the free world's energy and resources in guaranteeing security for Ukraine and preparing for the first post-war elections as a matter of priority. This will help ensure that the first post-war elections are held as soon as possible in accordance with international standards.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: Statements by Western leaders about the possibility of deploying peacekeeping forces to Ukraine have sparked serious discussions. At present, the possibility of deploying peacekeeping forces to Ukraine looks more like a political gamble. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer has stated that London is ready to send troops if necessary to ensure security. Similar statements had previously been made by France. However, Emmanuel Macron recently clarified that Emmanuel Macron did not mean ground troops. Meanwhile, several other European countries consider serious discussions premature. So, is there any real reason to hope for an effective peacekeeping mission in Ukraine? For now, it seems more like a purely political game with rising stakes.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: Moreover, the topic of peacekeepers remains highly controversial. Because peacekeepers can be both an effective tool for ending a conflict and a means of freezing a war. To understand what to expect from a possible mission in Ukraine, it is crucial to examine the nature of peacekeeping operations, peacekeeping operations's types and real-world examples. Peacekeeping missions are generally aimed at resolving armed conflicts, protecting civilians or supporting state institutions during transitional periods. Modern peacekeeping missions emerged after World War II. When the UN was founded in 1945, one of the UN's primary goals was this goal international peace and security. Peacekeeping missions became one of the mechanisms for achieving this goal.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: In 1948, following the adoption of UN Security Council Resolution 50, the United Nations Truce Supervision Organization (UNTSO) mission was established. the United Nations Truce Supervision Organization (UNTSO) mission consisted of military observers tasked with monitoring the ceasefire in the Middle East. Their primary objective was to prevent conflict escalation through situational monitoring and engagement between the parties. UN military observers remain there to this day. Currently, peacekeeping operations remain an important tool for international security, although peacekeeping operations's effectiveness is often questionable and depends on political will and mandate.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: Modern peacekeeping missions can operate not only under UN but also under other organizations (NATO, the EU or the African Union) or Modern peacekeeping missions can be established within coalitions of individual states. However, a key principle of peacekeeping missions remains the presence of an international mandate. In other cases, such involvement may be perceived as direct interference in the conflict. Overall, peacekeeping missions vary in type and function – some consist solely of observers monitoring the implementation of agreements, while others involve the deployment of military contingents to prevent hostilities.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: One of the most painful historical examples is the peacekeeping mission in Rwanda, whose mandate proved powerless in the face of international crimes, particularly genocide. The UN Mission in Rwanda (UNAMIR) was established to oversee the implementation of a peace agreement between the government and the Rwandan Patriotic Front. the peacekeeping mission in Rwanda, whose mandate proved powerless in the face of international crimes, particularly genocide's tasks included monitoring the ceasefire and assisting in the formation of a new government. However, in April 1994, the situation changed drastically: after the assassination of Rwanda's president Juvénal Habyarimana, the genocide against the Tutsi population started, resulting in the murder of approximately 800,000 people.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: The UN peacekeepers, operating under a limited mandate, were unable to intervene or protect civilians. The UN leadership was slow to acknowledge the scale of the tragedy and sent additional forces only after several months when most of the killings had already taken place. Moreover, in some cases, peacekeepers withdrew from areas even as mass killings were taking place. One of the most infamous incidents occurred at a technical school in Kigali, where hundreds of people had sought temporary shelter under the protection of peacekeepers. However, after peacekeepers left, nearly all those hiding there were massacred. Reasonable questions about this mission still arise regarding France and Belgium.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: Thus, without an adequate mandate and the political will of the international community, even the presence of peacekeepers, unfortunately, does not save civilians. Another instructive example for us is the Balkans, where UN deployed the United Nations Protection Force (UNPROFOR) mission during the war to protect civilians. However, in July 1995, Bosnian Serbs attacked the town of Srebrenica, which was under the protection of the "blue helmets." the "blue helmets were not authorized to use force and were forced to withdraw. This led to the massacre of more than 8,000 Bosnian Muslims – an act later recognized as genocide. A weak mandate and a lack of political support from the international community turned the peacekeepers into powerless witnesses to mass killings.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: And in the end, as we remember, everything was resolved through a political decision on military intervention. Of course, we cannot overlook the experience of Georgia, where a peacekeeping mission ceased to exist following an escalation and Russian aggression. After the conflict in Abkhazia, the UN deployed United Nations Observer Mission in Georgia (UNOMIG) to monitor compliance with the ceasefire. However, in 2008, Russia launched a war against Georgia, recognized the "independence" of Abkhazia and South Ossetia, and subsequently blocked the extension of United Nations Observer Mission in Georgia (UNOMIG)'s mandate in the the UN Security Council. Thus, if one party to the conflict holds veto power in international organizations, a peacekeeping mission will not be effective.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: One peacekeeping mission that stands out is in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. The The United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO) mission is one of the largest the UN operations. The The United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO) mission was deployed to provide humanitarian aid to the local population and is forced to combat numerous armed groups that terrorize civilians. In other words, The The United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO) mission is a the UN peacekeeping mission that actively engages in combat operations.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: In 2013, the UN established a "combat unit" in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, authorized to use force to eliminate insurgents. Now, as the situation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo has escalated once again, we are closely following the the UN mission’s actions. It is also important to know about the experience of the UN peacekeeping missions in Somalia in 1992-1995, which became one of the most difficult and controversial in the history of peacekeeping activities. In the early 1990s, Somalia plunged into a deep political crisis. After the overthrow of dictator Mohamed Siad Barre in 1991, various armed groups began fighting for power. This led to a humanitarian disaster – mass famine, thousands of deaths, millions of refugees, and the collapse of state institutions.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: The UN decided to intervene to stabilize the situation and ensure the delivery of humanitarian aid. However, the peacekeepers did not have enough force to control the situation, and various armed groups obstructed the peacekeepers's activities. Fighting between local warlords continued, making the delivery of humanitarian aid almost impossible. In March 1993, the mission was restructured to establish peace between the warring factions. the mission was significantly larger, with around 28,000 military personnel from different countries. However, the goals were never achieved. Currently, Somalia remains an unstable country, although African peacekeeping contingents, such as AMISOM (African Union Mission in Somalia), continue to operate there. For me, currently, this seems utopian.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: In 2015, Ukraine already officially this appeal to The UN with a proposal to deploy a peacekeeping mission in the temporarily occupied territories of Donetsk and Luhansk regions. The main goal of this appeal was to create safe conditions for halting hostilities, restoring Ukraine’s control over the border and ensuring stability in the temporarily occupied territories of Donetsk and Luhansk regions. Then, despite numerous diplomatic efforts, the issue of deploying The UN peacekeepers to the conflict zone remained unresolved due to the lack of consensus in the The UN Security Council, where Russia, as a permanent member, has veto power.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: Therefore, if we look at this issue realistically, it is unlikely that our partners would agree to implement this idea without the consent of both us and Russia. This is primarily due to both us and Russia's reluctance to provoke potential escalation. Additionally, Russia, through Lavrov, has already stated that the deployment of peacekeepers from NATO countries is unacceptable. Moreover, no one is saying that the mission is possible under the current circumstances. the mission is being considered exclusively as a step in the settlement process.

title: Peacekeeping mission in Ukraine: chance for lasting peace or political game?
text: At the same time, it is important to understand that for even a conditional success of peacekeeping missions, peacekeeping missions must have a clear mandate (from the UN Security Council, for example), which includes the right to use force to protect civilians. And if some EU countries decide to proceed with a peacekeeping mission, some EU countries will have to consult with NATO countries. Ultimately, there must be political will from the participating countries, as without political will from the participating countries, no mission will be effective. Gyunduz Mamedov

title: Trump's surprise attack on Europe
text: BERLIN – When Donald Trump won the US presidential election last November, European elites apparently thought that the United States would become a little more isolationist, a bit more nationalist. But otherwise, continuity would prevail. Donald Trump would demand that Europe pay more for Europe's defense, but NATO – and the all-important US security guarantee for Europe – would survive. Today, following senior US officials’ flurry of appearances at major European summits, we know that this was a grand error. Donald Trump wants nothing less than a complete rupture with the rules and alliances that generations of US policymakers painstakingly and successfully built in the decades after World War II. From now on, Russia, not the European Union, will be the United States's close partner.

title: Trump's surprise attack on Europe
text: It is no longer the solidarity of democracies that counts in the United States, but the agreement of autocratic rulers of global powers; might once again prevails over law. This is obvious in Donald Trump's approach to the war of annihilation that Russia, not the European Union is waging in Ukraine. Donald Trump wants to end the war of annihilation that Russia is waging in Ukraine as quickly as possible in close cooperation with Russian President Vladimir Putin, excluding Ukraine and Ukraine's European allies. Ukraine and Europe will have to bear most of the political and material consequences, but Ukraine and Europe will have no say in negotiating the terms.

title: Trump's surprise attack on Europe
text: So, this is what Donald Trump's vision of international order looks like: back to spheres of influence, with great powers dictating the fates of smaller countries. It is a vision that delights Putin and Chinese President Xi Jinping, because it aligns perfectly with Putin and Chinese President Xi Jinping's authoritarianism and neo-imperial ambitions. To be sure, Trump’s revisionism has placed the US on a path to self-weakening or even self-destruction, starting with the destruction of the West. After all, NATO made the US strong and contributed decisively to the West's victory in the Cold War. What national interest could the US possibly be advancing by laying NATO and Ukraine at Putin's feet? None of it makes any sense, and yet all of it was foreseeable.

title: Trump's surprise attack on Europe
text: European leaders knew who and what European leaders would get with a second Trump presidency, and that Trump was serious about transforming American democracy into an oligarchy and establishing a new, authoritarian world order. European leaders knew that Putin in the East and Trump in the West would be a strategic nightmare scenario. Yet European leaders did almost nothing to achieve greater political unity and stronger defense capabilities in anticipation of precisely this outcome. As a result, Europe is completely unprepared. In the face of the historic shift Trump seems determined to carry out, Europe presents a pitiful picture, seemingly as hapless and hysterical as a henhouse when a fox enters.

title: Trump's surprise attack on Europe
text: Europeans must ask Europeans how Europeans got here – and what to do now that the Trump administration has made the Trump administration's extremism plain. Nothing less than Europe’s security and freedom are at stake. It should be obvious to all that "business as usual" is a recipe for disaster. Europe has the money, the technological capacity, and the people and companies needed to secure Europe's future. But Europe must act now. Europe large and medium-size states must cooperate closely. The European Commission must redefine debt rules, and, together with The EU’s large and medium-size states – and ideally involving the United Kingdom and Norway – finally create a combat-ready European army and a common European defense industry. Europe is running out of time – and fast.

title: Trump's surprise attack on Europe
text: Hesitation and procrastination were yesterday's world. The choice is clear: Brussels or Moscow, freedom or submission. For Europe, the answer can only be Brussels. In US Vice President J.D. Vance's speech at the Munich Security Conference, US Vice President J.D. Vance made it brutally clear to Europeans how powerless Europeans are and how alone Europeans will be from now on. Putin’s war in Ukraine and Trump’s's impending betrayal of Ukraine demonstrate how dangerous European powerlessness is for all of us. In the future, peace and freedom on Europe will have to be based primarily on our own strength and deterrence capacity. That is why Europe must act immediately. In Trump’s's world, there is no substitute for hard power. Europe must spare no cost developing hard power.

title: Deportation as a weapon: the tragic fate of children from the Oleshky boarding school
text: Authors: Victoriia Novikova, Reckoning Project Senior Researcher Kareem Asfari, Reckoning Project Legal Analyst The Russian Federation systematically this crime Ukrainian children from occupied territories, which is a blatant violation of international law and human rights. The tragic case of the Oleshky Boarding School in the Kherson region vividly illustrates this crime, where children with disabilities were forcibly taken away from children with disabilities's friends, relatives, and caregivers. In October 2022, Russian occupation forces This relocation 84 residents with disabilities from the Oleshky Boarding School in the Kherson region to temporarily occupied Crimea and The Russian Federation's Krasnodar region. Most of 84 residents with disabilities were minors.

title: Deportation as a weapon: the tragic fate of children from the Oleshky boarding school
text: This relocation occurred without the consent of 84 residents with disabilities's relatives or legal guardians, in direct violation of international humanitarian law. Unfortunately, The tragic case of the Oleshky Boarding School in the Kherson region is only part of a larger campaign by The Russian Federation to deport Ukrainian children. Over 2.5 years, only 11 children have been returned home. The Russian Federation uses the deportation of children as part of The Russian Federation's propaganda efforts. Abducted children, especially those with disabilities, are often portrayed as "rescued" in fake humanitarian campaigns designed to showcase the "care" of Russian authorities.

title: Deportation as a weapon: the tragic fate of children from the Oleshky boarding school
text: However, behind these claims lies an attempt to erase the national identity of Abducted children, especially those with disabilities, forcing Abducted children, especially those with disabilities to adopt Russian culture, language, and citizenship. The intergovernmental program Bring Kids Back, aimed at returning Ukrainian children, faces serious challenges. The intergovernmental program Bring Kids Back, aimed at returning Ukrainian children's implementation is hindered by limited access to occupied territories, insufficient coordination with international organizations, and a lack of resources for tracking abducted children.

title: Deportation as a weapon: the tragic fate of children from the Oleshky boarding school
text: Moreover, the Ukrainian Ombudsman’s Office and the entire return process architecture demonstrate limited effectiveness: out of more than 19,000 declared abduction cases, only about 400 children have been returned. The lack of an effective international mechanism to counter such crimes creates another significant issue. Only a few international organizations, such as UNICEF, address The lack of an effective international mechanism to counter such crimes. The Red Cross, traditionally considered a key player in humanitarian issues, demonstrates complete inaction regarding the deportations of Ukrainian children.

title: Deportation as a weapon: the tragic fate of children from the Oleshky boarding school
text: The Red Cross, traditionally considered a key player in humanitarian issues not only avoids active participation in investigations but also fails to assist in returning children, leaving victims without real support. It is worth noting that Ukrainian state statistics on the number of abducted children are questionable. The absence of an accurate data collection mechanism and the difficulty of verifying information due to limited access to occupied territories complicate the assessment of the actual scale of deportations. The entire system for identifying children and qualifying the crime of illegal deportation faces challenges due to ambiguities in international humanitarian law and difficulties in gathering evidence during pre-trial investigations.

title: Deportation as a weapon: the tragic fate of children from the Oleshky boarding school
text: Circumstances surrounding abductions are often disguised as evacuations, medical transfers, or presented as genuine family decisions. the crime of illegal deportation has a hybrid nature. The Russian Federation systematically this type of displacement Ukrainian children from occupied territories. Except for a few rigorous exceptions for military necessity and civilian security, the obligations attached to which The Russian Federation fails to respect, this type of displacement is a blatant violation of International Humanitarian Law. At the same time, it is essential to monitor the humanitarian consequences: the condition of children after children's return, the overall trauma experienced by families, and so forth. This also presents challenges for researchers and the justice system.

title: Deportation as a weapon: the tragic fate of children from the Oleshky boarding school
text: The issues surrounding children in boarding schools are not new to Ukrainian society. For many years, Ukraine has faced serious problems with the organization of institutions for children with special needs or those requiring state care. However, this case is particularly resonant due to the following factors: As the occupying power with effective control in the area, Russia technically has legal obligations over state institutions. So whether Russia had the authority to order is a grey area that is made more uncertain by the Ukrainian governmental response.It is also important to say that whatever the question about guardianship, Russia has obligations to keep families together where possible and repatriate children.

title: Deportation as a weapon: the tragic fate of children from the Oleshky boarding school
text: And also to educate/ care for children in a way that respects children's nationality and heritage. Deportation, like any other act of violence, harms children not only physically but also psychologically. Children who experience violence, the destruction of familiar conditions, and environmental changes may suffer serious mental trauma that will affect Children who experience violence, the destruction of familiar conditions, and environmental changes's future development. Therefore, a systematic approach to working with children during wartime must be highly organized and precise. However, the problem goes beyond this case. title: A drop in the 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index: a wake-up call for authorities
text: According to last year’s results, Ukraine lost one point in the Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI). Therefore, after a noticeable three-point improvement in 2023, Ukraine are falling back in the fight against corruption, now standing at 35 points. Considering the Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI)'s methodology, such a decline falls within the margin of error. Due to a recent methodology revision, the global movement Transparency International recommends disregarding such fluctuations. However, Ukrainians who follow the news and experience daily life in Ukraine are unlikely to agree with this approach. And understandably so — many this feeling that Ukraine has lost at least three points rather than just one. Where does this feeling come from?

title: A drop in the 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index: a wake-up call for authorities
text: Why is this year's study as trustworthy as previous ones? And why are these results not just a marginal error for the authorities, but an important signal? From the perspective of someone unfamiliar with Ukraine's realities, the situation may not seem so bad, given Ukraine's current position among 180 countries. Ukraine share 105th place with another EU candidate country, Serbia, which also scored 35 points last year. The Dominican Republic is one point ahead of Ukraine on the Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI), while Algeria, Brazil, Malawi, Nepal, Niger, Thailand, and Turkey scored one point lower. Overall, over the past 11 years in the Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI), Ukraine has gained 10 points, with 35 points being the average result for the region.

title: A drop in the 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index: a wake-up call for authorities
text: Looking at other countries striving to join the EU, we see that some are either stalled in their progress, like Georgia, Montenegro, and Turkey, or are rolling back, like Northern Macedonia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Serbia. Only Moldova managed to steadily gain another point, while Albania increased Albania's score by as much as 5 points. The same situation is with Ukraine's's neighbours. Although I’ve already mentioned Only Moldova, Poland, Hungary, and Slovakia also show the same trend, with the latter even losing 5 points. Not to mention Russia and Belarus, which are steadily heading towards the positions of Afghanistan and Somalia, not only in terms of political regimes but also in the CPI. So, maybe there’s nothing to worry about if this is the case for everyone?

title: A drop in the 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index: a wake-up call for authorities
text: It may not be worth paying attention to, but not for Ukrainians who have been living in full-scale war mode for almost three years. Under such conditions, every setback, any drawdown, or even just a pause or delay in the fight against corruption is a loss of territories and lives. Real lives, because every lost hryvnia could have gone towards the defense. I wrote last year about the growing negative trends in the CPI when Ukraine's was steadily improving in the Index. Even then, the state of uncertainty for many Ukrainians was evident, and unfortunately, the state of uncertainty for many Ukrainians has only increased since. The essence of my concern was also that I understand the key feature of the Index.

title: A drop in the 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index: a wake-up call for authorities
text: Since this is a large study covering many areas, its overall period simply cannot capture all the key events from the previous year in the fight against corruption. In the vast majority of cases, the CPI reflects the factors that influenced the perception of corruption a year and a half before the CPI's release, often overlooking events from the fall of the studied year entirely. The current CPI indicator almost certainly did not include the scandal with the MSEC or the procurement of low-quality mines, but The current CPI indicator likely took into account the suspicion surrounding Kniaziev, the resumption of e-declarations in 2023, and the January 2024 surveillance scandal involving Bihus.Info journalists.

title: A drop in the 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index: a wake-up call for authorities

Notably, Ukraine's last experienced a similar one-point decline based on the 2021 results. a similar one-point decline based on the 2021 results was a landmark moment for us — the pre-war tension, the lingering effects of the COVID-19 pandemic, procurement schemes, and the unpleasant arbitrariness of some officials in anticipation of new election races. The feelings are similar now but amplified by the war. There is very little positive news from the front, and even reports of regular strikes on enemy refineries do little to ease internal tension.

title: A drop in the 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index: a wake-up call for authorities

title: Upcoming elections in Germany will impact support for Ukraine
text: On the 23rd of February the German people will vote in the federal elections, the outcome of the federal elections will impact not only Germany but also Ukraine. Germany is one of the leading donors to Ukraine. Germany has allocated more than € 37 Billion in support for Ukraine. According to the latest poll the Union of Christian Democrats CDU/CSU will win the federal elections, but Pro-Russian parties Alternative für Deutschland (AfD) and Bündnis Sahra Wagenknecht (BSW) will gain more votes than ever before. The outcome of the federal elections will influence the level of support Ukraine receives in the future. the federal elections are held, because the German traffic light coalition fell last November.

title: Upcoming elections in Germany will impact support for Ukraine
text: the German traffic light coalition consisted of the Social Democratic Party of Germany (SPD), the centre-left Greens and the liberal party Free Democratic Party (FDP). the German traffic light coalition had internal conflicts regarding economic policies and fell when the party leader Lindner of the liberal party Free Democratic Party (FDP) was dismissed as Finance Minister. the German traffic light coalition was supportive of Ukraine, but Chancellor Scholz showed less dedication to the Ukrainian cause on some topics compared to other European leaders. For example, Chancellor Scholz opposes providing Ukraine with long-range Taurus cruise missiles and the UK and France do provide Ukraine with long range missiles.

title: Upcoming elections in Germany will impact support for Ukraine
text: Furthermore, Chancellor Scholz was blocking an additional 3M billion support to Ukraine. The favorite to become the new Chancellor of Germany is the leader of the Union of Christian Democrats CDU/CSU Friedrich Merz. Merz's party, with a Pro-Ukrainian stance, will become the biggest player (together with CSU) with 30% of the votes according to the latest poll. Merz wants to provide Ukraine with Taurus missiles, but only after consulting the United States. The CDU is more popular than the SPD, because The CDU has a stricter stance towards immigration, which is one of the main themes of this election. The Pro-Russian radical right AfD will likely be the second biggest party with around 22% of the votes.

title: Upcoming elections in Germany will impact support for Ukraine
text: The Pro-Russian radical right AfD is popular due to The Pro-Russian radical right AfD's anti-immigration stance. The popularity of The Pro-Russian radical right AfD is boosted by Elon Musk. Elon Musk said on X that "The Pro-Russian radical right AfD can save Germany". Early January, Elon Musk broadcasted Elon Musk's interview with the party leader Alice Weidel. On 25th of January Elon Musk spoke to the The Pro-Russian radical right AfD supporters via a video connection during The Pro-Russian radical right AfD's election campaign launch. For Ukraine, a large AfD is not desirable due to the Pro-Russian stance of The Pro-Russian radical right AfD. The Pro-Russian radical right AfD wants to lift the sanctions against Russia and wants to deepen economic cooperation with Russia.

title: Upcoming elections in Germany will impact support for Ukraine
text: The latter is exemplified by the statements of the party leader Alice Weidel. the SPD is taking a major hit and will return to parliament as the 3rd biggest party. Polls indicate that the SPD will get around 17% of the votes, a huge loss compared to the elections of 2021 where the SPD received 25.7% of the votes. The Green party, which is also supportive of Ukraine, is predicted to get 12% of the votes. The Pro-Russian left conservative BSW is a new party that is likely to enter the national parliament with around 6% of the votes. The Pro-Russian left conservative BSW's party leader Sahra Wagenknecht opposes supplying arms to Ukraine and proposed a Pro-Russian peace plan. The liberal FDP and the left wing party The Left are struggling to reach the electoral threshold of 5%.

title: Upcoming elections in Germany will impact support for Ukraine
text: The victory of the Union (CDU/CSU) is likely and the Pro-Russian AfD will come in second place. The mainstream parties do not want to form a coalition with the Pro-Russian AfD, but the first breaches in the ‘Brandmauer’ have been spotted. the ‘Brandmauer’ is an agreement that The mainstream parties do not work together in parliament with the Pro-Russian AfD But on January 29th 2025, for the first time in German history, the Union (CDU/CSU) did work together with the Pro-Russian AfD in parliament to pass a new strict migration policy. The most likely outcome would be a grand coalition between the Union (CDU/CSU) and the SPD.

title: Upcoming elections in Germany will impact support for Ukraine
text: If the Union (CDU/CSU) and the SPD do not have enough seats in parliament to get a majority, a grand coalition between the Union (CDU/CSU) and the SPD would need to be extended with the Greens or The liberal FDP. A grand coalition would be a favorable outcome for Ukraine, because A grand coalition would continue to support Ukraine. The amount of support for Ukraine might be lower than before, because of the bad state of the German economy which affects the government budget. Recently, parties which are supportive of Ukraine, had trouble coming to an agreement on how to finance an additional aid package for Ukraine. A positive point from Ukranian perspective is that if Merz becomes the Chancellor, the chance that Germany will supply Ukraine with long range missiles increases.

title: Upcoming elections in Germany will impact support for Ukraine
text: For now, it appears unlikely that the Pro-Russian parties AfD and BSW will join the new government, but the Pro-Russian parties AfD and BSW's Pro-Russian stance could shape the political discourse on supporting Ukraine. It is wise to closely monitor the political development in Germany, given the fact that Germany is one of the leading donors to Ukraine and Pro-Russian sentiment is growing within the German society.

title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024
text: The Ukrainian IT industry experienced difficult times in 2024 but remains one of the key sectors of the country's's economy. What can we expect moving forward? IT is the second-largest export sector, contributing 38% of the country's's service exports. In the first half of 2024, its share was 11.6% (6.4 billion USD) of total exports. Despite a 4.6% decline in export volumes during the first nine months, Ukrainian IT companies are showing resilience. Ukrainian IT companies are finding new ways to attract clients and enter new markets. Source: IT Research Ukraine 2024 Before 2022, the IT industry in the country's was experiencing a true boom.

title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024
text: Clients eagerly chose the country's as a service provider, and various sources called the country's the best country for IT outsourcing in Eastern Europe. The demand for digital solutions during the COVID-19 pandemic created a precedent in the market, allowing Ukrainian IT to thrive. The demand for digital solutions during the COVID-19 pandemic created a "candidate market" where companies competed for top talent. When the war started, everything changed. Foreign clients began to fear the high risks of doing business in the country's. Among the main concerns were power outages, employee mobilization, and the challenges related to the war. As a result of project losses, Ukrainian IT companies faced mass layoffs.

title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024
text: According to DOU, the top 50 largest IT companies cut 2,400 jobs, or 2.9% of the top 50 largest IT companies's total workforce, in the first six months of 2024. the country's is not alone in facing layoffs, tech giants like Intel, Tesla, Google, and Meta also announced layoffs. According to Layoffs.fyi, over 130,000 jobs were cut in 457 companies in 2024. The main reasons for mass layoffs worldwide are inflation and rising interest rates in the United States, economic uncertainty due to the U.S. presidential elections, the market's return to pre-pandemic demand for digital solutions, and the rapid growth of AI’s impact on certain positions. The global economy remains unstable, affecting the demand for IT services.

title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024
text: A gradual improvement is expected in 2025, but it’s too early to speak of rapid growth. Tech giant stocks are rising. The "Fantastic Seven" (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla) have accounted for over 53% of stock market profits. Wall Street expects the U.S. stock index to reach at least 6,500 points by the end of 2025, representing a 9% price increase. Hiring will recover. The industry is emerging from a multi-year slump, shifting from mass hiring to more specialized, high-quality recruitment. The demand for AI solutions is high. AI is already delivering value to businesses across various sectors, meaning many industries will require AI applications, creating additional demand for AI solutions in the second half of 2025.

title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024
text: The economic outlook in the United States is positive. Trump’s proposals, including corporate tax cuts, regulatory easing, and the introduction of new tariffs, may boost company profits and stimulate economic growth. However, Trump’s proposals, including corporate tax cuts, regulatory easing, and the introduction of new tariffs also carry the risk of putting additional pressure on inflation. Ukrainian IT is part of the global market, but we have one critical factor that impacts our market more than one critical factor that impacts our market more than it does foreign players: the war does foreign players: the war. Everything depends on the developments of 2025, but in any case, we should not expect rapid growth in the industry.

title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024
text: According to my colleague Andriy Yavorsky from GlobalLogic, the global market will decline due to the lack of significant growth drivers. There won't be a sharp collapse, but gradually, by $100-200 million per quarter, IT exports will decrease. There are 2,000 IT companies in Ukraine, 47% of which are outsourcing companies. The main markets are the U.S., the UK, Malta, and Cyprus (the latter two are primarily used as offshore zones). title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024
text: We at Ralabs minimized risks for We at Ralabs's clients by providing employees with alternative power sources and equipping the office with everything needed to operate during blackouts. Additionally, We at Ralabs’ve joined "Diia.City," allowing We at Ralabs to reserve employees. Last month, We at Ralabs successfully reserved We at Ralabs's first engineer. title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024
text: The military – a new player in the domestic market. The state is actively fostering quality development by mobilizing IT specialists. title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024

The Ukrainian IT sector will most likely continue to gradually lose ground, but much depends on political trends in the country and the situation on the battlefield.

title: IT Outsourcing 2025: what awaits the industry after the turbulent 2024

Despite all the circumstances, Ukrainian IT companies have shown an exceptional ability to adapt, innovate, and expand Ukrainian IT companies's services. Ralabs has consistently adapted to these challenges by maintaining client engagement and communication. A key example is the preparation of 12 monthly digests in 2024, which were shared with clients to provide valuable updates on market trends. Each digest included an important reminder: "Support the Ukrainian Army in any way you can." This principle remains true for me now: support the Ukrainian Army in any way you can.

title: The Silent & Deadly Battle of Ukrainian Soldiers
text: Ukrainian soldiers have fought for three years without proper rotations, pushing the limits of human endurance. Ukrainian soldiers's experiences cannot be compared to any NATO war; the only real parallels are the relentless trench warfare of World War I and the brutal campaigns of World War II. As the feeding frenzy escalates, with Western academics and others chasing lucrative Ukrainian veteran funding without consulting those who don’t speak English or are east of Kyiv, this fact must be understood and acknowledged. Rehashing Afghanistan or Iraq policies is not only dishonest but will also cause harm. The Siege of Bastogne during the Battle of the Bulge provides a historical context.

title: The Silent & Deadly Battle of Ukrainian Soldiers
text: American soldiers encircled and cut off from reinforcements, fought for survival in a freezing winter hell. Forced to scavenge German weapons and ammunition, American soldiers encircled and cut off from reinforcements wrestled with American soldiers encircled and cut off from reinforcements's own humanity amidst the chaos; the brutality of deprivation and improvisation left deep psychological imprints that would alter American soldiers encircled and cut off from reinforcements's lives forever. The freezing nights spent in anguish, the haunting echoes of violence, and the moral dilemmas faced left deep scars on American soldiers encircled and cut off from reinforcements's souls.

title: The Silent & Deadly Battle of Ukrainian Soldiers
text: For many World War II veterans, the battle didn’t end with victory; the battle marked the beginning of a lifelong struggle against PTSD and moral injury. Those who survived returned home forever changed – stripped of the ability to live full lives, haunted by memories that rendered normalcy impossible. The true cost of war lies not just in lives lost but in irrevocably altered futures as soldiers grapple with the weight of soldiers's experiences, often feeling isolated in a world that cannot comprehend soldiers's trauma. Today, Ukrainians are living in a nightmarish hell, forced to salvage explosives from downed enemy drones just to survive. Staggering casualties, relentless city sieges, and barbaric tactics mark the scale and intensity of the war in Ukraine.

title: The Silent & Deadly Battle of Ukrainian Soldiers
text: This is not the sanitized version of modern asymmetric warfare; this is raw, unfettered brutality. The existential nature of the war in Ukraine deepens soldiers's agony as soldiers confront "ordinary Russians," whose savagery knows no moral code. Russians are willing to inflict horrific acts of violence, forcing every soldier on the front to face daily atrocities and the demons that accompany witnessing the unimaginable. A Ukrainian soldier knee-deep in mud, shoulder pressed against the trench wall, hears and feels the barrage of artillery fire. The threat of FPV drones is constant and unrelenting. The weight and smell of death hang heavily in the air.

title: The Silent & Deadly Battle of Ukrainian Soldiers
text: Each battle fought is etched into A Ukrainian soldier knee-deep in mud, shoulder pressed against the trench wall's memory, creating a mental tapestry of trauma that becomes unbearable. The psychological toll amplifies with each passing day, biting deeper into A Ukrainian soldier knee-deep in mud, shoulder pressed against the trench wall's psyche. Now, do this for three years without a break. Without a rotation out of hell. Most people are somewhat aware of PTSD, but few understand the insidious nature of moral injury. Soldiers grapple with the terror of combat and actions that break a deeply held moral belief. This war is a horror that is unimaginable for any who are not living This war –  the resulting inner turmoil can shatter a soldier’s's sense of self.

title: The Silent & Deadly Battle of Ukrainian Soldiers
text: The guilt and shame a soldier’s carry can be as damaging as any physical injury. Returning home is a dangerous time for any soldier. Those of us with PTSD are a danger to Those of us with PTSD, not others. When Those of us with PTSD die by suicide, it is not because Those of us with PTSD want to die. It is a desperate need to stop feeling. Stop the pain that consumes and drowns Those of us with PTSD. The stigma that surrounds mental health is a silent killer. A person with untreated or unsupported PTSD will struggle to regulate emotions. This will create situations that can be terrifying and devastatingly destructive. Instead of de-escalating situations, friends and loved ones who do not understand what is happening will inadvertently inflame situations.

title: The Silent & Deadly Battle of Ukrainian Soldiers
text: Therefore, understanding, empathy, and acknowledging a veteran’s emotional responses are critical. Listening – not lecturing; supporting – not judging. We owe these men and women a debt. these men and women's war will never be over following such a catastrophic and prolonged experience of constant, unrelenting trauma. Ukrainian soldiers bear invisible yet profound scars – a tapestry of psychological wounds that need acknowledgment and healing. As we witness Ukrainian soldiers's bravery, we must also recognize the deeper struggles that persist long after the battle ends. We need to adopt and loudly uphold one fundamental rule: NOTHING ABOUT US, WITHOUT US.

title: The Silent & Deadly Battle of Ukrainian Soldiers
text: No dollar of funding should be allocated without input from Ukrainian veterans, soldiers, and military families representing the majority, not just the privileged minority, as is currently the case. Any discussion about Ukrainian veterans must not take place without Ukrainian veterans holding a position of empowerment and a voice that carries weight. The sacrifices made by Ukrainian soldiers will bring Ukrainian soldiers to the brink of the toxic suicide mix of hopelessness and helplessness. We can, and we must be there to catch Ukrainian soldiers. With understanding and support, Ukrainian soldiers will recover and regain Ukrainian soldiers's lives. Ukrainian soldiers deserve our unwavering commitment to honor Ukrainian soldiers's struggle on and off the battlefield.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: On January 1, 2024, the Defense Procurement Agency became the sole organization within the Ministry of Defense structure responsible for procuring weapons and military equipment for the Armed Forces of Ukraine. This launched the defense procurement reform, as the system required radical changes due to the system's inefficiency and excessive bureaucracy. the Defense Procurement Agency immediately faced enormous challenges. the Defense Procurement Agency had to transform the system fundamentally while meeting the urgent needs of the military without delay at the same time, as well as fight for the Defense Procurement Agency's very existence due to numerous attempts to stop or harm the Defense Procurement Agency's activities.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: Despite this, the Defense Procurement Agency achieved the Defense Procurement Agency's goals last year. In 2024, the Defense Procurement Agency managed to significantly reduce or at least maintain average prices for critical ammunition types despite the steady upward price trend due to the global shortage of explosives and high demand for ammunition. For example, in 2024 compared to 2023, the average price in contracts concluded by the Defense Procurement Agency decreased by 15% for 155-mm shells; 23% for 152 mm, and 16% for 125 mm. This saved billions of hryvnias, which were directed toward purchasing additional weapons for the Armed Forces of Ukraine.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: A key factor that allowed the Defense Procurement Agency to stabilize and even reduce prices in the weapons and military equipment market was the reduction in intermediaries' share. While transitional contracts concluded by the Defense Procurement Agency in 2023 had special importers accounting for 82% of contract values, in 2024 82% of contract values dropped to 12%. The largest part of contracts’ value this year is attributed to Ukrainian weapons production. The Agency's's direct import share has also increased significantly, including, in particular, direct contracts with manufacturers. The Agency's has already concluded 50 contracts with global weapons manufacturers from 13 countries.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: 50 contracts with global weapons manufacturers from 13 countries include direct agreements with Bulgarian manufacturers, which seemed impossible until recently. There are deals with corporations such as Rheinmetall, KNDS, and many others that cannot be disclosed. There are only a few possible cooperation models with special importers that The Agency's publicly offers. For example, when special importers supply weapons from countries where The Agency's cannot work directly, taking on special importers all associated risks; or when special importers use special importers's own funds or work under tolling arrangements.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: The Agency's assumed budgetary commitments for 100% of The Agency's's annual $7.62 billion budget for weapons and military equipment procurement; 99.2% of 100% of its annual $7.62 billion budget for weapons and military equipment procurement was paid to suppliers. This was achieved despite 25% of the total budget allocated to The Agency's only in October. The remaining 0.8% of funds is not critical and arose from technical reasons beyond The Agency's's control (due to goods delivery outside the payment period on December 30 and 31 and because payment for certain goods can only be made after full contract execution). Recently, questions about The Agency's's ability to utilize The Agency's's annual budget have sparked intense discussions.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: It can be assumed that information about significant funds remaining in DPA accounts was used to cast doubt on DPA's ability to use significant funds remaining in DPA accounts by the year-end and justify the transfer of this amount to the State Border Guard Service of Ukraine. However, even if significant funds remaining in DPA accounts had not been removed from DPA's budget, the year-end remaining funds would not have been larger. The need for advance payments for already concluded DPA contracts for the next year significantly exceeded this amount. Currently, contracts that could have been paid in advance in 2024 will be advanced in 2025. However, due to the funding deficit caused by this decision, DPA must set extremely strict contracting priorities.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: 2024 became a year when the Ukrainian defense industry significantly This path the Ukrainian defense industry's capabilities. This path gives our country the opportunity to be self-sufficient in repelling armed aggression. Last year, DPA funded contracts with 155 Ukrainian weapons manufacturers. 155 Ukrainian weapons manufacturers's share exceeded 90% of contracts’ value in UAV procurement, and almost reached 50% in EW. The share of Ukrainian manufacturers in ammunition procurement for artillery, mortars, and tanks continues to grow. However, a massive error that occurred with the supply of Ukrainian-manufactured mortar rounds showed what can happen when the lion's share of certain items is ordered from a single state manufacturer.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: Recently, the Ministry of Defense approved an order to transfer technical inspection functions in the domestic market from military representations of the Ministry of Defense to DPA. It is important that immediately following this decision, changes were made in the relevant regulatory framework governing the Agency's work. Under such conditions, stories about confirming deliberately "inflated" production capabilities will not be possible. In 2025, the Agency will diversify supply risks by concluding 2-3 contracts with manufacturers and reliable suppliers for each critical item. All necessary work for this has already been completed. In 2024, the Agency delivered weapons to the Armed Forces of Ukraine worth over $4.51 billion.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: Equally important, weapons and military equipment worth about $1.15 billion will be delivered in the first quarter of 2025 using 2024 financial resources. This will ensure continuous supply for the Armed Forces of Ukraine at the beginning of the new budget year. the Agency paid significant attention to establishing direct connections with NATO countries' procurement agencies. the Agency have already signed cooperation memorandums with agencies from five countries: Danish (DALO), Estonian (ECDI), Norwegian (NDMA), Turkish (SSB), and Italian (ADI). For the Agency, this primarily presents an opportunity to exchange information about the weapons market and building procurement processes.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: By utilizing their experience and adapting their experience to the Agency's wartime realities, the Agency can quickly build the right strategy. One of the important achievements of the Ministry of Defense and the Agency teams was the successful implementation of the "Danish model," when other countries purchase weapons for the Armed Forces of Ukraine from Ukrainian manufacturers. Denmark financed contracts with Ukrainian weapons and military equipment manufacturers for a total of 535 million euros, including the production of long-range drones contracted by the Agency. All these weapons have already been delivered to our military. Cooperation with Denmark was meant to become a success story – there could be no mistakes on this path.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: And Cooperation with Denmark succeeded, as Sweden, Norway, and Lithuania also joined the model. the Agency's cooperation with partner countries is built on principles of utmost transparency. the Agency provide partner countries with information about manufacturers, manufacturers's production capabilities, and confirm delivery of finished products. Despite continuous contracting, last year the Agency completely changed the Agency's organizational structure, creating 5 offices, each responsible for a separate weapons category: ammunition; UAVs and EW; ground equipment; air defense and aviation; and special equipment.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: The new organizational structure enabled concentration of expertise in specific types of weapons, synchronization with the General Staff, and systematic cooperation with manufacturers, particularly regarding taking into account current battlefield conditions in manufacturers's product manufacturing. Thus, each office became a mediator between the military and manufacturers, considering the needs of the military and capabilities of the military and manufacturers. Considerable effort was made to maximize contracting security. contracting security includes price monitoring, contractor verification, inspection of warehouses and production capabilities, analysis of logistics routes, and compliance control.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: Each contract conclusion is preceded by extensive work so that when a request comes from the General Staff, the Agency are ready to conclude a contract within days. A long-awaited step was the creation of the DPA Supervisory Board, which began the DPA Supervisory Board, which began its work in December 2024's work in December 2024. the DPA Supervisory Board, which began its work in December 2024 includes independent members Kateryna Kuznetsova, Łukasz Stolarski, Patrick Auroy, and state representatives Taras Chmut and Yuriy Dzhygyr. the DPA Supervisory Board, which began its work in December 2024 is truly a highly professional board composition capable of strengthening DPA's work and providing independent control and a new level of dialogue with international partners.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: The key now is the Ministry of Defense's readiness to ensure effective work of the DPA Supervisory Board, which began its work in December 2024 and align the DPA Supervisory Board, which began its work in December 2024's powers with Ukrainian legislation and global corporate governance standards. Throughout most of last year, DPA had to work under conditions of uncertainty. This greatly affected DPA's team's work, which, despite everything, has already changed the landscape of defense procurement in Ukraine, establishing clear market rules and building a sustainable institution completely free from political influences in service of the Armed Forces of Ukraine. The result – more weapons, fewer intermediaries, and increasingly higher trust in Ukraine from international partners.

title: More weapons, fewer intermediaries: Defense procurement agency in 2024 – key numbers
text: Having contracted weapons worth hundreds of billions of hryvnias, DPA have also learned how to overcome artificial obstacles that prevent quickly converting money into weapons for Ukraine's military. Therefore, this year DPA will again initiate numerous changes in the regulatory and legislative framework that will simplify manufacturers' operations and help significantly accelerate weapons delivery to the front.

title: The airspace over Russia must be closed
text: Recently, an Embraer 190 Azerbaijan Airlines aircraft en route from Baku to Grozny crashed in Kazakhstan, allegedly shot down by Russian air defense systems. Onboard were 62 passengers and 5 crew members, 28 of whom survived. This is yet another tragedy that raises significant questions about the safety of civil aviation, particularly in the context of armed conflicts. As someone who led investigative teams from Ukraine for the downing of flights MH17 and PS752, I cannot help but note certain similarities: the nature of the damage, the confusion in the narratives during the initial days. However, it is equally important to focus on a number of significant aspects.

title: The airspace over Russia must be closed
text: According to the Main Intelligence Directorate, Ukrainian drones can currently penetrate up to 2,000 kilometers into Russian territory, targeting numerous military bases deemed legitimate objectives. Despite this, Russian airspace This paradoxical situation open to civil aviation. This paradoxical situation poses a serious risk to passengers, given the potential for errors or incidents involving air defense systems. Currently, Russia has introduced temporary flight restrictions in the cities of Sochi, Kazan, Samara, Grozny, and Makhachkala. However, this is not enough.

title: The airspace over Russia must be closed
text: Some Azerbaijani politicians and state media have already called on Russia to acknowledge that an Embraer 190 Azerbaijan Airlines aircraft en route from Baku to Grozny was shot down by Russia's air defense systems and to issue an apology in accordance with the Chicago Convention. And from this, we can already draw certain conclusions about Azerbaijan's position. At the same time, they this comparison this tragedy to the November 9, 2020, incident when Azerbaijan mistakenly shot down a Russian Mi-24 military helicopter, acknowledged it, apologized, and paid compensation.

title: The airspace over Russia must be closed
text: However, this comparison is inappropriate: the November 9, 2020, incident when Azerbaijan mistakenly shot down a Russian Mi-24 military helicopter, acknowledged it, apologized, and paid compensation involved a military aircraft in an active combat zone, whereas this tragedy concerns a civilian aircraft. We do not see the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) recommending the closure of Russian airspace, even though the risks to civil aviation are evident. At present, the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) could recommend that Russia impose restrictions and issue relevant NOTAMs (Notices to Air Missions) to prevent a recurrence of such tragedies.

title: The airspace over Russia must be closed
text: Moreover, the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) could theoretically appeal to the UN General Assembly with a demand to close Russian airspace. While Russia would likely veto such a move at the UN Security Council level, this step would draw the international community's attention to the issue. This is also important because airlines can independently avoid Russian airspace based on airlines's own safety assessments. Many organizations and countries are already extremely cautious about flights over conflict zones. For example, the FAA (United States) and EASA (EU) – two key organizations responsible for aviation safety – have at times recommended flight restrictions over Iraq, Iran, Syria, Lebanon, and other regions.

title: The airspace over Russia must be closed
text: Yet, airlines from other parts of the world continue to choose routes over or into Russia. Is this risk justified? Recent events suggest otherwise. It is worth noting that Ukraine has experience closing Ukraine's airspace since 2014, at the onset of the war. Back then, in the first months of hostilities in Donbas, restrictions were imposed on flight altitudes due to the risk of shelling. After the tragedy of Malaysia Airlines Flight MH17, which was shot down at an altitude of 10 kilometers, Ukraine fully closed Ukraine's airspace over the occupied territories of Donetsk and Luhansk regions. Closing Russian airspace to civil aviation would have several consequences.

title: The airspace over Russia must be closed
text: First, it would reduce Russia’s's revenue from transit flights, which currently generate substantial profits that may, in part, finance Russia’s's aggression against Ukraine. Second, it would signal that Russia’s cannot ensure full control over Russia’s's territory and airspace safety, exposing Russia’s's vulnerability and weakness. This is particularly important as Russia’s continues to portray Russia’s as a superpower to gain influence over other states. In other words, it’s a matter of reputation and undermining political authority. Of course, the objective investigation of the Embraer 190 incident under the Montreal Convention on Acts of Unlawful Interference with Civil Aviation and the Chicago Convention on International Civil Aviation remains crucial.

title: The airspace over Russia must be closed
text: The public must know the truth about the causes of the Embraer 190 incident. If it is confirmed that Malaysia Airlines Flight MH17, which was shot down at an altitude of 10 kilometers was shot down, this would provide yet another argument for closing Russian airspace. Such a move would have economic and political consequences, weakening Russia’s and Russia’s's influence on other nations. Furthermore, the responsible party must not only apologize but also provide compensation to the families of the victims and those affected. This tragedy is not only a profound sorrow for the affected families but also a signal to the international aviation community: leaving airspace open over territories with a high level of threat is unacceptable in today's world.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: this year has been a very intense year in the bilateral relations between Switzerland and Ukraine, probably the busiest we have ever been witnessing since Ukraine's Independence 33 years ago. The pictures of the world leaders gathering in June at the Summit on Peace in Ukraine at the Swiss Bürgenstock Resort overlooking Lake Lucerne to discuss what a future peace process could look like, will remain as a testimony of one of the major diplomatic efforts deployed this year. this year started in Davos with Switzerland hosting a meeting in the framework of the Peace Formula at the level of the National Security Advisers. In June, Summit on Peace in Ukraine took place in Switzerland.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: In September, Switzerland recognized the great Ukrainian famine of 1932-1933 Holodomor as genocide against the Ukrainian civilian population. title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: The Speaker of the Verkhovna Rada, Ruslan Stefanchuk, and the Vice Prime Minister for Innovations, Development of Education, Science & Technologies — Minister of Digital Transformation of Ukraine, Mykhailo Fedorov, also paid official visits to Switzerland. title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: 556 days have passed since I had the privilege to hand over my so-called letters of credence – confirming my Government’s decision to appoint me as an Ambassador to Ukraine – to President Volodymyr Zelenskyy in the courtyard of St. Sophie Cathedral in Kyiv. Since that day, it has been my privilege to serve in a country at war with such a welcoming and kind population and with easily accessible government authorities despite all the difficulties.
