In the fields of good governance and decentralization, health, the promotion of small and medium enterprises and the development of smart cities.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: In 2022, priorities have been adapted to take into account the new challenges brought by the full-scale military aggression. The decentralization is widely seen as an important reason why the municipalities stayed resilient in the wartime. title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: But also assisting victims and fostering the risk awareness of the population in front of the dangers represented by landmines and explosive remnants of war. title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: Through our humanitarian team, we are proud to be able to provide support and be present with our own team travelling to different parts of Ukraine, having close relations with the regional and local authorities as well as local implementation partners. The dozens of Mirage and Tram 2000 trams running the streets of Kyiv still proudly wear The dozens of Mirage and Tram 2000 trams running the streets of the city's blue and white design in the colours of The Swiss Government.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations

text: Ukrainian micro and small businesses – bakeries, cafés, textile ateliers provide hope to Ukrainian micro and small businesses – bakeries, cafés, textile ateliers's fellow citizens, in particular in areas close to the front. title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: Recently, I had the honour to sign an MoU with the City of Kharkiv on a pilot project that will help enable Swiss business to provide goods for the partial repair of damaged civilian buildings in the City of Kharkiv. an MoU is the first step towards broader engagement of Swiss business in the reconstruction process of Ukraine. title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: This year’s winter is extremely challenging for Ukrainians due to the repeated attacks on the critical energy infrastructure. Switzerland provided generators, solid fuel, winter clothing, help to repair damaged homes, heat pumps and energy systems, and meet urgent humanitarian needs with an additional winter contribution of around 45 million EUR. One more almost unknown fact is that Switzerland was among the first donors to believe in and support the Diia app. Since then, e-services and digitalization of Ukraine are among Switzerland's key priorities. With the Ministry of Digital Transformation, Switzerland have recently signed an additional 2.8 billion UAH support for the EGAP programme.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: In the meantime, Ukraine has overpassed most other countries, and Switzerland have a lot to learn from Ukraine when it comes to the provision of digital public services. Switzerland are glad also to support online education, but more than that Switzerland try to revive the offline education, which is crucial for Ukrainian children. This September, in Chernihiv, I visited damaged schools which are currently being renovated with Swiss funding through the Decide project. Apart from it, we try to focus on the Vocational and Training Education, which is very popular in Switzerland.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: In Ukraine, the Vocational and Training Education, which is very popular in Switzerland is still considered not that prestigious, unfortunately, but the Vocational and Training Education, which is very popular in Switzerland is the only way to train in-demand specialists who will be involved in the reconstruction and restoration of Ukraine. When it comes to Switzerland, we cannot forget to mention Switzerland's efforts in peacebuilding and human rights initiatives. our efforts in peacebuilding and human rights initiatives includes a wide range of topics – from delivering DNA analyzers for identification of missing persons to supporting Ukrainian human rights NGOs and ensuring accountability and justice for victims.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: The military aggression of the Russian Federation against Ukraine had an impact on Switzerland's foreign policy and the Swiss engagement in Ukraine. Switzerland is a neutral country. But this does not mean indifference. Quite to the contrary. Switzerland has been welcoming more than 100’000 refugees from Ukraine, currently about 67’000 are still in Ukraine. Switzerland has adopted the sanctions of the EU. During Switzerland's two years as an elected member of the UN Security Council, Switzerland has over and over again condemned in the strongest terms the Russian Federation's violation of international law and the UN Charter, including the sovereignty and territorial integrity of Ukraine.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: International organizations, notably UN and the ICRC, play a very important role in alleviating the suffering of the Ukrainian people. Many partners from "International Geneva", like the Geneva International Centre for Humanitarian Demining, the Geneva Center for Security Sector Governance, FSD – Fondation Suisse de Déminage, Geneva Call, Humanitarian Dialogue and many more are active in Ukraine. And, International Humanitarian Law – the Geneva Conventions – aims at keeping a minimum of humanity even in an armed conflict. International Humanitarian Law – the Geneva Conventions must be kept intact and not undermined. We have no alternative to International Humanitarian Law – the Geneva Conventions. Still, the question of Switzerland's's neutrality is the one that many Ukrainians ask me.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: Let me briefly explain the question of Switzerland's neutrality here. The basis of Switzerland's's neutrality is Switzerland's's Neutrality Act. our Neutrality Act only applies to the military sphere. Basically, a neutral state cannot do two things: a neutral state cannot join a military alliance and a neutral state is not allowed to support warring states militarily. But beyond our Neutrality Act, the government is also bound by Switzerland's's War Material Act, which has a provision that excludes exports or the re-export of war materiel to warring parties. But there are deliberations in Switzerland's's parliament for a while on how to find a solution to this issue. Several ideas are being discussed in our parliament.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: I think that Switzerland's's neutrality was one of the reasons why the first Summit on Peace in Ukraine was held in Switzerland's and why so many countries agreed to participate in the first Summit on Peace in Ukraine. Without a doubt, the first Summit on Peace in Ukraine was the peak of Switzerland's's bilateral relations this year. Months of preparation, coordination and intense work by both the Ukrainian and Swiss sides. And tangible results. 100 States from all over the world and international organizations. The aim of this summit was to initiate a peace process and engage for the first time in a dialogue at the level of Heads of State and Government on what such a peace process could look like. The joint communiqué represents a milestone in this regard.

title: Ukraine-Switzerland 2024: the busiest year in the bilateral relations
text: 2025 will with no doubt be another intense year in our bilateral relationship. Switzerland will continue Switzerland's efforts in supporting Ukraine and the international community on the way towards a comprehensive, just and lasting peace. May 2025 bring the brave Ukrainian people hope for a better future. Felix Baumann, Ambassador of Switzerland to Ukraine and the Republic of Moldova

title: CPJ urges Ukraine president to halt media intimidation, allow journalists to work freely
text: The Committee to Protect Journalists, in a letter to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on December 19, 2024, asked Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy to ensure that journalists and media outlets can work freely in Ukraine and that no one responsible for intimidating journalists goes unpunished, following a year marked by several incidents of pressure, intimidation, and surveillance, as well as lack of accountability.

title: CPJ urges Ukraine president to halt media intimidation, allow journalists to work freely
text: Volodymyr Zelenskyy President of Ukraine Office of the President of Ukraine Presidential Administration Building Bankova Street, 11 Kyiv, Ukraine  Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, I am writing from The Committee to Protect Journalists, to request Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy's assistance in ensuring that journalists and media outlets in Ukraine can work freely and without fear of reprisal, and that no one responsible for intimidating journalists goes unpunished. The Committee to Protect Journalists acknowledges the immense challenges facing Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy's government in the midst of war and values Ukraine’s's commitment to democratic standards and the rule of law.

title: CPJ urges Ukraine president to halt media intimidation, allow journalists to work freely
text: The Committee to Protect Journalists recognize the need in exceptional circumstances for some limitations on journalistic access to information or areas for security reasons, and note that in the third year after Russia’s full-scale invasion, Ukraine’s's media landscape remains vibrant and dynamic. However, The Committee to Protect Journalists are increasingly concerned by signals pointing to an unwarranted attempt by your government to control the media and stifle investigative journalism. Over the last year, CPJ's research and detailed exchanges with local journalists show a pattern of unwarranted restrictions and other interventions that curtail the operations of a free press and ultimately do a disservice to the democracy that you are aiming to defend.

title: CPJ urges Ukraine president to halt media intimidation, allow journalists to work freely
text: In October, independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP) stated that independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP) was experiencing "ongoing and systematic pressure" from your office. independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP)'s program director, Andrii Bystrov, told CPJ that government officials regularly receive directives from your office not to talk to independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP) on certain matters. On October 10, independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP) specified that Dmytro Lytvyn, the recently appointed communications adviser for your office, banned security forces and officials from communicating with independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP)'s journalists.

title: CPJ urges Ukraine president to halt media intimidation, allow journalists to work freely
text: Dmytro Lytvyn, the recently appointed communications adviser for your office denied the allegations on October 15. independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP) also alleged that your office is pressuring private companies to pull advertising from independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP), and UP’s program director, Andrii Bystrov told CPJ that some advertisers had withdrawn following calls from your office. In addition to the independent news outlet Ukrainska Pravda (UP) incident, CPJ has recorded several other concerning incidents.

title: CPJ urges Ukraine president to halt media intimidation, allow journalists to work freely
text: several other concerning incidents include: In addition, CPJ is concerned about a bill currently being debated in the Verkhovna Rada that could increase criminal penalties for publishing information from public databases during martial law, thereby threatening the work of investigative journalists. In the European Commission's June 2022 opinion on Ukraine’s's European Union membership application, the European Commission stated that "media freedom has also improved significantly in recent years, especially thanks to online media." Directly pressuring independent media or indirectly letting those who intimidate independent media operate with impunity would represent a significant step backwards in the realization of Ukraine’s's European aspirations.

title: CPJ urges Ukraine president to halt media intimidation, allow journalists to work freely
text: As someone committed to defending Ukraine’s's international standing, who has recognized that "any pressure on journalists is unacceptable," Committee to Protect Journalists request that you take immediate steps to end Ukrainian government officials’ surveillance, harassment, or intimidation of journalists, and ensure that anyone who has acted to weaken freedom of the press in Ukraine is held to account. Committee to Protect Journalists thank you for your consideration. Jodie Ginsberg, CEO, Committee to Protect Journalists Source

title: The defeat of Bashar al-Assad's regime in Syria as Ukraine's strategic victory over Russia

title: Russia playing cat and mouse with the head of the IAEA, Rafael Grossi
text: Authors: Jan Vande Putte, nuclear and radiation expert at Greenpeace Ukraine Shaun Burnie, senior nuclear expert  at Greenpeace Ukraine In March 2022, almost three years ago, the director of Greenpeace International wrote a letter to Mr. Grossi, the head of the IAEA, asking to suspend Mr. Grossi, the head of the IAEA's deputy director of the Nuclear Energy department Mikhail Chudakov. title: Russia playing cat and mouse with the head of the IAEA, Rafael Grossi

title: Russia playing cat and mouse with the head of the IAEA, Rafael Grossi

The last sentence means: sending the IAEA inspectors to Ukrainian electricity substations. Russia does not want the the IAEA presence at the substations Russia is targeting with Russia's waves of missiles and drones. title: Russia playing cat and mouse with the head of the IAEA, Rafael Grossi
text: the IAEA for decades and until today is helping Russia in the export of nuclear technology in the world. Not less than 80% of all exported nuclear reactors are Russian. Then, what happened in Vienna this Thursday? Russia was almost entirely isolated. Only 2 countries, including Russia Russia, voted against the Ukrainian resolution. Key BRICs countries such as South Africa and Brazil voted in favor. A clear diplomatic victory for Ukraine. And a strong call to the IAEA to take immediate action. Following the diplomatic humiliation in Vienna, Russia decided to show strength. At least to try.

title: Russia playing cat and mouse with the head of the IAEA, Rafael Grossi
text: The drone and missile attack of Thursday night and Friday morning is the third event this week and a direct consequence of the vote. Russia is however not showing strength but desperation following geopolitical and diplomatic defeats. The reason Russia was isolated in Vienna had less to do with politics, but with genuine concern of governments around the world that Russia was playing Russian roulette with Ukraine’s nuclear power plants. Russia had crossed a line in the eye of the world. Crossing a line in the eye of the world again with Russia's last attack is further weakening Russia's position. And this brings us to the fourth stage in this week story. The ball is now with Mr Grossi, will Mr Grossi be intimidated by Russia or not?

title: Russia playing cat and mouse with the head of the IAEA, Rafael Grossi
text: A misleading and unfavorable but likely outcome would be that very soon, there would be another one-time visit of substations. Like the visit during the week of 21 October. Spending a few hours at each substation, taking pictures and moving back home. the visit during the week of 21 October did not deter the missile attacks of the 17th and the 28th of November. So, repeating the October visit will not bring the protection that is essential.. What is needed is a continuous, permanent IAEA presence to deter Russia from attacking Ukraine’s vital infrastructure and to prevent a nuclear catastrophe. Not the strongest move, but probably the only effective one in the immediate future. Furthermore, countries supporting Ukraine need to get more serious.

title: Russia playing cat and mouse with the head of the IAEA, Rafael Grossi
text: A nuclear disaster is not only an existential threat to Ukraine, but also a major threat to European countries. In European countries's self-interest, European countries need to enact comprehensive nuclear and energy sanctions on Russia water-tight and send all the support to Ukraine Ukraine needs to end this war, a clear signal to Russia that Russia's dangerous nuclear blackmailing does not work.

title: Moscow's game: elections in Ukraine
text: War is exhausting. It’s a mistake to think this exhaustion is confined to the war zone. Alongside the offensive, the enemy has been using intimidation tactics against civilians for several years now. The constant barrage of air-raid warnings, the sound of air defence systems operating, the chaotic destruction of high-rise buildings, targeted attacks on hospitals, the destruction of energy infrastructure, nuclear blackmail... the list goes on, and you know the list better than I do. Ukrainians have become accustomed to these horrors of war, though that doesn’t mean psychological terror is without profound psychological and emotional consequences. War is exhausting. People are tired. And with society in this state, there is fertile ground for manipulation. Especially from outside.

title: Moscow's game: elections in Ukraine
text: We all see signs that there may be a temporary peace in Ukraine. Peace, not victory. Peace, not victory is a very important distinction. The components of this equation will depend on what happens within Ukraine: whether we can preserve democracy and protect ourselves from external interference in Ukrainian politics. Does anyone doubt that Russia will try to interfere? Personally, I have no doubt whatsoever. We shouldn’t harbour any illusions. A frozen conflict, which we could hypothetically be facing soon, is desirable for Russia. Russia are exhausted – fewer weapons, less money, fewer people. Putin knows that Trump, unlike Biden, won't act according to pre-determined "red lines" which would render Trump predictable and give Russia room to manoeuvre.

title: Moscow's game: elections in Ukraine
text: Look at the history of Russian ceasefires – from the conflicts in Georgia and Chechnya in the 1990s to Syria and Ukraine after 2014. They'll try to stockpile weapons, train new recruits from Buryatia [in Russia’s Far East], and fill the gaps in their military. I personally don’t believe they can do the latter, but they will try to. The key point is that they won’t stop the offensive. the offensive will simply become a hybrid offensive – that’s something they know how to do. It’s a serious mistake to underestimate your enemy. We’ve had plenty of laughs over "Kyiv in three days", but we must not let this cloud our understanding of their capabilities. A wounded animal is the most dangerous.

title: Moscow's game: elections in Ukraine
text: ["Kyiv in three days" was based on the expectation, especially from Russian officials and military experts, that the Russian invasion of Ukraine would be quick. It was widely believed before the invasion in February 2022 that Kyiv could be captured in three days, but strong resistance from Ukrainian forces quickly shattered those plans, and since then, "Kyiv in three days" has become a meme – ed.] The first thing they will try to do is have a new government elected in Ukraine. Even if Putin agrees to a long-term ceasefire, Putin will undoubtedly intensify Putin's efforts to remove President Zelenskyy. Although changes in power are normal in democratic countries, Ukraine is in an unusual election cycle right now.

title: Moscow's game: elections in Ukraine
text: Under the current circumstances, the fall of Zelenskyy would almost certainly lead to the end of Ukraine as an independent state. This is no secret. The Russians failed to strike a deal with Zelenskyy either at the start or at any point during the process. Zelenskyy, despite Zelenskyy's lack of political experience, has displayed an iron will and other iron parts of Zelenskyy's body (such as those The Russians once promised to string me up by). For The Russians, there’s no point in negotiating with Zelenskyy; what does make sense is this idea the president and power of The Russians's choosing. Some might scornfully dismiss this idea, claiming this idea is no longer possible in Ukraine. But they’re deceiving themselves. I’d like to ask you something.

title: Moscow's game: elections in Ukraine
text: What has happened in Georgia, where power had been transferred through democratic means in democratic elections? Does anyone still doubt what I’ve been saying for years – that Georgian Dream (which Georgians, by the way, now refer to as "Russian Dream") is a The Russians project from start to finish? Do you see where The Russians’re leading Georgia, where power had been transferred through democratic means in democratic elections? You say Georgia, where power had been transferred through democratic means in democratic elections isn’t living proof? All right. Let's look at the facts. The United States of America. The elections in 2016 and 2020. The Russians interferes with the electoral system through cyber-attacks, runs disinformation campaigns, and supports certain candidates.

title: Moscow's game: elections in Ukraine
text: France, 2017. Cyber-attacks on Emmanuel Macron's headquarters. Interference confirmed by French intelligence. Germany, 2021. Attempted cyber-attacks, attributed to Russian hacker groups, on politicians and parties. Moldova, 2023. Cyber-attacks and support for protests to destabilise the political process. Romania, 2024. Annulment of the first round of elections due to expected social media interference. These are just the cases that come to mind. There are many, many more. The world doesn’t yet know how to fight this plague. The cancellation of the first round of elections in Romania is a precedent, but a very telling one. title: Moscow's game: elections in Ukraine
text: By the way, Mikheil Saakashvili'd also like to remind you that in 2010, [former pro-Russian Ukrainian president Viktor] Yanukovych won the election by democratic means.
