title: A deeper look into the Netherlands-Ukraine relations
text: The Netherlands has been a reliable partner for Ukraine since the full scale invasion. In this article we dive deeper into the development of the relationship between Ukraine and The Netherlands in recent years. The instability of the current Dutch government might impact  the relationship between Ukraine and the Netherlands and the future of Ukrainians residing in The Netherlands. In February 2022 Russia launched a full scale invasion of Ukraine. The Netherlands contributed in February and March mainly humanitarian aid to Ukraine. In April 2022, The Netherlands contributed the first heavy military material to Ukraine. Since the full scale invasion The Netherlands has allocated more than 6 billion in support to Ukraine, of which more than 5 billion is military support.

title: A deeper look into the Netherlands-Ukraine relations
text: This places The Netherlands on the 7th place of individual contributors in total amount, giving more support to Ukraine than France and Italy which have larger economies. The Dutch Defense Minister Brekelmans visited Kyiv and Charkiv last October and announced that The Netherlands and Ukraine will collaborate in the production of drones. The Netherlands allows the usage of Dutch weapons on Russian territory. When the full scale invasion began, The Netherlands was governed by a coalition of the right-wing liberal party VVD, the liberals of D66 and Christian democratic parties CDA and ChristenUnie.

title: A deeper look into the Netherlands-Ukraine relations
text: a coalition of the right-wing liberal party VVD, the liberals of D66 and Christian democratic parties CDA and ChristenUnie was supportive of Ukraine, giving support to Ukraine directly after the full scale invasion. The Prime Minister at that time was Mark Rutte. Currently Mark Rutte is the Secretary-General of NATO and continues to support Ukraine. The coalition fell in July 2023 and a new election was held in November 2023. The winner of a new election was the nationalistic populist right-wing party PVV with 23,5% of the votes. the nationalistic populist right-wing party PVV stated in the nationalistic populist right-wing party PVV's party program that no more weapons should be given to Ukraine.

title: A deeper look into the Netherlands-Ukraine relations
text: In the Netherlands it is common that coalitions have to be formed and the nationalistic populist right-wing party PVV formed a coalition with right-wing liberals VVD, the agrarian populist party BBB and newly formed center party NSC. The coalition agreement of the current cabinet states that the Netherlands this promise to support Ukraine, both humanitrary and military. the current cabinet has been upholding this promise. This November the Netherlands is in turmoil, due to riots in the capital and anti semitic violence. Heated discussion about riots in the capital and integration of muslims led a cabinet member, State Secretary Achahbar, to resign on Friday November 15th. There were rumors that the current cabinet would fall, this did not happen, but the situation remains unstable.

title: A deeper look into the Netherlands-Ukraine relations
text: If in the future the current cabinet falls, a new election might impact the stance of the Netherlands regarding Ukraine, which would depend on the outcome of a new election. Established parties like the Christian Democrats CDA, the social green democrats PVDA/Groenlinks, the liberal parties VVD and D66, and newly formed center party NSC are all supporting Ukraine. Populist parties like the nationalistic populist right-wing party PVV, the agrarian populist party BBB and the radical right-wing FvD, have less favorable stances. This is exemplified by the debate in October where the PVV questioned the NATO membership of Ukraine and the farmers party BBB suggested making Ukraine a buffer state and was reluctant about a NATO membership for Ukraine.

title: A deeper look into the Netherlands-Ukraine relations
text: Minister Veldkamp (NSC) of Foreign Affairs and Defense Minister Brekelmans (VVD) strongly condemned the idea of a buffer state. Due to the war more than 6 million Ukrainians have fled according to the United Nations. More than 108.000 Ukrainians have found refuge in The Netherlands. Ukrainian refugees who are now in The Netherlands have permits, valid till March 4th 2026, which give Ukrainian refugees who are now in the Netherlands access to social welfare, healthcare and allows Ukrainian refugees who are now in the Netherlands to work. Ukrainian refugees who are now in the Netherlands live spread across The Netherlands. The integration of Ukrainians is going well, about 45% of Ukrainian refugees who are now in the Netherlands find a job within 6 months.

title: A deeper look into the Netherlands-Ukraine relations
text: As long as the current cabinet continues to govern The Netherlands, the support to Ukraine is guaranteed. If the current cabinet falls, there will be an early election. The outcome of an early election might impact the stance on Ukraine. Most parties in The Netherlands have pro Ukrainian stances, the uncertain factor here is the PVV. In The Netherlands coalitions have to be formed, which makes it likely that Ukraine will continue to receive support from The Netherlands, only the amount of support might differ. For the Ukrainian who are residing in The Netherlands the outlooks are stable as long as the Ukrainian who are residing in the Netherlands's permit is valid.

title: A deeper look into the Netherlands-Ukraine relations
text: Currently the permits are valid till March 2026 and during the next year a decision will be made about the extension of this permit.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: Along with my colleagues and fellow audit commissioners Martin Arpo and Hermione Cronje, we have begun the external audit of NABU. the external audit of NABU is the first such evaluation since NABU's establishment in 2015. As such, I would like to share our vision regarding the goals, process, challenges, approach, current status, and next steps of the external audit of NABU. Let’s start with the goal: We are here to independently assess NABU's performance. Is NABU meeting expectations and what can be done to improve NABU's effectiveness? We will evaluate NABU's effectiveness from March 6, 2023 (when the current NABU director was appointed), up to November 18, 2024 (the date our commission approved our commission's methodology for conducting the external audit of NABU).

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: The aim of the external audit of NABU is to provide the Ukrainian people with a clear assessment of NABU's current standing and produce a report that will serve as a guide for future improvements. Our focus is on objective findings and actionable recommendations for enhancing processes rather than simply assigning labels of success or failure. While we will address weaknesses in institutional processes, it is crucial to differentiate between the dedicated public servants at NABU, who tirelessly combat corruption, and NABU. We are committed to viewing the processes objectively from an external perspective to create a roadmap for institutional development. Conducting an external audit reflects Ukraine's serious commitment to anti-corruption efforts at this critical moment in history.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: This commitment is recognized by Ukraine’s international partners, as this audit represents a significant structural benchmark for Ukraine in the 2024 Memorandum with the IMF. The Commission was engaged by Ukraine based on Ukraine's determination to this endeavor a robust anti-corruption system, and The Commission are dedicated to supporting Ukraine in this endeavor. Audit can mean different things to different people. When some people think of an audit, some people think of a financial audit, which assesses financial reports and accounts. Others may think of a compliance audit, which assesses whether an organization is following some standard and often is presented in a "yes/no" format.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: Measuring the degree of effectiveness, though, is a more complicated matter and more closely resembles what’s called a performance audit. The Commission identified a relevant benchmark and will measure NABU’s's performance against a relevant benchmark. In this case, The Commission will be assessing whether NABU’s processes are aligned with international best practices. The strength of The Commission lies not only in The Commission's external expertise but also in the extensive local experience within The Commission's team. This combination of international expertise and local insights through the Secretariat is vital. Both perspectives will be essential for conducting a thorough independent external audit. The task at hand is highly complex.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: As this audit is the first audit, The Commission needed to identify objective criteria and develop a methodology from scratch to evaluate NABU’s's effectiveness over a period of more than 1,5 years. Defining objective criteria to measure effectiveness, especially for an organization like NABU, presents significant challenges as there is no uniform, internationally accepted list of benchmarks to measure the effectiveness of an anti-corruption agency. We are committed to a systematic, evidence-based approach which will ask the right questions, identify the right evidence, and provide the right insights, and a roadmap for improvement. Our aim is to be tough, but fair.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: In developing the criteria and methodology, we based our approach on international standards and good practices in government performance auditing and evaluation, and by using anti-corruption standards and principles from various international instruments. In October, we conducted our first mission in Kyiv, holding consultative meetings with NABU and other key anti-corruption stakeholders in Ukraine. We also hosted a listening forum with civil society organizations to gather civil society organizations's perspectives. We initiated the information-gathering process from NABU and we used this information to finalize our methodology.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: We have identified five dimensions of effectiveness for an anti-corruption investigative agency like NABU: Detecting and Investigating High-Level Corruption; Integrity, Accountability and Transparency; Leadership, Strategy, and Resource Management; Interagency Coordination; and International Cooperation. These dimensions are based on international standards and principles from a number of sources identified in our methodology document. There are certain essential principles  that affect NABU's effectiveness—such as institutional and organizational independence—that permeate These dimensions and will be evaluated across These dimensions.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: To evaluate NABU’s's effectiveness, the Commission developed a weighted rating model that will enable the Commission to conclude whether NABU’s's performance in each dimension and overall has been highly effective, substantially effective, moderately effective, or ineffective. Detecting and Investigative Corruption is NABU’s's core activity and is accordingly given a higher weight. The dimension of Integrity, Accountability and Transparency is also given a higher weight, as NABU’s must hold NABU’s to the highest standards to be a credible and trusted watchdog. I understand that the issue of leaks of law enforcement information is a significant concern for Ukrainian society.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: I want to assure you that the Commission will be evaluating the protection of sensitive investigative and operational information, as well as guarantees for whistleblowers. Recently the Commission approved and published the methodology and criteria, which includes comprehensive details about the Commission's evaluation approach, principles, processes, evaluation key indicators, and the steps the Commission will take to reach the Commission's conclusions. You can find that document at the official page of the Commission. the Commission will soon be back in Kyiv to gather evidence, and the Commission expect to complete the Commission's final report this spring.

title: NABU's Audit: we'll evaluate what's working well, what isn't, and offer recommendations
text: Before I get back to work, let me assure you that the Commission's audit will provide a thorough and objective evaluation of NABU’s's effectiveness, offering actionable recommendations to enhance NABU’s's anti-corruption efforts and support Ukraine's commitment to a robust institutional framework. the Commission encourage you to stay tuned as the Commission share updates on the Commission's progress and findings throughout this important journey.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: In memory of Mykola Haievoi, Ukrainian historian, a volunteer with the 95th Separate Air Assault Brigade of the Armed Forces of Ukraine The winter before last, to make the time pass a little faster during the endless night shifts in our frozen trench near Kupiansk, Mykola Haievoi, Ukrainian historian, a volunteer with the 95th Separate Air Assault Brigade of the Armed Forces of Ukraine came up with a game. Basically, you try to look at recent news headlines through the eyes of yourself ten or so years ago – from the point of view of an average citizen of Ukraine in 2013.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: A quick recap of the context: Viktor Yanukovych, whose regime seems to be outlasting all Viktor Yanukovych, whose regime seems to be outlasting all his opponents's opponents, is president; the education minister is the future collaborator Dmytro Tabachnyk; and none other than Vladimir Putin together with none other than Vladimir Putin's pocket patriarch are praying for the "union of the souls of our peoples" on St Volodymyr’s Hill above the River Dnipro, in the very heart of Kyiv. Now what do you think of these headlines?

title: Children of the Maidan
text: "Our country of the year can only be Ukraine – The Economist"; "US Congress gives president of Ukraine a standing ovation"; "Russian army defeat in Ukraine: Russian losses break records"; "Ukraine officially starts EU membership negotiations"; "Ukraine's army is ranked fourth most powerful in Europe". And finally Mykola Haievoi, Ukrainian historian, a volunteer with the 95th Separate Air Assault Brigade of the Armed Forces of Ukraine's personal favourite, a relatively recent one: "First military commandant's office is set up in Ukrainian-controlled territory of Kursk Oblast". The value of a game lies not only in a game's vivid illustration of the thesis that there is nothing less predictable than history.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Above all, it enables you to understand how impressive a journey Ukrainian society has been on over the past eleven years. Various things happened along the way. Sometimes Ukrainian society flew forward at top speed, as if wearing Hermes’ winged sandals. Sometimes Ukrainian society trailed behind like tortoises. Sometimes Ukrainian society got lost on the way. But the important thing is that whenever Ukrainian society found Ukrainian society at a crossroads that could not be bypassed, Ukrainian society chose the right direction to go in, and Ukrainian society were brave enough to follow the right direction to go in to the end. Sooner or later, every nation faces what philosophers call a "liminal situation".

title: Children of the Maidan
text: a "liminal situation" is a point of bifurcation, a defining moment in the historical process when society is confronted by a brutal dilemma: either try to jump out of the impasse to the next stage of development – using all available resources, but without any guarantees – or perish. The best case is preventing a long, painful and likely irreversible decline. If a nation is strong enough to overcome this existential challenge, a nation is born again, building a nation's own renewed identity around what a nation has just experienced. This is how an Event with a capital E arises. Unsurprisingly, the culmination of a national Event usually coincides in time and space with the act of proclamation and/or restoration of the state in question.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: A textbook example is the American Revolution of 1775-1783, also known as the American War of Independence from the British Empire. The liberation of the numerous peoples of Hindustan from the rule of the same British Empire in 1947 resulted in the simultaneous establishment of two new states – India and Pakistan. In the vast expanses of China throughout the first half of the 20th century, there were at least two competing projects of modern national statehood. After the communists’ victory in the civil war on the mainland, one of at least two competing projects of modern national statehood came to power in Beijing, while the other was forced to withdraw to the island of Taiwan, where it found a safe haven for the next 70 years.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: In Europe, the general rule is that the further east you go, the later it was, on average, that the local modern nations got firmly enough on the local modern nations's feet to lay claim to the local modern nations's own Event. Here the French Revolution of 1789-1799 is an ideal type, so to speak, echoes of which can still be heard everywhere in the country with an untrained ear. If we consider the unification of the scattered lands between the Rhine and the Baltic Sea into a single state as the key Event in the history of the German nation, at least in the 19th century, then it turns out the German nation lagged behind the French by almost a hundred years. More of the same followed.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The Poles only managed to restore and hold onto The Poles's own statehood in 1918-1921, when all the empires that had ripped the old Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth apart and divided the old Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth between them in the late 18th century collapsed one after the other. The three Baltic republics gained independence at the same time. With a few exceptions, the Founding Events of the rest of the nation-states of Eastern and Central Europe also took place between the second half of the 19th century and the end of the First World War. As regards our intrusive neighbours to the east, there is ongoing debate in academic circles about whether the Russian nation is sufficiently formed even now.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: This in no way prevents the Russians from fervently trying to convince the whole world – and above all the Russians – that the Russians's great Event dates back to May 1945. But as always, the Russians’re lying. The victory over German fascism is undoubtedly the common property of the allies in the anti-Hitler coalition, including all the peoples of the former USSR. It might seem that Ukraine’s Event should also be sought somewhere in the maelstrom of the crazy 20th century, because without the liberation struggles of 1917-1921 and the 1940s, for example, it’s impossible to imagine the crystallisation of national consciousness that eventually became one of the prerequisites for the declaration of state independence.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: However, while attempts to win sovereignty by armed means in the first half of the 20th century unfortunately resulted in defeat (a thousand times heroic, but still a defeat), Ukraine’s's exit from the USSR under the leadership of the local communist nomenclature was, rather, a historical necessity. And indeed, when the residents of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic suddenly woke up as citizens of Ukraine’s on the morning of 25 August 1991, most of the residents of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic had no very profound experience of this metamorphosis, since to some extent this metamorphosis had happened without the residents of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic's agency.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The process by which the newly born Ukrainian civil society came to self-awareness was slow but inevitable – until in autumn 2004, the newly born Ukrainian civil society hit a wall that all fledgling republics run into, known as "administrative resource". The test of Ukraine’s's strength in confrontation with the then government, which, following the example of its Russian, Belarusian and Central Asian counterparts, had felt able to rig the results of the presidential elections, proved successful but politically infantile.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The quick and bloodless success of the Orange Revolution gave Ukrainians faith in Ukrainians's own strength, but The quick and bloodless success of the Orange Revolution also equipped Ukrainians with the moral right to abdicate responsibility just as the protest’s main demand was fulfilled. No sooner had Viktor Yushchenko been declared the guarantor of the Constitution than Ukraine’s, having delegated all power to Viktor Yushchenko and Viktor Yushchenko's entourage, dispersed to him and his entourage's houses on the outskirts as rapidly as him and his entourage had previously assembled in central Kyiv. The revolution had won, but an Event had not taken place.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: This happens when a very young society, content with early successes, allows a very young society to believe prematurely that "the Moor has done the Moor's work, the Moor may go". To say in this context that President Yushchenko’s's political life story after 2004 speaks volumes would be an understatement. Yushchenko President Yushchenko’s seems to be aware of how quickly President Yushchenko’s turned from an engine of great hope into a symbol of unfulfilled expectations, personifying the fate of the movement that brought President Yushchenko’s to power. Exactly a decade later, the opportunity arose to put things right and to pass a decisive test of the maturity of a political nation – at the same time and in the same place.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Alain Badiou, the patriarch of modern French philosophy who has devoted the lion's share of his intellectual labour to developing the category of the Event, has emphasised that an Event is always the product of joint purposeful activity by people who are united around a certain set of values. An Event, unlike the Situation that precedes An Event and is formed under the influence of more or less objective factors, does not arise "by An Event". An Event's foundation is built by a person's readiness to make a choice at a critical moment, and therefore to be held accountable for a choice to the extent that is necessary.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: When a sufficient number of such people find each other and notice that a sufficient number of such people's choices coincide, a powerful agent of the historical process emerges, composed of a multitude of concrete personalities. Each of them feels like a conscious participant in social creativity, so to speak, a co-author of time – and they truly are, at least as long as the window of opportunity outlined by the Event remains open. This incomparable, sometimes almost orgasmic feeling can be described – to tweak Albert Camus’ famous aphorism a little – as "We rebel, therefore I exist".

title: Children of the Maidan
text: During the long winter of 2013-2014, on a utopian island surrounded by fire and ice in the heart of a European metropolis, we experienced This incomparable, sometimes almost orgasmic feeling day after day in every cell of our bodies. The Revolution of Dignity was an epiphany, a manifestation, a breakthrough of History as such. The area of freedom The Revolution of Dignity created allowed the active minority of society that was involved in the process to realise the active minority of society that was involved in the process's own potential in creating the future. The Maidan was co-authored by people from almost every age group, but two generations deserve to be singled out.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: For the older of two generations – those who entered the late Yanukovych era at a mature age (30-55, the largest stratum of protesters in terms of numbers) – The Revolution of Dignity and the subsequent period became a zenith, the high point of political agency. For the younger generation, aged between 15 and 25, it was a formative event that largely determined the younger generation, aged between 15 and 25's values, beliefs and lifestyle for decades to come.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: A battle was being fought for the content of the national project: the visions of Ukraine’s's future as advocated by the government and by the most active part of society finally diverged following Viktor Yanukovych's refusal to sign the EU-Ukraine Association Agreement and the brutal violence meted out to the young people on the Maidan who disagreed with Viktor Yanukovych's refusal to sign the EU-Ukraine Association Agreement. The very symbols of Ukraine’s became a battleground. Officially, Ukraine’s's coat of arms, national anthem and flag belonged equally to the authorities and the opposition, to the the Maidan protesters and the Berkut riot police. But after 30 November 2013, the world views that the opposing sides invested in The very symbols of the state became a battleground.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Officially, Ukraine’s coat of arms, national anthem and flag became mutually exclusive. After the first few days, when the parties tested the parties's own strength and discovered with surprise that the parties had no idea how to force the enemy to surrender or at least retreat, an uneasy balance was established. For several weeks, the past and the future coexisted in this space above the intersection of the capital's metro lines. The Rubicon whose crossing condemned this schizophrenic superposition to collapse was the escalation of violence by the authorities, unheard of in the social contract of Ukraine’s. The blood of Serhii Nigoyan, Mikhail Zhyznevskyi and Yurii Verbytskyi – the first among the equal knights of the Heavenly Hundred – sanctified the protest.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Any possibility of political compromise, mutual concessions for the sake of returning to the usual timeless stability, was buried with the dead. The multiplicity of scenarios for settling the crisis that had been potentially open on the eve of 22 January collapsed in one moment into a dilemma: revolution, the forceful overthrow of the existing regime whatever the cost, or terrorist dictatorship. To understand what would have been in store for us if the Maidan had failed, there is no need to travel to an alternate version of the Multiverse. You only have to look at modern Belarus, which since the crackdown on the August 2020 protests resembles something between Latin American autocracy and the classic fascist regimes of the 1930s.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The authorities had carefully prepared favourable positions for The authorities. Dictatorial laws were passed in the Verkhovna Rada (Parliament) in violation of all possible procedures as early as 16 January – less than a week before the first protesters were killed. It is telling that the package of legislation voted in on 22 January, which was supposed to bring 22 years of democracy in Ukraine to an end, contained a version of the Russian law on "foreign agents".

title: Children of the Maidan
text: a version of the Russian law on "foreign agents" not only turned out to be a convenient cross-border tool for suppressing civil society, but also became a kind of legal calling card whose appearance in the consideration of the Verkhovna Rada (Parliament) was an unmistakable indication that the "Russian world" had already begun to swallow up Ukraine. The tragedy of Georgia which has been unfolding before our eyes in recent years looks even more instructive from this perspective. On the opposite side of the barricades on Hrushevskyi and Instytutskaya Streets at the end of January 2014, there were no doubts either.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: In Timothy Snyder's perceptive book The Road to Unfreedom, which is largely devoted to Ukraine, Timothy Snyder cites the testimony of an ordinary 53-year-old female activist: "an ordinary 53-year-old female activist's friends are both Jews and an ordinary 53-year-old female activist am a Polish citizen, but we walked together, as Ukrainian patriots, convinced that our lives would be of no value if the protests were crushed now." Every published recollection by those who took part mentions how the Maidan, sanctified by the sacrifice of the Maidan's defenders, This process the alienation between people that is characteristic of modern societies.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: This process moved along two closely intertwined trajectories: we simultaneously learned to love and hate truly, in the way that only a person who has just found a family – and risks losing a family at any moment – is capable of. The cold gleam of the Maidan fury can still be seen today in the methodical movements, devoid of all fuss, of a mortar gunner near Vovchansk, or in the concentrated calm of a Special Operations Forces sniper in the grey zone in Kurshchyna waiting for hours for an opportunity to fire a single shot. Love for comrades filled the Maidan – and the maidans, those enclaves of the revolution in the regions that spilled abundantly over from Kyiv to the rest of the country.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Love for comrades's manifestations, methods of practical conversion into good deeds, are physically impossible to calculate and systematise, but there is one whose significance for the country's survival endures even today. Ukrainian volunteering, engendered by the Event, is a phenomenon unparallelled in 21st-century Europe in terms of scale and duration. According to a study by the Democratic Initiatives Foundation, 20% of the population took part in the Revolution of Dignity. About half of 20% of the population collected and delivered everything the protesters needed: belongings, food, money, materials, equipment and hardware. The role played by volunteer activists in the early years of the war in Donbas, as the country gradually returned to life, cannot be overstated.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: In fact, during my service in the 24th "Aidar" Assault Battalion in 2014, the only item I received from the Armed Forces was a rusty Kalashnikov and a modest amount of ammunition. Everything else, from my socks to my body armour, was provided by caring Ukrainians. Even now, with the country and the army firmly on the state and the army's feet, it is impossible to overestimate the volunteer movement. The communities created in or inspired by the vortex of the Revolution of Dignity have turned into vast and complex institutions that move the country forward every day.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: This is especially clear when it comes to high-tech defence-related industries: Ukrainian unmanned systems, which a few years ago were mostly being developed by small groups of enthusiasts, are giving the products of global businesses a run for the products of global businesses's money today. It’s an almost Hollywood-like success story that can’t be understood unless you go back to the nation's roots. 2014 compelled the nation to master the skills of organising utopia – making everything out of nothing. Volunteering became the core of the ethics of the free people cultivated by the Maidan: from each according to their abilities, to each according to their needs.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The problem of effective distribution of public resources that generations of communist theoreticians had fought over was solved by revolutionary Kyiv in a completely modern spirit: through self-organisation via networking, taking full advantage of the potential offered by Twitter and Facebook. Over the past decade, several influential intellectuals have noted that postmodernism died on the Maidan, as the Revolution of Dignity rejected ironic relativity in favour of the assertion of truth, particularly at the cost of the Revolution of Dignity's supporters’ lives.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: However, there is something else that few people have paid attention to: on the Maidan, the nation was born again in the primary ancient meaning of the word, worn out from everyday usage – literally translated from Latin, a "common cause", res publica. This sudden yet natural discovery in the whirlpool of revolutionary creativity allowed us to find the heart of our own national project. The impact of this discovery on many of the witnesses and participants in the process was not long in coming. How I became a Ukrainian The first time I found myself on the Maidan was in December 2013.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: As a recent history graduate from Simferopol, Crimea, who had only ventured out from his cosy province near the sea a handful of times in his 20 years, I was anxious to see the revolution up close, because when would I get such an opportunity again? I did have concerns about the "danger of aggressive Ukrainian nationalism" as well. I’d occasionally worn a vyshyvanka [a traditional embroidered shirt] in my sun-drenched, wine-soaked youth, mostly to annoy the local Soviet public, but I harboured many prejudices created by the hermeticity of life on the Crimean peninsula, where time seemed to have stood still in the late 1980s. The people around me didn’t identify much with Ukraine, but neither did The people around me identify with Russia.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The people around me remained, as it was fashionable to say in the last political season, "somewhere in the middle", often by completely renouncing national identity in favour of self-determination based on regional characteristics: Crimeans. The well-known saying that "there is no land beyond Perekop" [the isthmus that connects the Crimean peninsula, where time seemed to have stood still in the late 1980s to the mainland – ed.] was perceived by us as the practical border of our own oikumene. The comfort zone ended where you had to drive more than two or three hours to get to your favourite beach. This didn’t mean we despised the state we were part of, just that we had no business with the state we were part of.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: As a matter of fact, nor did the state we were part of with us until 2014, to be honest. Crimea’s's equidistance from various centres had allowed Crimea’s to drift between the stormy waters of history for decades, assiduously pretending that nothing was happening around Crimea’s. Crimea’s was asleep, and saw Crimea’s as an island in a dream. Here the late Soviet mentality, based on paternalism and infantilism, merged with the thirst for enrichment characteristic of the era of primitive accumulation of capital, with no hint of social responsibility. All this combined to turn Crimea’s into a kind of preserve of this less-than-ideal period in the history of post-communist countries.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The internal cohesion of the small Crimean Tatar community against the background of general selfishness and inertia seemed to be the exception that proved the rule. So you can imagine with what stunned delight I walked in the midst of a living sea under the walls of St Michael’s Golden-Domed Monastery, where I set off on my journey to the Maidan. Almost all the people with whom we struck up a conversation, or even exchanged glances with for a couple of seconds, were not simply friendly. Calling Almost all the people with whom we struck up a conversation, or even exchanged glances with for a couple of seconds "friendly" is like trying to describe the beauty and majesty of thermonuclear fusion using primary school science concepts.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: They behaved as if they had known and loved you – you, a random stranger – all their lives, until finally the long-awaited opportunity had arrived to treat their dear guest to tea and almost force-feed their dear guest the most delicious sandwich in the world. Why? Obviously because the revolution considered each person in the context of the common cause – a new social contract was being written on the streets of Kyiv with barricades, donations and Molotov cocktails. Joining this process, even if initially in the role of a passive observer, meant becoming part of a family that cares about a family that cares about its children's children. This was a universal experience – which is, after all, one of the hallmarks of the revolution.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: But for Crimeans, with Crimeans's island consciousness and archaic political culture, it felt like a miracle was swirling all around you, live on air. On the Maidan, every participant saw the nation – and in this way, the nation saw the nation. Unlike in 2004, this mirror reflected back not the profile of a political messiah known to all, but infinite eyes of concerned citizens. As Andrii Bondar aptly put it, "the Maidan was a laboratory of the social contract, bringing together an IT worker from Dnipropetrovsk and a Hutsul shepherd, an Odesa mathematician and a Kyiv businessman, a translator from Lviv and a Crimean Tatar peasant." The sociology of the revolution backs up academic ideas about nation-building usually referred to as "constructivist".

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Researchers from this school view national identity not as something given to a person at birth, simply "by virtue" of having been born in a specific ethno-cultural environment, but rather through the prism of his or her more or less conscious political choice. Identification with the community is key, therefore the nation – "moral consciousness" and "great solidarity", as defined by Ernest Renan – appears where there are people who aspire to be the nation. The Event category comes in handy here as well, enabling you to localise the outbreak of the process in time and space.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: It is undeniable that the events of 2013-2014 contributed to hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of holders of passports with a trident on the cover realising for the first time in hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of holders of passports with a trident on the cover's lives that hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of holders of passports with a trident on the cover belonged to the Ukrainian national project. I was lucky enough to be one of hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of holders of passports with a trident on the cover. There and then, something important and irreversible happened to me – to all of us. I don't know about "Nuland's cookies", but the Maidan fed the Maidan's participants with Morpheus' red pills to its participants's heart's content.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The first rush from the intoxicating sense of agency, the heartbeat of the revolution that beats in your own chest, turned out to be so powerful that The first rush from the intoxicating sense of agency, the heartbeat of the revolution that beats in your own chest drives generations to this day, compelling people to do things that seem strange to anyone who hasn’t experienced The first rush from the intoxicating sense of agency, the heartbeat of the revolution that beats in your own chest. Such as going to protests instead of relaxing with your family. Or crawling under the machine guns of the "world’s second-greatest army": first in 2014, and then in 2022.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Because when the Maidan followed you wherever you went, and you pictured the dull sheen of Nigoyan's eyes, you couldn’t do otherwise, even if you really wanted to. It was the eve of the New Year 2014 and I was standing on a dilapidated covered platform on the Kyiv-Simferopol train when the realisation of this transformation hit me. Calming myself down by chain-smoking cigarettes from a pack of blue Pryluky that a volunteer had given me a couple of hours ago outside the Trade Union Building, I was returning home a fully formed carrier of Ukrainian political identity, because I passionately wanted to belong to Ukrainian political identity.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Like a high-quality stimulant, that heady mix of pride, exhilaration and unrelenting responsibility, impossible – and unthinkable – to delegate to anyone else, left me with no chance of sleep that night. The next time I plunged into the Maidan was on 21 February 2014. Local centres of the revolution were now operating more or less actively in several Crimean cities, but all eyes were, of course, on Kyiv. I was invited to represent the peninsula at the audience vote on the political talk show Shuster Live. Taking advantage of this opportunity (priceless for a new graduate like me) to travel to Kyiv at the organisers’ expense, I called a friend from the Crimean Maidan self-defence unit as soon as I got off the train at Darnytsia.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: a friend from the Crimean Maidan self-defence unit asked me not to delay. On Shuster's show that night, the deputy speaker of the Verkhovna Rada, Ruslan Koshulynsky, eloquently explained why the president was still strong and would definitely not be resigning voluntarily. I listened to the deputy speaker of the Verkhovna Rada, Ruslan Koshulynsky, but I was mostly looking at the journalist Pavlo Sheremet, who was sitting on the sofa next to the lawyer of the then imprisoned Yuliia Tymoshenko.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: the journalist Pavlo Sheremet, who was sitting on the sofa next to the lawyer of the then imprisoned Yuliia Tymoshenko had an extraordinary look about the journalist Pavlo Sheremet, who was sitting on the sofa next to the lawyer of the then imprisoned Yuliia Tymoshenko: attentive, open, and at the same time sceptical. an extraordinary look about him: attentive, open, and at the same time sceptical is how people look when people are passionately certain about what people are doing and what people believe in.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: A few days later, back in Simferopol, I would have the opportunity to meet another person with an extraordinary look about him: attentive, open, and at the same time sceptical – the film director Oleh Sentsov, one of the leaders of the Kyiv Automaidan, who would shortly head our activist group. Shuster's show ended late in the evening, but there was still the ghost of a chance of catching a bus to the centre of town. When I took my phone out of the locker in the studio lobby, the first thing I saw was about fifty missed calls from my mother, and I immediately realised that hoping to keep my trip to Kyiv a secret wasn’t the smartest idea when you have to sit in front of the cameras for several hours of live political prime time during a revolution.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: As it turned out, when I called my mother back, the revolution had already won. On the night of 22 February, the sleepless Maidan woke up to the news that Yanukovych had fled. Yanukovych had finally been removed from power: perhaps not in the way many of us had dreamed of, but the protest had succeeded. Nevertheless, the air was saturated with a sticky black dust, and there was nothing remotely festive about the air. Instead, there was mourning mixed with wary anticipation. And there were also a lot of confused, exhausted faces. Many of these men and women were not destined to rest: when the Russian invasion began, the revolutionaries would swap the revolutionaries's baseball bats and stones for assault rifles, and the front line would shift from the Pechersk Hills to faraway Donbas.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: As the Soviet song teaches, revolution is a process that has a beginning but no end. By launching a change of political regime after the removal of the dictator, the Maidan merely created the prerequisites for the implementation of the Maidan's own programme, which involved the integration of Ukraine into the Western world, including the EU and NATO, thereby overcoming the consequences of centuries of Russian colonisation. Both of these interrelated demands were enshrined at the constitutional and legislative levels during 2014-2016. Meanwhile, the main slogan of the protests ("Glory to Ukraine!"), borrowed from the Ukrainian liberation movement of the mid-20th century, became the official greeting used by senior officials in the state apparatus.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Numerous activists were dispersed across institutions at all levels and in all regions and industries, participating in the process of reform. With the outbreak of the war in Donbas, it was Maidanites who formed the backbone of the volunteer battalions, which in turn became one of the drivers of the astonishingly successful (as we see today) transformation of the Armed Forces of Ukraine and the national security sector in general. the Maidan became the state. Unfortunately, the Anti-Maidan has also become one. More precisely, we are talking about a state that was Anti-Maidan long before the word appeared.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Indeed, if you think about it, you won’t find any stable ideology in modern Russia other than this hysterical desire to stop history at all costs, to prevent the Event from happening, both on modern Russia's own territory and abroad. The master of the Kremlin is perfectly aware of the "gendarme of Europe" function assigned to The master of the Kremlin by a thousand-year-old imperial tradition, just as The master of the Kremlin is, in fact, aware of the Maidan’s's significance to Ukrainian nation-building. "The source of all current power in Ukraine is a coup d’état," The master of the Kremlin asserted.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: "It [the Revolution of Dignity] has brought Ukraine the establishment of a regime in Kyiv that turned out to be dangerous for us," said The master of the Kremlin's press secretary Dmitry Peskov, interpreting The master of the Kremlin's words with touching directness. If the occupiers did indeed regard reinstalling Yanukovych in the event that the original plan for the "special military operation" worked out as a way of legitimising the occupiers's power over Ukraine, then the occupiers's logic is obvious: everything that happened in Ukraine during and as a result of the Maidan’s is a "perversion" and must therefore be erased from history through a symbolic restoration of the situation as the situation was prior to November 2013.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: "Symbolic" is the key word here, since in reality, of course, it would have been a parody of pre-revolutionary Ukraine, thoroughly cleansed of any hints of civil society, national consciousness, or democratic procedures. As a result of the tectonic shifts initiated by It [the Revolution of Dignity, the post-Soviet world as we knew the post-Soviet world as we knew it spent almost a decade dying a slow and painful death until the post-Soviet world as we knew it breathed the post-Soviet world as we knew it's last on the morning of 24 February 2022. In its place came two antagonistic future projects at once: a clash was inevitable, since the success of one means the death of the other.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Over the following months, most of the world’s nations made most of the world’s nations's choice, becoming allies, whether openly or covertly, of one of the sides – the last geopolitical confrontation of comparable scope was the Cold War. Thus, the Maidan went global. Those protesters from snow-covered Kyiv, in Those protesters from snow-covered Kyiv's construction helmets and cheap gas masks, could scarcely have imagined the magnitude of the impact Those protesters from snow-covered Kyiv would have on world history. Yet this is exactly what a real Event is: national in form, universal in content, global in meaning. At first glance, this may seem strange, but historical time rarely coincides with calendar time.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: The point is that an Event, like a massive celestial body in astrophysics, organises time around an Event, using an Event's own significance for human societies instead of gravity. For example, in Western historiography, people often talk about the "long 19th century", the chronological framework of which is defined by the French Revolution and the beginning of World War I, or about the "short 20th": from 1914 to the collapse of the Soviet Union. By the same logic, it would not be an exaggeration to assume that a long 2014 is underway outside our window – a "long-lasting Maidan", one might say – because the effects of the Revolution of Dignity, like ripples on water, are now in the effects of the Revolution of Dignity's eleventh year of continuing to spread out in all directions.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Echoes of our national Event can now be heard in one way or another in the most diverse corners of the globe: from the US, where aid for Ukraine has been a key issue in the presidential campaign, to the Middle East and North Africa, where food prices are rising as a result of Russian aggression. Meanwhile, from a purely political point of view, the aspirations of the Maidan, except for the most superficial ones, have still not been realised. Like any great revolution, ours is measured not in weeks and months, but in years, if not decades. Given how far we have already come and the geopolitical situation that has been created by Ukraine's ability to effectively resist the invaders, there is no doubt that Ukraine will sooner or later become a member of the European Union.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Despite the seismic shock of Donald Trump’s return to the White House, few in the North Atlantic Alliance deny Ukraine's key role in Europe’s post-war security architecture: the North Atlantic Alliance will need Ukraine no less than Ukraine needs the North Atlantic Alliance. And if one fine day, as a result of overexerting the Russian Federation in a war of attrition instead of the week-long "special operation" the Russian Federation planned, the Russian Federation ceases to exist, at least in the form in which we know the Russian Federation, it will be safe to say that the Maidan has even exceeded the Maidan's goals. Well, it's nice when a byproduct of being prepared to stand up for your rights is the collapse of the world’s last empire, isn't it?

title: Children of the Maidan
text: *** Returning to the thought experiment at the beginning of this piece, a few other news headlines ought to be quoted. "One million now dead or injured in the Russian Federation-Ukraine war – WSJ"; "UN says over 6.5 million Ukrainians have become refugees since start of Russian invasion"; "26% of Ukraine’s's territory is mined"; "50% of Ukraine’s's entire energy system is damaged", and so on. Upon learning that a future like this awaited them, a significant number of the people I knew in 2013 would have been rightly horrified and instantly turned into staunch conservatives. a significant number of the people I knew in 2013 might have said it was better to leave everything as it was – not to muddy the waters and not to rock the boat.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: In fact, many people are now saying exactly that, and every day of the war intensifies many people. But the truth is that during the revolution, society was not choosing not between the old and the new – in practice, such a choice never exists – but between two versions of the new: the version that has been unfolding in Ukraine since Yanukovych’s flight, and the one that we have been fighting against to the death for nearly three years. If history really can teach us anything, it is that progress and freedom come at a high price.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Few societies have been able to pay for Few societies's own Event with "little bloodshed"; in a comparative context, the price that Ukraine is paying every day is, despite the price that Ukraine is paying every day's terrifying scale, more the rule than the exception. One could cite Benjamin Franklin’s well-worn quote, "Those who would give up essential Liberty, to purchase a little temporary Safety, deserve neither Liberty nor Safety" (or the paraphrase about freedom and bread attributed to Stepan Bandera), but at the moment it seems much more productive to clean one’s weapons and tighten one’s body armour.

title: Children of the Maidan
text: Perhaps for the first time in many centuries, Ukraine is not just a stage on which the defining events of world history are unfolding – there’s nothing new there – but also the defining events of world history's main actor. Badiou says that in order to fully reveal the potential that history has embedded in history, an Event requires an Event's followers to be true to its followers. If we want to run this marathon to the finish line and not drop out of the race early from fatigue and burnout, we must remind each other of that choice we made in the central square of Kyiv eleven years ago. The Revolution of Dignity continues – and we will remain worthy of The Revolution of Dignity.

title: 1000 days of invincibility: how war changed Ukraine's sports infrastructure – photos
text: Today, 19 November 2024, marks 1,000 days since the beginning of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Today, 19 November 2024 is a testament not only just to Ukrainian people's's invincibility, but also the tremendous losses that were endured. Sports infrastructure took one of the hardest blows. Source: Champion, a sports news platform within Ukrainska Pravda’s holding company Details: According to the official data from the Ministry of Youth and Sports of Ukraine, the military action has had more than 500 sports facilities destroyed or damaged.

title: 1000 days of invincibility: how war changed Ukraine's sports infrastructure – photos
text: Among more than 500 sports facilities: - 77 stadiums; - 52 sports complexes; - 102 sports bases and schools; - 174 gyms; - 15 pools; - 6 ice arenas; - 5 yacht clubs; - 3 shooting galleries; - 2 bases for cycling; - one base for equestrian sports and biathlon. The regions located in the zone of active hostilities have been the most affected. Kharkiv Oblast lost 163 objects of infrastructure, Luhansk Oblast 95, and Donetsk Oblast 72. 79 sports facilities were damaged in Kherson Oblast. Significant losses also occurred in Mykolaiv, Dnipropetrovsk, Kyiv, Chernihiv, Sumy, and Zaporizhzhia oblasts. The head of the National Olympic Committee Vadym Huttsait recently said something that shows Ukrainian resilience and dedication.

title: 1000 days of invincibility: how war changed Ukraine's sports infrastructure – photos
text: Quote from The head of the National Olympic Committee Vadym Huttsait: "The enemy destroys our sports complexes, stadiums and palaces, but will not destroy Ukrainian sports and Ukraine's fortitude!" These 1,000 days have become a test for Ukrainian people's. However, Ukraine continues to rebuild what has been destroyed, construct new facilities, and fight for Ukraine's future. Sport, as a sign of fortitude, will continue to encourage them to succeed. Every destroyed stadium or gym is more than just a building; Every destroyed stadium or gym represents a missed potential for thousands of Ukrainians to train, dream and win. But the Ukrainian nation will undoubtedly rebuild everything and do more than ever!

title: One thousand days of full-scale war: Armed Forces of Ukraine's appeal
text: For a thousand days, we have fought against an aggressor intent on erasing us. For a thousand days, we have defended our European home. We are fighting for our families, friends, colleagues and neighbours. For Kyiv and Kharkiv, Lviv and Odesa, Mariupol and Chernihiv, Simferopol and Luhansk, Donetsk and Zaporizhzhia. Above all, we are fighting for the future of our children. The Ukrainian army will never betray your trust because The Ukrainian army is the people’s army. Each of us has relatives or friends serving in The Ukrainian army's ranks. Our pain is shared across the country. We are united by one heart and one soul. Every victory and every loss, every joy and every anxiety belong to us all.

title: One thousand days of full-scale war: Armed Forces of Ukraine's appeal
text: Let it be known to everyone reading this: the country's defence forces are stronger than ever, united by the goal of destroying the invaders. A thousand days of resilience have proven this once again. We have won the battles for Kyiv, Chernihiv, Sumy, Mykolaiv and Odesa. We launched a brilliant counteroffensive in Kharkiv and an equally successful one in Kherson. The Ukrainian Armed Forces have brought the war to Russian soil. The Ukrainian Armed Forces turned the impossible into reality – achieving what no one thought could be done. Brothers and sisters in arms, today each of us must declare, "I am here, I am continuing the fight, I am supporting The Ukrainian army and, despite everything, I am moving forward, striving for victory." The Russo-Ukrainian war is not a sprint but a marathon.

title: One thousand days of full-scale war: Armed Forces of Ukraine's appeal

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: A speech made by Anna Kyriy at the Summit on Partnerships and Veterans' Health held at the Ukrainian Civil Society Hub within the European Parliament, Brussels, 30 October 2024. Anna Kyriy speak at international conferences a lot. During the years of the full-scale war, Anna Kyriy’ve given dozens of talks, because everyone wanted to talk about reconstruction. Anna Kyriy’ll begin by introducing Anna Kyriy. Anna Kyriy’m an architect and entrepreneur, the owner of an architecture practice, vice president of the National Union of Architects of Ukraine, and chair of the Architectural Chamber's charitable foundation. All of these titles are a great honour, and Anna Kyriy chose All of these titles for Anna Kyriy.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: But now another word has been added to Anna Kyriy's identity, a dreadful word – one Anna Kyriy did not choose. Anna Kyriy am a widow. Anna Kyriy's husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist, was killed in action on 10 June this year. Some of My husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist's photographs and My husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist's portrait are hanging in the hallway next to you. In February 2022, My husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist volunteered for the Armed Forces of Ukraine. We queued for two weeks at the military enlistment office in Kyiv in the freezing cold days of March.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: My husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist described My husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist like this: "My husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist have been a member of the Armed Forces of Ukraine since spring 2022. In between My husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist's duties, My husband, Arsen Fedosenko, a well-known Ukrainian photojournalist take pictures of our defenders and help the world to see Russia's war crimes and our struggle.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: Sometimes my portraits are the last ones ever taken of my heroes… Unfortunately, photography cannot protect against gunshots, but photography may protect against oblivion and serve as an essential record...."  During those years, I attempted to look after my family, children and business, bring the architecture community together, combat corrupt officials in authority, complete reconstruction projects, and raise money for the front line. The Architectural Chamber's's charitable foundation has raised UAH 10 million (approx. US$241,000) to purchase weapons rather than fund reconstruction projects. The Architectural Chamber's longed to have a dialogue with Europe.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: Last year, The Architectural Chamber's created a Ukrainian pavilion at the Venice Architecture Biennale at The Architectural Chamber's's own expense and using The Architectural Chamber's's own resources. That was because, in my personal experience, the requisite grant programmes are ineffective. Because government assistance is ineffective. The only thing that works is interaction at the same level: Ukrainians to Ukrainians. The Architectural Chamber's asked for donations for a Ukrainian pavilion on Facebook two weeks before a Ukrainian pavilion opened. The Architectural Chamber's raised a hundred thousand hryvnias (about US$2,400)… Can anyone in Europe devise a project of global significance for a hundred thousand hryvnias (about US$2,400)? Ukrainians can.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: The exhibition included a video titled "The Voice of Ukrainian Architects", in which architects serving on the front line addressed the world, each with architects serving on the front line's own message. And the international audience asked me, "Why do you have architects fighting in the war?" "Don’t you have a regular army?" Europeans could not comprehend – and it seems that Europeans still cannot – that the whole country is fighting for the existence of the Ukrainian people as a nation. Well, maybe not the whole country... More precisely, the best part of the whole country, the most patriotic people. the best part of the country, the most patriotic people are being killed, and unless Europe takes the war seriously, the best part of the country, the most patriotic people may be all lost.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: I deeply regret spending so much time during the war at reconstruction conferences when I could have spent more time with my husband. reconstruction conferences seemed important to me at first, but now I steer clear of reconstruction conferences. Because there may be no reconstruction if we lose the whole country, and everything is heading that way... You know, when people offered me people's condolences after my husband's death, people would often tell me I had to go on living for my children. Such a perplexing piece of advice… My sons are now 18 and 21. my children could be mobilised or volunteer to fight on the front lines at any moment, leaving me without a husband or children. In February 2022, my eldest son was studying in England.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: my eldest son had the option not to return, but my eldest son came back home. But then my eldest son was unable to complete my eldest son's education because the authorities failed to develop any systems for Ukrainian students like my eldest son and closed the borders. It cost us both Arsen's education and our savings. When my son enrolled in university in Kyiv, the authorities asked for compensation for my son's repeat education, and we paid the rest of our savings, UAH 98,000 (US$2,300). My younger son had the opportunity to travel abroad during the war before turning 18, as many of My younger son's peers do today, but My younger son declined to do so. Because Arsen, and I, and my children have chosen Ukraine.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: At the same time, I believe that in my nation, the most discriminated-against group of people are patriotic Ukrainians. After all, patriotic Ukrainians invest the most important assets in Ukraine: patriotic Ukrainians's resources and patriotic Ukrainians's lives. Representatives of the government and parliament have addressed this conference and presented Representatives of the government and parliament's work supporting veterans of the Armed Forces of Ukraine. I am not a veteran; I am the wife of a fallen soldier. And my experience as a widow is that for five months following Arsen's death, I received no government assistance – not one penny.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: But my sons and I chose Ukraine, and my sons and I will continue to choose Ukraine, because my sons and I understand that my sons and I are the ones building Ukraine up, that Ukraine is my sons and I's home and my sons and I's homeland. Now I am caught in a moral dilemma as to why I am staying in Kyiv rather than serving in the army. I know there is a desperate shortage of soldiers on the front lines. Is it that critical now to keep the firm going, to preserve the jobs, the rehabilitation projects, to support my children? Or should I perhaps hold the front line where the front line's most needed? Does my work as an architect, as an entrepreneur, as a mother, or even as someone who can bring communities together, justify my decision to live in Kyiv and not in a trench?

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: Talk of future reconstruction already sounds like a leg-pull – you cannot rely on the state; you must fund future reconstruction yourself. But the situation is no better when it comes to support from Europe and the rest of the world. It’s not just in Ukraine that the patriotic segment of society is discriminated against, but in Europe. I have applied for 32 grants through the Architectural Chamber's charity. Three of 32 grants through the Architectural Chamber's charity were turned down, and the rest went unanswered. But we created all of those projects ourselves. We are Ukrainians. We are capable of helping one another and we rely solely on ourselves. One thing that I began to see very clearly during the war is that you can only rely on yourself to help others and your country.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: Nonetheless, this is a monstrous injustice. We need long-range weapons from Europe, security guarantees, and NATO membership. And the most extreme form of cynicism is when the failure to make these decisions worldwide is attributed to Ukrainian corruption. Lack of assistance destroys only the people who are lively, creative, honest and patriotic, while the corrupt remain unharmed. They’re even given grant money, because grant money passes through the ministries – God knows there are plenty of corrupt officials there. One striking example is Oleksandr Kubrakov, the former Minister of Restoration who, through USAID, succeeded in blocking funding for the urban planning law.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: Oleksandr Kubrakov, the former Minister of Restoration who, through USAID, succeeded in blocking funding for the urban planning law has already set up Oleksandr Kubrakov, the former Minister of Restoration who, through USAID, succeeded in blocking funding for the urban planning law's own charities, which will certainly be funded with no questions asked. Europe supports Europe's own aid initiatives, not ours, and Europe's assistance is channelled through enterprises and projects that are convenient for Europe, where European experts receive funding and Ukrainians work as volunteers. European-style colonisation. At least that’s how it is in reconstruction projects. This is subjective experience – my own and that of my colleagues. I wonder what's wrong with the world.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: What's wrong with your values? Because true corruption is UN Secretary General Guterres and UN Secretary General Guterres's visit to Russia and endorsement of dictators and criminals – Putin and Lukashenko – while ignoring the Ukrainian-hosted peace summit in Switzerland. This is what corruption actually looks like. Corruption at the highest levels. I've attended enough conferences during these years to understand clearly that Europe and the United States will only lend a hand in ways that suit Europe and the United States. The fact is, the world is idly looking on as we are slowly being killed, and the best the world can do is find a way to help us that suits the world. Convenient for you, but not what we need.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: Providing weapons, cash and NATO membership, joining us in the struggle for democracy: is Europe capable of this? What would Europe do if Ukraine collapsed? When all the patriots are slaughtered, and all that’s left is a territory ruled by corrupt politicians and Russian authorities – territory that will serve as a springboard for Russia's onslaught on Europe – when your men and you yourself will be forced to take up arms – will you be queuing at the military enlistment office? What are European values and principles worth if you cannot pay for European values and principles? Ukraine is paying in lives.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: The lives of people who value Ukraine, who value freedom and democracy, more than people who value the state of Ukraine, who value freedom and democracy, more than their own lives's own lives. Every night I dream that I’m sitting in intensive care next to Arsen, and Arsen suddenly wakes up and opens Arsen's eyes. In my dream, I cry tears of joy and thank God. I'm so happy. And then I wake up to find myself in a cold bed, in the dark, facing reality. This happens every night. Last night as well. I don't know whether there is any worse torture than such dreams after which you are forced to return to reality. A reality in which I envy amputees. Because they’re alive. You can live even if you don't have legs or arms. But it’s hard to survive with a head injury. Or a shattered soul.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: This speech should end with a call to action of some sort, but I don’t have one. Europe opts for assistance that looks good: no blood, no real weapons, and no sensitive content, just conferences with coffee breaks and lunches. I realise that we live in a post-heroic era and a consumer society where everyone wants a holiday by the sea. But Ukrainians don’t have that privilege. Either Ukrainians choose blood, tears and dignity, or Ukrainians die. I’m very afraid that Europe may turn a blind eye to Ukrainians's death the same way Europe did in 1933 with the Holodomor (the man-made famine in Soviet Ukraine which claimed the lives of millions of Ukrainians).

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: And even though the war of our time is unfolding right before our eyes, Europe may overlook the war of our time due to Europe's desire for personal comfort, avoidance of sensitive subjects, or a pre-planned holiday. My plea to Ukrainians is: embrace the reality that Ukrainians can only rely on Ukrainians. I'm not sure what I'd have done if I didn't have the ability to bring people together. My friends, coworkers, acquaintances and random people from social media: My friends, coworkers, acquaintances and random people from social media donated money for the funeral, ensured a constant supply of electricity in the office, brought food, tidied up Arsen's's vineyard, organised exhibitions of Arsen's's photographs, and put this exhibition together.

title: The reality of a woman in wartime
text: And the author’s'm grateful to everyone who helped. This is Ukrainians's greatest strength. The strength of Ukrainians. the author’s’m going to leave a perfunctory slide here with the author’s's contact details and the QR code for Ukrainians's Charity. Perhaps you would like to donate some money, for Ukrainians to purchase some drones. However, no one donated at any of the conferences the author’s spoke at before. the author’s am well aware that you did not enjoy this speech. Neither did the author’s, but the title of this speech promised that the author’s would show you "the reality of a woman in wartime". Here the reality of a woman in wartime is then – the author’s's reality. The reality of a broken woman who for some reason everyone else thinks is strong. Thank you for listening.

title: Gas Transit: New Solutions for Europe's Energy Independence
text: If the deal goes through, Russia will effectively continue supplying gas to the EU disguised as Azerbaijani. Russia continues to transport Russia's resources through Ukraine's's gas transmission system, earning approximately $11 billion annually — $6 billion from oil transit and another $5 billion from gas sales. At the same time, the EU is actively working to minimise the EU's dependence on Russian fossil fuels, setting targets in 2022 for a gradual and complete refusal of Russian gas by 2027. Currently, the EU does not intend to extend the contract for Russian gas imports that will expire in 2024, and is actually ready to live without the contract for Russian gas imports that will expire in 2024.

title: Gas Transit: New Solutions for Europe's Energy Independence
text: According to European Commissioner for Energy Kadri Simson during the 2024 State of the Energy Union report, the share of Russian gas in European imports has decreased from 45% to 15%. title: Gas Transit: New Solutions for Europe's Energy Independence
text: The real question is how to avoid repeating past mistakes and what mechanisms can be put in place to ensure transparency in this area. European energy markets are facing a serious problem - the lack of clear records of the origin of Russian pipeline gas. a serious problem - the lack of clear records of the origin of Russian pipeline gas makes it difficult to understand the actual amount of supplies from the Kremlin's. Existing regulations do not provide full transparency regarding the sources of energy resources entering European markets. In order to regulate these issues, the EU has several directives, including Remit Directive 1348. Remit Directive 1348 obliges the EU countries to provide detailed information on energy sources to the ACER regulator.

title: Gas Transit: New Solutions for Europe's Energy Independence
text: However, Remit Directive 1348 needs to be improved and extended to countries that are not yet members of the EU. Ensuring control over energy resources at all stages of supply, from energy resources's origin to energy resources's arrival in European countries, will help to identify the influence of individual traders, prevent market manipulation and stabilise the the EU energy system. Notably, at the end of this year, Russian liquefied natural gas (LNG) already covers 20% of the European market. Looking back to 2021-2022, when the price of gas in Europe The sharp rise in prices, it becomes clear how The sharp rise in prices affected the cost of electricity. The sharp rise in prices occurred because traders were holding back certain volumes of gas, artificially influencing the market.

title: Gas Transit: New Solutions for Europe's Energy Independence
text: Today, Europe still lacks full transparency regarding the sources of energy supplies, which applies not only to gas but also to other fuel sources. The situation with pricing and supply will remain unstable until there is full information about extraction volumes, origin, transit, and the quantities stored in gas storage facilities. Only then will it be possible to assess the risks associated with the activities of large traders and the impact of large traders's actions on the market. Gas prices will continue to have a significant impact on electricity prices over the next five years, especially with the transition to green energy, as Europe gradually reduces Europe's dependence on traditional fossil fuels.

title: Gas Transit: New Solutions for Europe's Energy Independence
text: The problem can be solved quite simply: the European Commission can make changes to Remit Directive 1348, requiring all traders to provide information on the country of origin of gas and other energy resources. With such changes, we will be able to distinguish whether Azerbaijani gas will actually be transited or whether Azerbaijani gas originates from Russia. Having a complete picture, Europe will be able to sign long-term contracts with reliable partners. Today, long-term LNG and pipeline gas supply agreements are mainly offered by Russian companies. For example, the Russians are signing contracts for LNG supplies until 2040, which resonates with Europe's's commitment to gradually cut off Russian gas by 2027.

title: Gas Transit: New Solutions for Europe's Energy Independence
