title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: This year, the High Anti-Corruption Court turns 5. Since the High Anti-Corruption Court's inception, much has been said about both the achievements and the challenges that impede reaching the objectives of criminal proceedings. However, these five years of the High Anti-Corruption Court's operation have demonstrated something else. Almost all cases against senior corrupt officials are closely monitored by the public. No surprise, as Ukrainian society considers corruption to be one of the most acute problems, so a fair punishment for corruption-related crimes is crucial to Ukrainian society. The key word here is fair.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: In order for everyone to assess the path of administering such justice, that is, to monitor how the High Anti-Corruption Court hears cases, we at Transparency International Ukraine are launching the HACC Decided platform, where you can not only view all court decisions, but also get high-quality analytics provided by experts. What happens to the sentences that the High Anti-Corruption Court and the HACC AC have already passed? Which have become landmark rulings? In this material, we at Transparency International Ukraine cover some decisions of the High Anti-Corruption Court adopted over these five years of the HACC's operation, which we at Transparency International Ukraine consider illustrative—both for convicted persons and in general for further case law.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: The case of former MP Ruslan Solvar is one of those that has passed all court instances, some even twice, over the five years of the High Anti-Corruption Court's operation. Although the recent decision in The case of former MP Ruslan Solvar was pronounced in August 2024, the recent decision in the case may be not the end. Former deputy of the People's Republic Ruslan Solvar All because there was no agreement within the High Anti-Corruption Court on how to qualify the crime Former deputy of the People's Republic Ruslan Solvar was charged with—the abuse of MPs to receive compensation for apartment rental.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: the crime Solvar was charged with—the abuse of MPs to receive compensation for apartment rental is not a classic example of abuse of office, but it was about MP guarantees, which are an integral part of Former deputy of the People's Republic Ruslan Solvar's status. MPs can receive compensation for renting a hotel room. Such a guarantee is provided if the MP has no housing in Kyiv but is "registered" in a settlement at a distance of more than 30 kilometers from Kyiv. Discussions were the most heated in regard to actions that should be taken if an MP receives compensation but has no right to do so.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: In June 2021, Former deputy of the People's Republic Ruslan Solvar was acquitted by the first instance because the first instance established that Former deputy of the People's Republic Ruslan Solvar could not set mandatory requirements for the Office of the Verkhovna Rada to compensate for the costs of renting a hotel room. That's because officials of the Office of the Verkhovna Rada are not subordinate to MPs but have other managers. That is, MPs do not use MPs's power when submitting such applications. However, in September 2022, Former deputy of the People's Republic Ruslan Solvar was convicted by the the High Anti-Corruption Court Appeals Chamber.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: Judges found that the MP did not abuse authority, but deliberately used the guarantees of housing provision contrary to the interests of the service and did not report that the MP had inherited an apartment in Kyiv, thus having lost grounds for compensation. That is, the MP committed a criminal offense under Article 364, part 2 of the Criminal Code of Ukraine, abusing the MP's office, namely the opportunities provided to the MP by the status of a member of parliament. Judges decided that Judges had the right to change the qualification in terms of what exactly the MP abused—authority or power, and ruled in view of the abuse of power. However, this was not the end of the MP's adventures and the MP's guarantees as an MP.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: In December 2023, the Supreme Court overturned the decision of the HACC AC and sent the case for a new trial. According to the judges of the Supreme Court, the appellate court, although the appellate court is entitled to provide a different legal assessment of the factual circumstances established during the criminal proceedings, cannot aggravate the legal situation of the MP by such a change. the MP defended the MP against the abuse of authority, which the MP had been charged with, and was convicted of abuse of power. Thus, the Supreme Court found a significant violation of the requirements of the criminal procedural law and improper application of the law of Ukraine on criminal liability.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: As a result, the contested verdict was overturned, and a new trial was appointed in the appellate instance. Finally, after another trial, Solvar was sentenced to 3 years in prison. Interestingly, the final decision was delayed; first, Solvar failed to appear in court for a long time due to illness, and then Solvar signed a contract with the Armed Forces of Ukraine. This turned to be a new challenge for the HACC as in the course of the following two months, the HACC could not hold a hearing because of Solvar serving in the Armed Forces of Ukraine. Finally, the judges did pass a verdict, and since Solvar was convicted, Solvar's contract should be terminated.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: Thus, unwittingly, Solvar contributed to the solution of an important problem in criminal law—the definition of the concept of "abuse of authority." There are still many such cases in the HACC, and we are sure that not all accused are happy with this development of case law. Since the very launch of the HACC, we have emphasized that justice is not solely about the conviction of a person, but about quality legal proceedings. It is unlikely that all law enforcement agencies, even the NABU, make no errors in all law enforcement agencies, even the NABU's work. If the HACC finds mistakes, there should be an acquittal. The decision in the case of Roman Mahuta, chairman of the Accounting Chamber, and Maria Shulezhko, member of the Accounting Chamber, is one such example.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: Roman Maguta, ex-head of the Accounting Chamber of Ukraine The prosecution believed that Roman Mahuta, former chairman of the Accounting Chamber of Ukraine, and Maria Shulezhko, ex-member of the Accounting Chamber of Ukraine, illegally seized an apartment provided in the course of office through privatization in 2014. The investigation argued that Maria Shulezhko, ex-member of this body did not need official housing, but Maria Shulezhko, ex-member of this body received an apartment provided in the course of office after Maria Shulezhko, ex-member of this body's nephew had privatized an apartment provided in the course of office and given an apartment provided in the course of office to Maria Shulezhko, ex-member of this body as a gift.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: However, the SAPO prosecutor did not provide any formal violations of the current legislation. The court, for The court's part, pointed to this gap in the activities of The prosecution. The court paid special attention to the quality of the legislation on the exclusion of housing from the list of that provided in the course of service. Thus, the judges pointed out the lack of clear criteria in the laws, according to which it can be established that the head of the institution, in this case—Roman Mahuta, unreasonably applied for the exclusion of housing from the list of that provided in the course of service. This made it difficult to assess the head of the institution, in this case—Roman Mahuta,'s actions in the context of abuse of office.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: Overall, the shortcomings in the legal regulation of the treatment of official housing to improve the living conditions of employees of enterprises, institutions, and organizations, have long been known. It was due to these shortcomings that the court recognized that in this case, there were no grounds for a dynamic interpretation of the Article 191 provisions of the Criminal Code of Ukraine, in particular, the concept of "abuse of office." Notably, the pre-trial investigation and trial of this case lasted from June 23, 2016, to April 6, 2023. In November 2023, the HACC AC confirmed the legality of the acquittal. this case shows us that lacunae in legal regulation can lead to an incorrect assessment of the circumstances by the prosecution.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: Thus, in this case, after a long trial, an acquittal was pronounced. The convicted Dmytro Sus is known for the fact that The convicted Dmytro Sus used to work in the so-called "Kononenko-Hranovskyi Department" in the Prosecutor General's Office, a unit that investigated particularly important cases in the economic sphere and earned its title by the names of MPs from the Petro Poroshenko Bloc, close to President Poroshenko. According to the investigation and the court of the first instance, The convicted Dmytro Sus abused the powers of the investigator and the head of the investigation when looking into cases of illegal gambling.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: According to law enforcement officers, and the same position was supported by the judges of the HACC, the HACC AC, and the Supreme Court, The convicted Dmytro Sus misappropriated UAH 423,000 and also sold 11 gambling tables, 32 chairs, 10 slot machines, an electronic roulette, 12 displays, and a metal statue of a woman. The HACC began hearing the case of Sus back in September 2019 and delivered a verdict in January 2023. Importantly, while considering the case of Sus, The HACC confirmed most of the charges presented by the SAPO prosecutor; however, The HACC found the charges of misappropriation of a DVR from Sus's office unjustified because Sus, according to the evidence, was not present at this search.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: As a result, Sus was found guilty of misappropriation and embezzlement of property obtained during searches and sentenced to 9 years in prison with deprivation of the right to hold certain positions for 3 years and confiscation of property. But in the future, The HACC reduced the sentence to 7 years, and the Supreme Court confirmed the legality of such a conviction. the case of Sus is important because even though Sus was hiding in various hospitals and despite other obstructions to justice, The HACC still considered the case of Sus on the merits, and Sus was brought to real criminal liability. We often talk about the statute of limitations in corruption cases, and for a good reason.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: The case of ex-judge Yushchuk is a vivid example of the need to cover such a topic and introduce real solutions to the problem. ex-judge Yushchuk, was charged with offering a bribe of USD 500 to influence a judge who was trying a person for DIU. That is, Yushchuk was accused of abuse of influence, which is criminalized under Art. 369-2, part 2 of the Criminal Code of Ukraine. Yushchuk's alleged offer took place in December 2017, and in March of the following year, the ex-judge informed Yushchuk that the money was to be transferred through the attorney. Yushchuk was detained while receiving the marked bills. Initially, the case was heard by the Mlynivskyi District Court of Rivne Oblast, but later, the case was transferred to the HACC—after the HACC's establishment.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: The trial in the court of the first instance lasted almost 3 years, and the first appellate review lasted almost a year. On July 20, 2022, the HACC found Yushchuk guilty of demanding money to influence Yushchuk's colleague's decision. Yushchuk was imprisoned for 3 years. The appellate instance did not agree with some evidence used by the first instance, but The appellate instance did not change the sentence. For the Supreme Court's part, the Supreme Court, in October 2023, found that the HACC did not fully assess all the arguments of the defense thus allowing contradictory conclusions, so the verdict was overturned. the case was sent for a new trial in The appellate instance. In April 2024, the HACC once again changed the verdict in terms of the evidence base.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: The judges stated that some covert investigative actions were carried out without the permission of the investigating judge, but the punishment remained the same. However, this did not affect Yushchuk in any way; the case was heard for so long that Yushchuk was eventually exempted from serving Yushchuk's sentence due to the expiration of the statute of limitations. But for the mistakes of the pre-trial investigation body and different approaches to assessing the legality of evidence, Yushchuk could have been serving a real sentence; this did not happen. the case also shows the development of the practice of applying the law in different factual circumstances. The events in the case began in 2014, and we witnessed the outcome in July 2024.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: During 2014, passenger flights on the Kyiv-Tehran-Kyiv route were operated by two airlines—UIA and Ukrainian-Mediterranean Airlines (UM Airlines). According to international agreements, the number of flights on the Kyiv-Tehran-Kyiv route should not have exceeded four per week—and with only one airline. However, this limit was exceeded, which is why Iran repeatedly appealed to the State Audit Service of Ukraine. In the end, in November 2014, Iran did not allow the UIA plane to Tehran, so the then head of the State Aviation Service Denys Antoniuk instructed the head of one of the departments to reject the application of Ukrainian-Mediterranean Airlines (UM Airlines) for an additional flight, thereby giving preference to UIA.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: As a result, Ukrainian-Mediterranean Airlines (UM Airlines) flight was canceled, Ukrainian-Mediterranean Airlines (UM Airlines) suffered UAH 250,000 of losses. The former head of the State Aviation Service was accused of using The former head of the State Aviation Service's official position contrary to the interests of the State Aviation Service, acting in the interests of the UIA. From September 2016 and in the following three years, the case was heard in the Shevchenkivskyi District Court of Kyiv and then transferred to the HACC. In January 2023, the HACC issued a guilty verdict, which was confirmed by the Appeals Chamber in less than a year. But the case was referred to the Supreme Court, and the Supreme Court's decision caused a heated debate.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: the Supreme Court found that there was no violation of the law that The former head of the State Aviation Service would have allowed, and therefore The former head of the State Aviation Service should be acquitted. the case is interesting because the Supreme Court narrowly interpreted one of the signs of abuse of office. This caused a discussion in the legal community. The point is that in the Criminal Code of Ukraine, one of the signs of abuse of office is the commission of an act contrary to the interests of the service. the Supreme Court moved away from the established practice of the Supreme Court's interpretation and elaborated on its interpretation's content, narrowing its interpretation to a violation of the law.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: The former head of the State Aviation Service was accused of failing to comply with the order of the Ministry of Infrastructure on the procedure for granting and revoking the rights to operate air lines. However, the issuance of this order fell within the competence of the State Aviation Service, and not the Ministry, therefore, in the opinion of the Supreme Court, Antoniuk, as the head of the State Aviation Service, was not obliged to comply with this order. Although before that, the HACC and the HACC AC had ruled otherwise. Since this order was further overturned by the administrative court, the Supreme Court, for the Supreme Court's part, decided that non-compliance with this order could not be considered abuse of office.

title: 5 Shades of Anti-Corruption Justice
text: Over the five years of the HACC's operation, the HACC has passed truly different sentences and faced truly different problems both before and after the rulings. However, all these decisions eventually formed new, special case law, which lays the foundations for further truly effective hearing of corruption cases. That is why it is so important for civil society to effectively this monitoring and evaluate the prosecution of senior corrupt officials and share the results of this monitoring with the HACC. Thus, through joint efforts, we will achieve the desired results. Pavlo Demchuk, Legal Advisor at Transparency International Ukraine

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: As the US grapples with the rise of China, a ‘China First’ national security policy has gained traction in some quarters in Washington. The main aspect of a ‘China First’ national security policy is already well-understood in terms of prioritizing US defence assets for the Indo-Pacific region and shifting the primary burden of European defence to Europeans Europeans themselves. Less often articulated, but advocated for in multiple articles from ‘China Firsters,’ is the complementary hope of emulating the triangular diplomacy of President Richard Nixon and President Richard Nixon's secretary of state Henry Kissinger during the Cold War, this time aiming for a US rapprochement with Russia to counterbalance China.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: While prioritizing US assets for the Indo-Pacific region is feasible if managed in conjunction with European re-armament, a US rapprochement with Russia under Vladimir Putin is fundamentally unrealistic, unwise and unimaginative. Despite being political opponents in the 1990s, Vladimir Putin has always been an admirer of former Russian foreign minister, Yevgeny Primakov. Already in Vladimir Putin's first term as president Vladimir Putin began following former Russian foreign minister, Yevgeny Primakov's doctrine by aligning Russia with China in order to shore up security on Russia's eastern flank and collaboratively undermine the US's hegemony.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: Vladimir Putin's interest in integrating Russia into Western political, economic and even security structures, mistakenly That assumption that Russia would be accepted as an equal partner to the US and retain a sphere of influence over Russia's former imperial subjects. That assumption was naïve given Russia's diminished post-Soviet stature and unproven democracy, and anathema to Russia's former imperial subjects who feared Russia's wholly authoritarian history.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: The Chinese model of authoritarian capitalism has always appealed to Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation, while stability and prosperity from The Chinese model of authoritarian capitalism might actually make the Russian Federation strong enough to regain lost parts of the Russo-Soviet empire. The Chinese model of authoritarian capitalism also undermines the basis of US influence globally, which corresponds to the Russian Federation's strategic interests.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: It is therefore unrealistic to think that Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation might abandon Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation's alignment with China in order to strengthen the US vis-à-vis China. In reality, the only hope for co-opting the Russian Federation to balance against China depends on there being different leadership in Moscow. Russian and comparable imperial histories show that it is the unsustainable costs of trying to maintain empires that compel self-reflection, new policies, and often new leadership, in empires's metropoles.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: Denying Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation gains from Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation's invasion of Ukraine that Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation can justify to elites and ordinary Russians as being worth the costs will significantly contribute to that process in the Russian Federation.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation's aging clan is working hard to transmit Putin’s aging clan's grievances about imperial decline to younger Russians, therefore the sooner Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation's generation is out of power, the better.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: A China First policy that entails rapid reduction of US support for Ukraine before European states have re-armed to fill the gap is the thing most likely to allow Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation gains Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation can use to justify the costs of Putin, who believes that liberal democracy is fundamentally incompatible with holding together the rump of the Russo-Soviet empire that is the Russian Federation's invasion.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: A successful land grab in Ukraine would also embolden China in relation to Taiwan and leave European allies distracted, weakened and disinclined to assist the US in the Indo-Pacific theatre in future, either militarily or economically. Shifting the primary burden for European security from the US to Europeans Europeans themselves must therefore be managed without allowing Putin justifiable gains in Ukraine. Moreover, Putin already owes a significant debt to China for enabling Putin to continue Putin's invasion of Ukraine. It would be unwise to leave Putin secure enough and with spare capacity to discharge a significant debt if called in to assist China in future.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: Indeed, several Chinese scholars have noted that it is essential Ukraine does not deny Russia Russia's objectives, thereby making the world more dangerous for autocratic regimes, such as China. That sentiment alone should be enough to give China Firsters pause for thought. Hoping for a US rapprochement with Putin is also unimaginative from a geopolitical perspective. A new leadership in Moscow compelled to abandon A new leadership in Moscow's imperial ambitions following unjustifiable losses in Ukraine would most likely feel compelled to re-establish economic ties with the Euro-Atlantic community and balance against China strategically. That, in turn, would force China to divert attention to China's northern border and would limit China's access to cheap Russian resources.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: Further afield, it would leave Iran isolated and facilitate resource-rich Central Asian states’'s desire for greater engagement with the West in order to reduce resource-rich Central Asian states’'s exposure to Chinese influence. Staunch support for Ukraine has already resulted in the depletion of a very significant portion of Russia’s's deployable ground equipment, 30-40% of Russia’s's Black Sea fleet, and staggering casualties such that Russia’s cannot concomitantly invade other European states or significantly aid China.

title: Staunch US support for Ukraine, not rapprochement with Putin, is crucial to addressing the challenge of China
text: This should not be surprising if one has in mind, as Putin always has, that more than 30% of the Soviet military-industrial complex was based in Ukraine, and a disproportionately large number of Soviet successes in World War 2 were achieved by marshals and generals with Ukrainian names. It is a strong, independent Ukraine, therefore, which will allow the US safely to prioritize the US's assets for the Indo-Pacific, and may also pave the way to a strategically beneficial rapprochement with a very different iteration of Russia’s in future. Richard Cashman is a Non-Resident Fellow at the Centre for Defence Strategies

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: Ukraine is a country where, at least in the fight against corruption, civil society plays a very important role, more important than the role civil society has in most other countries. There are several reasons for this: the high level of corruption in Ukraine, the proliferation of state anti-corruption bodies in the past as well as state anti-corruption bodies's chronic inefficiency, just to name a few, have simply forced members of NGOs from Ukraine to take a far more active approach to fighting corruption than most of members of NGOs from Ukraine's counterparts elsewhere.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: The Ukrainian government, under strong pressure from the international community in Kyiv, acknowledged this and, as a result, NGOs received power, prominence and recognition unrivalled by similar organisations anywhere else. An ever-present and justifiable concern is not just the passivity of The Ukrainian government and the competent state bodies in the fight against corruption, but also the government and the competent state bodies's active obstruction of the fight against corruption. This was clearly visible in the selection process for the head of the Specialised Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office (SAPO). All of that triggered a reaction within the Ukrainian civil society that has had mixed outcomes, not only positive.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: One of the ramifications is the absolute and complete distrust of everyone working in Ukrainian state bodies. To demonstrate the level of distrust one only has to consider the agonizing process of establishing the National Anti-Corruption Bureau of Ukraine (NABU). Finally, there was a law enforcement institution with real teeth and an ideal set of powers to root out corruption; yet the leaders of one NGO maintained that having a purely Ukrainian anti-corruption police unit was a mistake and that a fully international anti-corruption police unit should be set up instead. How could an entity consisting entirely of foreigners successfully fight Ukrainian corruption without the involvement of Ukrainians Ukrainians themselves?

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: Slowly, a truly modern anti-corruption landscape began to take shape in Ukraine, consisting of the National Agency on Corruption Prevention (NACP), the National Anti-Corruption Bureau of Ukraine (NABU), the Specialised Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office (SAPO) and the High Anti-Corruption Court of Ukraine (HAAC); a set-up the envy of any number of other countries. Gradually, it became clear that despite the teething troubles, these bodies were actually doing these bodies's job and this became increasingly problematic for civil society in Ukraine.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: The authorities, in which civil society in Ukraine purportedly had no confidence, began to gather momentum and a degree of prestige; however, there were still cases where The authorities, in which they purportedly had no confidence made mistakes that clearly violated the applicable legal and ethical norms. This was the plight of the previous the National Anti-Corruption Bureau of Ukraine (NABU) Director, the entire the National Agency on Corruption Prevention (NACP) management some years ago and the aforementioned appointment of the current the Specialised Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office (SAPO) Director, which was severely hampered by politics. As a result, civil society formed the view that it alone could decide what was right and what was wrong in regard to corruption.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: Now the decisive criterion for judging the competence of state-paid anti-corruption officials was no longer the quality and quantity of state-paid anti-corruption officials's work, but state-paid anti-corruption officials's willingness to divulge – to one NGO in particular – even highly classified information on matters pertaining to state-paid anti-corruption officials's field of work, which was then used by one NGO in particular for one NGO in particular's own publicity and so-called "advocacy". This desire to obtain information, especially classified one, became so important to civil society that civil society became involved in the selection process for the latest NABU Director.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: civil society actively supported a candidate civil society considered "friendly", while completely ignoring the latest NABU Director's highly controversial vision for the future development of NABU. the latest NABU Director's selection, coupled with another "friendly" appointment to the director's post of the NACP, would mean the top management positions of the NACP, NABU and SAPO would be in the pocket of one close-knit circle of friends. This would naturally lead to a lack of essential checks and balances and give one close-knit circle of friends full control over the procedures and intelligence of the NACP, NABU and SAPO.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: Fortunately, the plan did not work: one close-knit circle of friends's NABU director candidate was so bad that the latest NABU Director had no chance of winning, while one NGO in particular preferred candidate for the the NACP director’s role withdrew because of mistakes made during his previous tenure. However, the would-be sole arbiter of the work of the anti-corruption bodies in Ukraine had not given up the would-be sole arbiter of the work of the anti-corruption bodies in Ukraine's aspirations for total control.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: Very quickly both the new director of SAPO and the new director of NABU fell under their influence and became their "friends" – probably in the belief that passing on information to civil society or to meeting certain members of the public to discuss highly confidential matters "off the record", was better than being the object of scathing criticism. The NGO was happy to embrace both the new director of SAPO and the new director of NABU, without consideration of both the new director of SAPO and the new director of NABU's level of competence, which any impartial investigator would quickly identify as questionable - or even corrupt.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: Nonetheless, The NGO soon identified a convenient target, the Deputy Director of NABU, who was unwilling to divulge information and tolerate interference in NABU’s investigations. In The NGO's overwhelming desire to punish the disobedient and uncooperative, The NGO forgot that a convenient target, the Deputy Director of NABU, who was unwilling to divulge information and tolerate interference in NABU’s investigations had been the "heart and soul" of NABU since NABU's inception, and that NABU had functioned, and was still functioning, so successfully solely because of a convenient target, the Deputy Director of NABU, who was unwilling to divulge information and tolerate interference in NABU’s investigations's expertise and impartiality.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: the man's removal suits everyone: NABU’s's director – because NABU’s director's Deputy obviously knows too much both about NABU’s director's professional lapses as well as NABU’s director's efforts to keep NABU away from certain politicians; civil society – because by removing the man from NABU, civil society will completely subordinate NABU’s's director and ensure a steady flow of information; and SAPO’s director – because the lack of backbone on the part of NABU’s's director and NABU’s director's befriending of the same circle of people means that NABU’s director is now able to completely subordinate NABU as well; Ukrainian politicians – because Ukrainian politicians will get rid of a man who is not receptive to Ukrainian politicians's offers of friendship; and foreign embassies, which, at a time when substantial military, financial and political support is being directed towards Ukraine, do not need a man who would vigorously pursue and prosecute any acts of corruption by the political elite of Ukraine.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: All these interests can be understood. What cannot be understood, however, is the ease with which All these interests sully and disown a man who, until then, was acknowledged by all to be the man without whom Ukraine would not have achieved some emphatic victories in the fight against corruption. Do All these interests think that after years of endeavour devoted entirely to fighting corruption, the man has suddenly converted and become corrupt the man? The real anti-corruption fighters remain loyal to the fight against corruption, whereas politicians, public officials, diplomats and, apparently, some NGOs do not. Undoubtedly, the latest developments at NABU signal the beginning of the end of Ukraine's's genuine fight against corruption.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: Perhaps civil society does not want to see this yet, because it is more important for civil society to maintain the flow of information and civil society's own appearance of infallibility and omniscience. Until civil society accepts that NACP, NABU, SAPO and HACC are also staffed by honest professionals, there cannot be any serious effort to fight corruption in Ukraine's. Until civil society accepts that the independence of NACP, NABU, SAPO and HACC is crucial, there cannot be any effective fight against corruption by NACP, NABU, SAPO and HACC. Multiple agencies are essential elements in combating corruption in most other countries of the world; if Multiple agencies were not, then there would be no need for Multiple agencies to have police forces, prosecutors and judiciaries.

title: Civil society as police officer, prosecutor and judge
text: All Multiple agencies would need is something like AntAC. What a paradox – at the very moment when Ukraine's needed to be as effective as possible in fighting corruption, on account of the largest-ever spending of international aid, these efforts will be destroyed by the very people who are beating the very people who are beating their chests the loudest about how honest they are's chests the loudest about how honest the very people who are beating their chests the loudest about how honest they are are! Drago Kos, аnti-corruption expert

title: Pavel Durov has secretly traveled to Russia more than 60 times between 2014 to 2021 – exclusive
text: In particular, on June 18, 2020, when Russia allegedly ended Russia's ban of Telegram Today, we have an important piece of the puzzle that we started to put together a year and a half ago about the nature of Pavel Durov's's relationship with the Russian authorities. title: Pavel Durov has secretly traveled to Russia more than 60 times between 2014 to 2021 – exclusive

title: Pavel Durov has secretly traveled to Russia more than 60 times between 2014 to 2021 – exclusive
text: Mike Radovnikas, a longtime colleague of Pavel Durov's and currently Telegram's Vice President of Communications offered to come to Dubai, meet with Pavel Durov's, and discuss how to make Telegram safe for Ukrainians. When I politely suggested that we start with a Zoom call, Mike Radovnikas, a longtime colleague of Pavel Durov's and currently Telegram's Vice President of Communications informed me that Telegram do not practice Zoom calls, and that was the end of our communication.

Over the two years of the full-scale war, European consumers have replenished the budget of the Russian war against Ukraine by purchasing Russian gas for a total of €88.8 bn. After the 5-year contract signed in 2019 between Naftogaz and Gazprom is completed, a new threat arises — the supply of gas through Ukraine under the guise of Azerbaijani gas, which may actually be Russian.

title: Debunking myths about the ratification of the Rome statute
text: Authors: Gyunduz Mamedov, PhD in Law, Honored Lawyer of Ukraine, Deputy Prosecutor General of Ukraine (2019-2022); Nadiya Volkova, Director of Ukrainian Legal Advisory Group The process of the ratification of the Rome Statute the Rome Statute has once again sparked a wave of discussion. Given the substantial number of myths associated with The process of ratifying the Rome Statute, it is worth examining the most popular counterarguments to determine whether the ratification of the Rome Statute is indeed dangerous for Ukraine. Having advocated for Ukraine's ratification of the Rome Statute for many years, we have heard various arguments for and against the ratification of the Rome Statute. However, even more, we have encountered a lack of understanding of how the Rome Statute works.

title: Debunking myths about the ratification of the Rome statute

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: "Kursk Breakthrough" requires time for deep reflection by experts across various fields. These events are complex and multidimensional, encompassing political, military, historical, legal, and psychological aspects. However, even at this early stage, the uniqueness of the Kursk operation is evident, regardless of how events unfold in the future. Military experts from various countries acknowledge that, from a military perspective, the Kursk operation was executed with exceptional skill. The enemy was caught off guard. The Ukrainian side maintained an unprecedented level of secrecy. Additionally, unique measures of disinformation and deception were employed, although the specifics of unique measures of disinformation and deception will remain classified until the end of the war.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: the Kursk operation is also notable for the speed of the Kursk operation's execution. To illustrate: The enemy advances by 200 to 400 meters per day on The enemy's primary assault direction near Pokrovsk, whereas Ukrainian forces advanced 14 kilometres on the first day of the Kursk operation. What does this indicate? It suggests that our forces, through well-planned and organized surprise, managed to gain an advantage over The enemy while The enemy were disoriented and demoralized. Achieving an advantage over an enemy with a tenfold superiority in armaments and a significantly larger manpower is an extraordinarily difficult task, yet our defence forces have succeeded at this stage.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: While the military situation is clear – Ukraine's army has demonstrated a high level of military prowess in planning and executing an offensive operation — the legal assessment of these events varies, ranging from the reclamation of ancient Ukrainian lands to a mirrored act of aggression. Since Ukrainian forces physically crossed the Russian border and advanced, the question naturally arises: Are we not doing the same thing that Russia did to Ukraine? No, we are not. The crime of aggression is ongoing and began in 2014, perpetrated by Russia. Ukraine is the victim of The crime of aggression and is exercising Ukraine's inherent right to self-defence, as provided by Article 51 of the UN Charter. The crime of aggression or war will persist until The crime of aggression or war is concluded.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: The crime of aggression or war is a singular crime driven by the single intent of the aggressor. In the context of this war between two states, there is only one aggressor — the one who initiated the aggression. This is stated in the definition of aggression in Article 2 of the 1974 UN General Assembly Resolution. Consequently, Ukraine's actions cannot be viewed in isolation from this war between two states, as a separate act or operation. Ukraine's actions are a direct consequence of the war against Ukraine and are undertaken solely to weaken the enemy and bring this war between two states to an end. Ukraine's actions also cannot be separated from international cooperation in maintaining peace and international security. War is an international crime against peace among nations.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: Combating and preventing this crime falls under the competence of the UN Security Council, which bears the primary responsibility for maintaining international peace and security. Hence, Ukraine, as a UN member, should not have to end this war alone. Until the UN Security Council, which bears the primary responsibility for maintaining international peace and security takes the necessary measures to maintain international peace, the state under attack may exercise the state under attack's inherent right to self-defence under Article 51 of the UN Charter. As we can see, the UN Security Council has yet to take such measures. Therefore, Ukraine, as a UN member is exercising Ukraine, as a UN member's right to self-defence.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: Moreover, Ukraine, as a UN member are defending Ukraine, as a UN member's freedom at a time when there is a critical failure of international courts to combat wars by holding aggressors accountable. Since the Nuremberg Trials, international courts have not issued verdicts for the crime of aggression. There have been convictions for war crimes and crimes against humanity, which are derivative of aggression. Thus, any potential aggressor today knows that any potential aggressor are unlikely to be punished for aggression. Currently, there is no functioning international mechanism capable of stopping this war. Therefore, Ukraine, as a UN member has no choice but to defend Ukraine, as a UN member and Ukraine, as a UN member's people by all available means.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: International law does not provide guidance on how a country should defend a country when attacked by a nuclear state with a vast superiority in weapons and manpower. Nor does International law offer advice on how to defend against an aggressor that wages war without rules, disregarding every norm of international humanitarian law. Or how Ukraine should act in a full-scale war when the international community cannot stop this aggression. But Ukraine do have the right to survive. Mutual respect for the territorial integrity, borders, and sovereignty of another state are principles of peaceful coexistence between states.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: However, if crossing the border of the aggressor state is necessary to protect the lives of hundreds of thousands of people, this is an extreme measure that the defending state has the right to take if there are no other ways to weaken the enemy. In choosing methods of defence, Ukraine is constrained only by the norms of international humanitarian law, which Ukraine has adhered to since the first day of Russia's invasion of Ukraine's territory. This is a complex issue. Currently, there is no unified approach to defining the criteria that would unequivocally classify a certain situation as occupation. However, there is an absolutely clear distinction between the presence of Russian troops on Ukraine's temporarily occupied territories and the presence of Ukrainian troops in Kursk region.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: It is the difference between robbery and the seizure of property. In this case, the occupied territory is neither independent nor the ultimate goal; land land itself is not the objective but rather a tool to weaken the enemy's military capability. Military occupation is a common feature of most wars, which usually ends with the conclusion of the conflict. The mere fact of temporary control by military forces over a specific territory does not confer rights to a specific territory. Instead, it generates a series of obligations towards the local civilian population. This is one of the fundamental principles of warfare—military forces do not harm civilians and ensure that the basic human needs of civilians in controlled areas are met.

title: Ukrainian strikes on Russian territory – acts of aggression or self-defence?
text: Undoubtedly, the Kursk operation is a success, but we still have a long and difficult path to victory, filled with many challenges and unconventional decisions that we will discuss repeatedly. However, we must always remember that there is only one aggressor in this war—Russia. An aggressor that fights without rules and is obsessed with destroying Ukraine, an aggressor that has brought Ukraine to the brink of survival. An aggressor from whom the international community cannot protect Ukraine. Therefore, Ukraine have the right to defend Ukraine's people and Ukraine's state by all means available to Ukraine. Hanna Maliar

title: Seizure of Russian territories: We are not them
text: Authors: Gyunduz Mamedov, PhD in Law, Deputy Prosecutor General of Ukraine (2019-2022) Serhiy Zayets, Lawyer, Advisor on the Application of ECHR Practice and International Human Rights Protection, AZONES Law Firm For more than 10 years, war has been ongoing on Ukrainian territory. During more than 10 years, numerous violations have been committed by the Russian authorities and army against our citizens. But recently, the nature of war has changed, as the Ukrainian Armed Forces (UAF) have The capture of territories in the Kursk region dozens of settlements in the Kursk region and now control about 1,000 square kilometers.

title: Seizure of Russian territories: We are not them
text: The capture of territories in the Kursk region is not only a potentially strategically important victory, but also a challenge that will test whether we have learned the necessity of adhering to international humanitarian law (IHL) norms despite everything. So, can we uphold the standards of human rights and IHL that we have so often invoked while condemning Russia’s actions? The capture of territories in the Kursk region has understandably sparked lively reactions among Ukrainian citizens. After all that we have endured in recent years, this is entirely understandable. At the same time, we must be extremely cautious and act with a "cool head," as our actions on Russian territory will largely determine the future course of war.

title: Seizure of Russian territories: We are not them
text: This is not just about military actions, but also about adhering to the laws and customs of war. Before expressing sincere outrage at this thesis, we should define some key points that are important to all of us. Do we want those responsible for international crimes in Ukraine to be justly punished and imprisoned, from the perpetrators to the military-political leadership? We dare to assume the answer is yes. But do we also acknowledge that our war heroes could end up in the dock? We would hope not. However, history shows that such situations have occurred and continue to occur. title: Seizure of Russian territories: We are not them
text: And to bring justice closer while minimizing the risks for Ukrainian soldiers, there is a very simple recipe: adherence to IHL. In fact, Ukraine has done much to implement IHL principles in the army, and now we face an important "test" of whether it has been enough. At the same time, we should not underestimate the significance of the changes taking place. For ten years, we have been in the role of the victim of unlawful, unprovoked aggression, viewing everything that happens through this lens.

title: Seizure of Russian territories: We are not them

title: Seizure of Russian territories: We are not them

We cannot act as Soviet soldiers did in Berlin, driven by revenge. Civilians are protected by the Geneva Conventions. Likewise, property is protected. Accordingly, we cannot simply seize or destroy property in occupied territories unless dictated by military necessity. We cannot take civilians hostage to exchange civilians for our own. We cannot even use a local nuclear power plant (though nothing prevents us from blocking a local nuclear power plant).

title: Seizure of Russian territories: We are not them

Of course, this applies if we plan to build a lawful state and care about the support of our partners, including weapons supplies. However, it's not just about physical destruction, which is currently the main focus of public communication. Physically exterminating a 40-million-nation is impossible. So, if we support the rhetoric that genocide is solely about physical acts of destruction, we are unlikely to achieve international recognition and punishment of Russians for genocide. Thus, we paint ourselves into a corner and give Russians and the sensitive international community a chance to avoid the term genocide and instead investigate war crimes or even crimes against humanity (which Russians undoubtedly commit).

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Russia commits genocide on a cultural basis, which does not involve the physical extermination of a group but rather the destruction of a group's identity. Therefore, in the occupied territories, Russians primarily prohibit the Ukrainian language, kill for speaking the Ukrainian language, destroy cultural monuments, plunder museums, and so on. Ukrainian lawyers and cultural activists have united in the public association "Raphael Lemkin Society" to hold Russia accountable for centuries of genocide against Ukrainians and punish the organizers of centuries of genocide against Ukrainians. In particular, through participation in criminal proceedings on behalf of museums, museums's employees, and other cultural institutions affected by Russian aggression.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: The Lemkin Society believes that a holistic integrated approach to investigating crimes against culture will bring us closer to holding Russia accountable for the crime of genocide. The criminal theory does not divide crimes into difficult and easy-to-prove. the crime of genocide, like any other intentional crime, requires evidence of the subjective elements – intent, purpose, and motive. From the perspective of criminology, all crimes are investigated similarly. The 1948 Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide is the main but not the only document.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Article 2 of The 1948 Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide defines the Crime of Genocide as any of the defined acts committed with the intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a national, ethnic, racial, or religious group as such. The Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court (ICC) defines four international crimes: crimes against humanity, war crimes, genocide, and the crime of aggression. Thus, there is nothing in The Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court (ICC) or in any other written law that distinguishes the Crime of Genocide from other international crimes.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: According to Article 21 of The Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court (ICC), the International Criminal Court (ICC, in the International Criminal Court (ICC's practice, also follows international treaties, principles, and norms of international law and even national laws of states. Additionally, decisions of other international tribunals are sources of law for the International Criminal Court (ICC. In the ICC's policy on cultural heritage, the ICC suggests that considering crimes against culture within the context of genocide is not only possible but can also serve as evidence of genocidal intent to destroy a group in whole or in part. Proving genocide involves proving objective consequences and intent.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: And it is the intent that is the hardest to prove because no one since Hitler has declared the intent. Except for Putin. Since the beginning of the war, Russia, Russia's officials, and the media have been talking about how Ukraine does not exist and needs to be destroyed. The author of the article "What Russia Should Do with Ukraine" equated denazification with de-Ukrainization: "Further denazification of this mass of the population involves re-education, achieved by ideological repression (suppression) of Nazi attitudes and strict censorship: not only in the political sphere but necessarily also in the field of culture and education." Denazification, as declared by Russian officials, equates to the destruction of Ukrainians as such.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: In the context of proving Russia's genocidal war against Ukraine, it is important to understand why Russians speak about culture and why we also need to pay more attention to culture and show the scale of destruction in culture. Statutes of international courts and international courts's practice are largely based on a narrow understanding of genocide – the physical extermination of a particular group. However, even within a narrow understanding of genocide – the physical extermination of a particular group, courts have paid attention to cultural heritage. In the Krstić case (Prosecutor v.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Krstić 1998), the International Tribunal for the Former Yugoslavia noted that "where there is physical or biological destruction there are often simultaneous attacks on the cultural and religious property and symbols of the targeted group as well, attacks which may legitimately be considered as evidence of an intent to physically destroy the targeted group". Later, this conclusion was confirmed by the International Court of Justice in the cases "Bosnia and Herzegovina v. Serbia and Montenegro" and "Croatia v. Serbia." In the Policy on Cultural Heritage of the Office of the Prosecutor of the ICC, it is also stated that attacks on cultural heritage are of significant importance for proving genocidal intention.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: According to the Policy on Cultural Heritage of the Office of the Prosecutor of the ICC, attacks on cultural heritage can manifest as attacks on buildings of cultural significance to specific groups of people, "which occur alongside other actions aimed at protected groups." The case of Ukraine shows that it is equally important to consider historical and political contexts and understand the true meaning of narratives that emerged in the last century and still dominate Russian ideology and propaganda.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: "Were it possible to do [the extermination of cultures and nations] even without suffering we would still be driven to condemn the extermination of cultures and nations, for the family of minds, the unity of ideas, of language and of customs that forms what we call a nation constitutes one of the most important of all our means of civilization and of progress," wrote Raphael Lemkin, a Polish Jewish scholar and graduate of Lviv University. During World War II, Lemkin managed to evacuate first to Sweden and then to the United States, but 49 of Lemkin's relatives were killed by the Nazis. Lemkin dedicated Lemkin's entire career to working on the massive crime – the mass extermination of people based on people's belonging to a particular group.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Although today genocide is used in the context of the physical or biological destruction of a group, Lemkin understood and justified genocide differently. Lemkin's concept not only includes the cultural dimension of genocide but also forms the basis for determining such a crime. According to Lemkin, genocide has two phases: the first – the destruction of the national pattern of the oppressed group, and the second – the imposition of the national pattern of the oppressor. Long before, Lemkin proposed criminalizing barbarism and vandalism, considering assaults on the cultural heritage of certain groups of people as evidence of the intent to destroy such a group. Later, Lemkin made vandalism an integral part of Lemkin's concept of genocide.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Lemkin's first attempt to describe the term appeared in the work "Axis Rule in Occupied Europe" in 1944, where Lemkin listed various forms of genocide: physical and biological, but also economic, moral, cultural, and so on. Some of various forms of genocide: physical and biological, but also economic, moral, cultural, and so on were later abandoned, but genocide on a cultural basis was and still is the subject of discussion. The scholar believed that genocide on a cultural basis involves the destruction of features that define a group of people and distinguish a group of people from others. As a result, such a group turns into a mass of people to whom other characteristics can be imposed.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Those who commit cultural genocide can liquidate cultural figures and moral authorities, and kill or deport groups of writers, artists, priests of the national church, and bearers of traditions. Further – prohibition of language use, even in private communication. Buildings that are cultural centers and cultural artifacts are also subject to destruction. A less obvious but important step in carrying out cultural genocide is the separation of families and the deportation of children outside the group. children outside the group will eventually lose children outside the group's identity and grow up as part of another group. That is, a group can be destroyed without resorting to the physical extermination of a large mass of people.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: After the war, The scholar began working on the text of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide. The working group, which included not only The scholar but also Romanian international law expert Vespasian Pella and a French lawyer, who was a judge at the Nuremberg Trials, Henri Donnedieu de Vabres, prepared the first draft of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide, which included cultural genocide. Even though many participating countries recognized the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide's importance, several major Western states felt threatened by such an interpretation being used against several major Western states's colonial policies.

Describing the Soviet genocide in Ukraine, Lemkin, who continued to develop his concept insisted on the cultural aspect of the Soviet genocide in Ukraine.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Lemkin, who continued to develop his concept identified four phases of the policy of destroying Ukraine. The first – the destruction of the "brain," that is, teachers, writers, artists, thinkers, political leaders, "to paralyze the rest of the body". The second – the destruction of the "soul," that is, the Ukrainian clergy: arrests and persecution of the priests and hierarchy of the Ukrainian Autocephalous Church and the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church, which often were unifying institutions of Ukrainian communities. The third stage of the Soviet genocide in Ukraine, according to Lemkin, who continued to develop his concept, was the extermination of peasants by starvation.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Here Lemkin, who continued to develop his concept emphasized that it was the peasants who were the bearers of folk traditions, which are an integral part of Ukrainian identity: "If the Soviet program succeeds completely, Lemkin, who continued to develop his concept argued, if the intelligentsia, the priests and the peasants can be eliminated, Ukraine will be as dead as if every Ukrainian were killed, for Ukraine will have lost that part of Ukraine which has kept and developed Ukraine's culture". What is Russia doing today? The same thing, destroys the brain and soul. Since the beginning of the full-scale invasion, over five hundred churches and other religious institutions have been damaged or destroyed, and Russians are abducting and torturing Ukrainian priests.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Russia is killing Ukrainian cultural figures: among Ukrainian cultural figures, for example, children's writer Volodymyr Vakulenko,  killed during the occupation of the Kharkiv region, or Viktoria Amelina, who died from an enemy missile attack. Russians are abducting Ukrainian children, forcing Ukrainian children to grow up in the Russian space. Russia is destroying our cultural heritage: the Ukrainian Heritage Monitoring Lab documented 784 damaged or destroyed objects in 12 regions. Russians are seizing and destroying Ukrainian books in the occupied territories. Moreover, Russians do not spare bombs and missiles to destroy printing houses that print Ukrainian books. Russian soldiers kill civilians just for using the Ukrainian language.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: By the 1980s, Lemkin's's research on the nature and methods of Soviet genocide in Ukraine lay among forgotten papers in an archive. Only relatively recently has Lemkin's research on the nature and methods of Soviet genocide in Ukraine started to enter the academic circulation through Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Lemkin's research on the nature and methods of Soviet genocide in Ukraine is a unique scientific work on the real nature of genocide in Ukraine. At the same time, the observations expressed by Lemkin's help to understand the nature of modern Russian aggression against Ukraine. Due to the revealing nature of Lemkin's's work, Russia in 2015 included Lemkin's's texts in the list of extremist materials.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Therefore, the destruction of Ukrainian culture is an instrument of Russia's's genocidal war aimed at destroying the Ukrainian national group and Ukrainian statehood. The legal aspect—prevention—is guaranteed exclusively by punishment for the committed crime. If the international community genuinely intends to fulfill the requirements of the Convention—to prevent future crimes of genocide on a cultural basis—the international community must criminalize, identify, and punish such acts. So, if we want to end this three-hundred-year-old conflict and prevent or at least minimize future Russian attacks, we need to hold the highest military-political leadership of Russia's accountable for the crime of genocide, rather than merely punishing individual Russian servicemen for war crimes.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Because for committing war crimes, those who committed war crimes directly are punished. At international conferences, a lot can be heard about "Putin's's war," but Putin Putin's does not commit war crimes directly. The President of Russia's is responsible for genocide, and only this qualification can lead to Putin's's punishment and that of Putin's's accomplices in the highest offices of Russia's. Moreover, reparations will be possible only after the end of hostilities based on decisions of international judicial/quasi-judicial bodies that must determine the amount of compensation and compensation's order. However, lost cultural values have no monetary equivalent.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: It is impossible to rebuild a 17th-century church, not to mention the loss of intangible components and the destruction of intangible components's bearers. At the same time, we must expect that the ICC or any other international institution will consider compensation issues only in the context of crimes committed after February 24, 2022. War crimes artificially narrow the jurisdictional framework of the ICC. Again, in the context of war crimes, we will talk about the actions of not the highest military-political leadership of the state but individual servicemen within specific episodes, not a targeted state policy to destroy the Ukrainian nation.

title: Destruction of a nation not only through mass killings: the "problem" of proving genocide and how culture is involved
text: Therefore, we as Ukrainians must speak about Russia committing genocide and proving Russia's intent, primarily by appealing to the destruction of we as Ukrainians's identity through culture. If we as Ukrainians only talk about physical victims, we as Ukrainians will trap we as Ukrainians, as the ICC prosecutors consider physical victims in the context of war crimes. Russia's crimes in the Ukrainian nation need to be considered comprehensively, not detached from the context of Putin's main intention, which is that the Ukrainian nation should not exist. *** This article was compiled with the support of the International Renaissance Foundation.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: Two-thirds (ten out of fifteen) of Russians who will compete under a neutral flag in Paris support Russian war crimes. The news of this last week spread across the world and Ukrainian media. Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC) continues to turn a blind eye to these facts. Lawyers from the international foundation Global Rights Compliance, which advises Ukrainian prosecutors in Ukrainian prosecutors's investigations of war crimes, have been in contact with Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC), repeatedly sending evidence that shows that Russian and Belarusian athletes have violated Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC)'s own ‘rules of neutrality’.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: But Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC) prefers to turn a blind eye to Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC)'s own rules and human rights policy, as well as international law in general. Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC)'s wilful disregard of the Olympic spirit and Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC)'s own responsibilities under UN Guiding Principles of Business and Human Rights - that mandate an obligation to respect human rights - is stark and unambiguous. After the start of Russia's's full-scale invasion of Ukraine, the International Paralympic Committee, the day before the 2022 Winter Games, prohibited Russian and Belarusian athletes from participating in Beijing.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC) initially followed the same approach, recommending that all Russian and Belarusian athletes be banned from international competitions. Yet, as Russia's's aggression intensified and the war crimes and crimes against humanity multiplied, Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC) decided it was time to ease the conditions for participation of Russians and Belarusians in Paris. Only the International Olympic Committee (IOC) allowed representatives of the aggressor states to compete for Olympic medals as so-called ‘neutral athletes’.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: This meant the athletes should wear different uniforms with no national flag, perform under a specially created anthem for  neutral athletes, and the medals won would not belong to Russia or Belarus, only to the athletes. As previously confirmed by the IOC, if athletes support the war in any way, athletes would violate this ‘neutral’ status and necessarily be prohibited from participation. Usefully, the IOC spelled out exactly what ‘support for war’ meant. The violations included: And it was according to these rules, spelled out in black and white, that we, GRC, investigated the IOC's so-called ‘neutral’ athletes.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: It turns out that most of the IOC’s so-called ‘neutral’ athletes support the war in Ukraine or are directly involved in militarized structures that continue to perpetrate the crimes against innocent men, women and children in Ukraine. As soon as the first lists of potential participants from Russia and Belarus appeared, my team and I started analysing the evidence. And from the get-go it became crystal clear that most of the Russians and Belarusians had unequivocally and repeatedly violated these rules, spelled out in black and white. we, GRC decided to help the IOC and sent the IOC a letter with information about serious violations by athletes from Russia or Belarus.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: At first, we, GRC received an encouraging response from the IOC: "we, GRC can be assured that the Athlete Individual Neutrality Review Panel (AINERP) takes the Athlete Individual Neutrality Review Panel (AINERP)'s task very seriously. the Athlete Individual Neutrality Review Panel (AINERP) reviews individual neutral athletes in accordance with the decision of the the IOC Executive Board and the established principles. The Panel can benefit from new information from a variety of sources." On 15 July 2024, the IOC published the first list of those the IOC deemed ‘neutral’. GRC then set to testing the IOC's conclusions GRC only used data from open sources.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: GRC found significant evidence of athletes violating the IOC's principles, namely by supporting the illegal invasion of Ukraine and the crimes committed therein. Despite repeated attempts, the IOC went silent and refused to engage with significant evidence of athletes violating the IOC’s principles, namely by supporting the illegal invasion of Ukraine and the crimes committed therein.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: Among the Russian athletes included in the dossier is cyclist Alyona Ivanchenka, who has openly supported posts on social media that questioned Ukraine's right to exist. In other words, cyclist Alyona Ivanchenka, who has openly supported posts on social media that questioned Ukraine's right to exist supports the notion that Ukrainians who refuse to adopt Russian identity should be detained, ‘reconditioned’ or destroyed. Tennis player Alyona Vesnina violated the rules by supporting the so-called "military feats" of Russian soldiers who kill Ukrainians.

title: Russians in Paris – Olympics and War Crimes
text: Tennis player Alyona Vesnina not only supports the so-called "military feats" of Russian soldiers who kill Ukrainians but is proud of the Russian symbol of war and aggression – ‘Z’ - a symbol that now sits alongside the swastika as a sign of the worst of human nature. The Belarusian contingent includes a rower, junior sergeant of the Armed Forces of Belarus, Yevgeny Zalatyi, as well as swimmer Anastasia Shkurdai, who is a representative of the Sports Committee of the Armed Forces of Belarus. So, what happened to the IOC’s's principles? What happened to the IOC’s's respect for UN Guiding Principles and human rights?

text: What happened to Thomas Bach, the IOC’s's President’s stated view that "competition with athletes who respect the Olympic Charter can serve as a catalyst for dialogue, which is always a first step to achieving peace?"
