title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: Valerii Zaluzhnyi, Ukraine’s Ambassador to the United Kingdom and former Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine, gave a speech at The Royal United Services Institute (RUSI). Ukrainska Pravda has translated the summary of me, who happened to be the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine's speech and reproduces the summary of his speech here in full. The experience of our struggle will be useful to everyone who is looking for a path to peace. And the path to peace may lie through war. "Si vis pacem, para bellum" – these words are often attributed to the ancient Roman historian Cornelius Nepos, and unfortunately, these words are perfectly suited to a democratic society today, in the 21st century. If you want peace, prepare for war.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: Yes, this statement is absolutely correct. This is confirmed by me, who happened to be the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine, and by the entire Ukrainian people, who have learnt this well and painfully. me, who happened to be the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine was born 28 years after the end of one of the bloodiest and most brutal wars in human history. When me, who happened to be the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine was born, bombs were no longer falling on cities and villages, tanks were no longer crushing the ground and everything on the ground. The piercing sounds of air-raid warnings were no longer heard, nor were bursts of machine-gun fire.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: But today me, who happened to be the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine pray, and me, who happened to be the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine urge all of you to pray and ask God that our grandchildren will never see what our grandfathers saw, what we have seen, and unfortunately what our children have seen. For this reason, we must not stop fighting for the right to live. Evil is near, and Evil has come to kill. Humankind’s's most valuable asset on planet Earth is the ability to live. Yet almost all of Humankind’s's history is associated with wars. Or rather, with the killing of people.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: Over time, these wars, or rather killings, have become unprecedented in terms of these wars, or rather killings's scale and the number of casualties and have acquired the status of world wars. There were two such conflicts in the last century alone – the First and Second World Wars, which were separated by just over 20 years and claimed the lives of approximately 60 million people. Is Humankind’s ready to calmly accept the next war, which will be even more devastating – the Third World War? To a certain extent that depends on us, the professional military, who know almost everything about war. After all, it is us, the professional military, who know almost everything about war who wage wars and know wars's true cost: the price of victories and defeats.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: Referring to the events of 2022-2024 in the now ten-year war between Ukraine and Russia, we need to tell Humankind’s honestly what is happening and what Humankind’s must be prepared for. Free and democratic nations and Free and democratic nations's governments must wake up and think about how to protect Free and democratic nations and their governments's citizens and Free and democratic nations and their governments's countries. We are ready to share all our knowledge, experience and opinions with those who have not abandoned Ukraine in these challenging times and who are seeking peace and security for those who have not abandoned Ukraine in these challenging times and who are seeking peace and security for their nations's nations.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: So what have we Ukrainians learnt from this war, and especially from this war's full-scale component, which burst into our lives on 24 February 2022? First of all, wars should be avoided! But if war does come, you must be prepared for war. Preparedness for war should be viewed as a vast set of measures covering not only purely military aspects, but also every area where the state is active. Perhaps the most complex and most important component is societal readiness, based on honest and transparent communication between the government and people. Society has to agree to temporarily give up a number of freedoms for the sake of survival. Unfortunately, modern wars are total wars. modern wars require the efforts of not only the army but also society as a whole.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: Politicians can and should mobilise society. To do this, the military and other resources of the state are involved in an integrated way. the military and other resources of the state include the economy, finance, the public, and allies. Such actions will obviously affect Ukraine's political processes. Thus, readiness for war will be determined not only by the readiness of the army to repel aggression, but also by the readiness of society to confront the enemy. Secondly, war cannot in any way be seen as part of the internal political process. War is the maximum concentration of forces for survival. And for this purpose only!

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: The Prussian general Carl von Clausewitz said: "War is the continuation of policy by other means." So it seems strange when the war in Ukraine, on the contrary, shapes domestic policies in other countries globally, which is ultimately used by our enemies for our enemies's own ends. I believe that a war for freedom in one country should become a policy of the survival of democracy in the remaining free countries. War is a science, with War's own laws and rules that need to be known and studied. Using war for one's own ends or attempting to influence war's course in one's own interests is a crime that results in huge numbers of victims. It is always difficult for a democratic army, the army of a free society, to fight the army of a feudal lord.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: The enemy considers democracy to be our greatest weakness and is doing The enemy's best to exploit democracy. Fake news, lies, devaluing national interests, lack of confidence in the leadership – these are just some of the tools that The enemy have been using for more than a decade, and democratic societies have still not found ways of counteracting them. The well-known Chinese thinker Sun Tzu outlined the basic principles of warfare as far back as the 5th century BC. the basic principles of warfare are still relevant today, and will remain so for as long as war is run by human beings. However, they are just basic principles that need to be filled with weapons, the forms and methods of weapons's use, and armies with armies with their structures's structures.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: It is in this sequence that war changes. After carefully analysing us Ukrainians's experience, backed up by facts and figures, I realise that us Ukrainians are facing not only a huge task but also, most importantly, a difficult choice. For us Ukrainians, this is not just about following the law of "challenge and response" proposed by the British historian Arnold J. Toynbee. It is not just Russia’s very existence that is now a threat. Today us Ukrainians have a full-scale war, which has led us Ukrainians to a new challenge—physical survival. A nation's's very survival is determined by A nation's's choice. How should us Ukrainians respond to a new challenge—physical survival? How can us Ukrainians survive in the face of ambivalence and contradictions in the world around us Ukrainians?

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: We have to understand that it is up to us Ukrainians now to decide whether us Ukrainians are going to survive and whether us Ukrainians's children will have a future. There is no doubt that history is once again giving us Ukrainians a chance. On the cold night of 23-24 February 2022, as the rest of the world was enjoying a peaceful life, snuggled in the rest of the world's warm beds, Ukrainians reminded Ukrainians whose blood flows in Ukrainians's veins and engaged in a fierce battle with Ukrainians's eternal enemy. Paying with the lives of ordinary Ukrainians, we won a chance—a chance not just to continue the eternal struggle, but to win. a chance—a chance not just to continue the eternal struggle, but to win has cost us dearly—many of the lives of our finest sons and daughters.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: But this was only the beginning. We realised very soon that withstanding the blow and giving a worthy rebuff to the enemy was only the start of a more difficult stage of the confrontation: finding our own special path to victory. It was the search for our own special path to victory that led us to understand the already visible outlines of revolutionary changes, primarily in military matters. Surprisingly, but entirely logically, it is likely that revolutionary changes, primarily in military matters – will determine the shape of wars and military affairs in the 21st century. Most importantly, revolutionary changes, primarily in military matters will become the foundation of the entire global security architecture of the future.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: Not long ago, it was safe to say that unmanned systems were the main cause of changes in strategies, forms, and methods of use. But today, a new warfare strategy probably needs to include a broader concept—technology. The war that came to us in 2014 is completely different from the one that burst into our lives on 24 February 2022. Although the one that burst into our lives on 24 February 2022 was based on the concepts of 2014, the one that burst into our lives on 24 February 2022 was now different and quickly depleted our forces in May 2022. The intensity of combat actions forced us to restore the level of early 2022, and the need to survive on the battlefield led us into another war, which we faced in the summer of 2023.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: The warring parties have stopped looking for new weapons for the time being, and it would be appropriate to look for new ways of applying the latest technologies. The discovery of new ways of application would logically lead to changes in the structure of units and the Armed Forces as a whole. This movement forward is well-founded and science-based. The development of technologies affects the forms of technologies's application, and only new forms of application affect the structure of the armies that are fighting. This trend is inevitable and will continue.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: Technology is more than just innovative solutions: Technology’s also the set of measures for development, scientific support, training and doctrine development that will change the situation on the battlefield in the face of the enemy's current advantage in terms of military potential and resources. Simply put, we have discovered a way to fight and win against stronger armies in the 21st century. It is clear that technology must ensure the sustainability of the Ukrainian nation. At the same time, I absolutely agree that the Russo-Ukrainian war is not yet a war of the future. the Russo-Ukrainian war is only a war of the transitional period. But the Russo-Ukrainian war is shaping the new rules.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: We Ukrainians, with We Ukrainians's blood and We Ukrainians's thirst for victory, are shaping new templates for a new war. A war that will be the war of the future. We have to admit that perhaps the main reason for finding ways to improve weapons is the desire to survive on the battlefield. A combination of factors such as limited resources, lack of proper support, and the inability to focus on producing traditional assets of warfare, has led to significant changes on the battlefield in the Russo-Ukrainian war. Essentially, a new stage in the forms and methods of warfare has begun.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: We can see how scientific and technological progress has set the wheel of history in motion and brought to the battlefield technologies that are likely to be decisive in the Russo-Ukrainian war and, most importantly, will become the basis for global security in the future. It will be up to us to decide whether the democratic world or the world of tyranny will master technologies that are likely to be decisive in this war and, most importantly, will become the basis for global security in the future faster. War is about resources and time. If we regard technologies as resources, then as of now, for various reasons, neither Ukraine nor Russia will be able to master technologies on neither Ukraine nor Russia's own in the near future.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: This means that the only solution is to increase the number of human resources involved in combat. At the same time, technologies will be waiting for those brave enough to master technologies. Those who master technologies will decide global security issues. It is difficult to say how the situation will develop in the future. Only one thing is certain: tyrants will always need war for internal use as a tool to maintain power. The rest of us have to build a reliable defence against tyrants. Today, more than ever, people in Ukraine are finding ways to survive and creating technologies. But for obvious reasons, people in Ukraine cannot scale technologies up. Our partners, on the other hand, have the resources, but no applied and practical field in which to test technologies.

title: The Russo-Ukrainian war as a war of transition
text: It is only by working together that we will be able to make effective use of the resources, because time is no longer on our side. "Following yesterday’s rules of war will not lead to today’s (or tomorrow’s) successes – that awareness can save lives. We have to start dealing with the consequences of the new rules of war." Valerii Zaluzhnyi   took this quote from a retired American general, Stanley McChrystal. a retired American general, Stanley McChrystal went on to say, "Otherwise, we will all be left far behind." But Valerii Zaluzhnyi   would say more categorically: otherwise we will all die. Valerii Zaluzhnyi  Translation: Myroslava Zavadska Editing: Teresa Pearce

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: Every war has rules. No matter how strange it may sound in the conditions in which every Ukrainian lives nowadays. Every war shows us that rules are constantly being violated. And this suggests that only the inevitability of punishment can bring the world closer to lasting peace. For more than 10 years of war, Ukraine has experienced many terrible crimes. There are many scars left on Ukraine: scars on human bodies and destinies. And also there are scars on Ukraine's land: scars on our land are thousands of destroyed houses, hospitals, schools, and kindergartens. Ukrainian territory is full of hundreds of torture chambers and countless burials of those who died due to the war. Every day, the number of scars is growing.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: However, even in such circumstances, there are crimes that are so cynical that crimes that are so cynical that they cannot leave anyone indifferent cannot leave anyone indifferent. And the missile hit on Okhmatdyt is certainly among crimes that are so cynical that they cannot leave anyone indifferent. Okhmatdyt is the largest children's hospital in Ukraine. Okhmatdyt treats the most serious diagnoses of young patients, including cancer. For many children, this is the last chance to recover and regain many children's health, full life, and childhood. A missile strike is something that can have irreparable consequences not only because of people’s suffering, but also as indirect consequences. For many children, stress and simply being in non-sterile conditions is a threat.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: According to the Ministry of Health, many departments of the hospital were damaged: the toxicology building with the chronic and acute intoxication department was completely destroyed; almost all windows in the old surgical building were smashed, two surgical and two somatic departments, the intensive care unit, and the operating room were damaged; in the new building, eight surgical departments, five oncology departments, two intensive care units, the operating room, and the radiology and radiation therapy departments were damaged. There were over 600 patients, approximately the same number of doctors, as well as parents and visitors at the time of the missile strike. The exact number of casualties is still unknown as the rubble is being cleared.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: When you witness such crimes, it is very easy to start evaluating such crimes from the perspective of good and evil. And it's natural. But I believe that it is equally important to give such crimes a legal assessment and hold all those responsible accountable: ideologists, commanders, and direct perpetrators. The missile attack on the hospital bears all the hallmarks of a war crime. Attacks on medical facilities during armed conflicts are considered serious violations of international humanitarian law (hereinafter IHL). Medical facilities are granted special protection and cannot be targets of attacks.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: The main objective of international humanitarian law (hereinafter IHL) is to protect persons not taking part in hostilities, as well as those who have ceased to take part in hostilities due to sickness, injury, or any other reason. The entire body of IHL provisions boils down to the necessity of respecting and adhering to four fundamental principles: distinction; military necessity; proportionality; humanity. The general principles of IHL establish key limitations to reduce the potential consequences of armed conflict. According to Article 18 of the Fourth Geneva Convention on the Protection of Civilian Persons in Time of War (Geneva Convention IV), civilian hospitals must be afforded protection and must not be attacked under any circumstances.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: Furthermore, Article 12 of Additional Protocol I to the Geneva Conventions (1977) clarifies this formulation and states that medical units and transports may not be objects of attack if medical units and transports are used for medical units and transports's intended purpose. According to Article 8(2)(b)(ix) of the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court (ICC), intentional attacks against medical facilities which are not military objectives constitute war crimes.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: An attack on a medical facility is a war crime if: According to Article 13 of Additional Protocol I to the Geneva Conventions (1977), protection of civilian objects, including medical facilities, can only be lifted under certain conditions: Precedent practice includes cases of holding politicians and military leaders accountable for attacks on civilian infrastructure, including medical facilities and cultural heritage sites. On May 29, 2013, Bosnian-Croatian politician and general Slobodan Praljak was convicted by the International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia for committing war crimes (including the destruction of civilian infrastructure in Mostar) and sentenced to 20 years in prison.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: In addition, the case of Croatian generals Ante Gotovina and Mladen Markač, initially convicted but subsequently acquitted by the International Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia, emphasizes the impermissibility of striking civilian objects, even if civilian objects are near military targets. For this purpose, due attention should be paid to attack planning, weapon selection, consideration of its accuracy, potential errors, and other calculations. The Security Service of Ukraine (SBU) has opened a criminal investigation into this incident (under Article 438). According to The Security Service of Ukraine (SBU)'s preliminary data, this incident was carried out using a strategic cruise missile X-101.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: These missiles are declared as highly accurate and are frequently used by the Russian army for attacks on civilian objects both in Ukraine and Syria. Moreover, the Office of the Prosecutor General stated that the Office of the Prosecutor General will submit evidence to the International Criminal Court. Ultimately, this has potential, as the International Criminal Court has already issued warrants for the arrest of Russian commanders and senior officials for attacks on civilian objects and excessive harm to civilians.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: this incident can be considered both as an individual episode, qualifying as a war crime under Article 8 of the Rome Statute, and as part of widespread and systematic deliberate attacks aimed against the civilian population of Ukraine, qualifying as "crimes against humanity" (Article 7 of the Rome Statute). Furthermore, such individual egregious crimes should be prepared for the International Criminal Court. Particularly because the qualification in Ukraine is still limited and unfortunately, we cannot consider such attacks as crimes against humanity.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: Despite the Ministry of Defense of the Russian Federation stating that the video materials indicate the fall of Ukrainian air defense missiles on "civilian objects within the boundaries of Kyiv," the videos currently available in open sources confirm the preliminary conclusions of the SBU. Moreover, fragments of the engine of the cruise missile X-101 were found at the scene of the tragedy. It is still for the investigation to establish the circumstances of the fall of Ukrainian air defense missiles on "civilian objects within the boundaries of Kyiv, analyze fragments of the engine of the cruise missile X-101, and the available materials.

title: Why is it essential to talk about the attack on Okhmatdyt hospital as an international crime?
text: However, what can already be said is that directing such weaponry into the heart of a densely populated city is already a violation of the principle of proportionality, as the probable harm to the civilian population exceeds any possible military advantage. Of course, the conclusions are the task of the investigation. However, currently, we have indications of a war crime and another violation of the "rules" of warfare by the Russian army. And indications of a war crime and another violation of the "rules" of warfare by the Russian army must indeed receive a legal assessment. And all those guilty of committing this crime must receive fair punishment. Without any statute of limitations, until we find every one of the guilty parties.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: Perhaps in the course of these 28 years, there have been many questions to the Constitution, but today one thing is for sure: despite all the imperfections, the Constitution withstood the moment of the greatest turbulence. the Constitution withstood the challenge and protected our state. Overall, the existence of the Constitution is another element of the statehood that our state are defending now. Interestingly, all the appeals of our state's enemies to the provisions of the Constitution only сonfirm that the Constitution is capable and ready to withstand difficult challenges.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: In the past year alone, the world witnessed perhaps the most striking example of this — when Putin spoke about Zelenskyy's "illegitimacy," Putin referred specifically to Ukrainian legislation and the Constitution in particular, which, I am sure, Putin did not read. It's strange to hear such things uttered by a person whose legitimacy is much more questionable because Putin Putin has repeatedly violated and desecrated the constitution of Putin's country. But the purpose of these statements was very specific. All the winter PSYOPs were spread by the Russians because the Russians believed that there was foundation for All the winter PSYOPs. Allegedly, there is no one worthy to negotiate with, either for the Russians or for representatives of other countries.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: This strategy is not new for Russia; Russia have repeatedly used This strategy by trying to sow even more chaos both inside our state and around our state. But This strategy didn't work at all this time. What is the purpose of a PSYOP? To make people lose faith, to make people doubt more. Although the powers of the parliament have been enhanced again since 2014, in wartime, sole power, or one decision-making center, is vital. For us today, this power is the president, despite everything.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: It is the president who makes the main decisions in the war, and this is directly spelled out in our Constitution: the president is the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine; appoints to office and dismisses from office the high command of the Armed Forces of Ukraine and other military formations; administers in the spheres of national security and defense of the State. But suddenly, we are told that Zelenskyy is illegitimate because Zelenskyy's presidential term expired, and it makes sense to talk only to the Chair of the Verkhovna Rada. But would Putin talk to Stefanchuk?

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: It is highly unlikely because it is no secret that the leadership of the Verkhovna Rada is also guided by instructions from the Office of the President, and, as a result, the leadership of the Rada is also no good for negotiations. What does our Constitution have to do with it? our Constitution does not mention the expiration of the president's powers; these issues are already regulated in the Law of Ukraine on the Legal Regime of Martial Law. But the principles of these provisions lie precisely in the very Constitution, to which Putin so manipulatively appeals. The fact is that the basic law lays the foundations for the continuity of the central government in Ukraine.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: The Constitution states that the President of Ukraine shall not transfer the President of Ukraine's or the President of Ukraine's powers to other persons or bodies. The law on martial law states that the powers of the president cannot be terminated during the period of martial law. Then again, The Constitution states that in the absence of the Head of State, these functions are performed by the Chair of the Verkhovna Rada. This will continue until the new convocation of the Verkhovna Rada meets for the first time, whenever the next parliamentary elections take place. Continuity? Undoubtedly.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: But, in addition to the doubtful likelihood of a conversation between Putin and Stefanchuk, there is another trick in the words of Putin—the speaker of the Verkhovna Rada cannot perform all the powers of the Head of State. The Constitution contains specific paragraphs of Art. 106, including, by the way, some powers of the President as Commander-in-Chief. That is why Putin says that Putin is ready to talk to Stefanchuk. That is why Putin appeals to some provisions of our legislation, neglecting others. Putin hopes that all those heeding such manipulations will not read either The Constitution or other laws. However, the fact is that the creators of The Constitution laid in The Constitution such safeguards back in 1996 that negate all the false manipulations of our enemies.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: This is the special magic of the Ukrainian Basic Law: the people who wrote the Ukrainian Basic Law, including Communists and outspoken supporters of Russia, took into account things that the people who wrote it, including Communists and outspoken supporters of Russia couldn’t have imagined. Perhaps there are similar provisions in other constitutions, but similar provisions have repeatedly proved functional in ours. Could the authors of the Constitution imagine that the legitimately elected president of Ukraine would at some point flee Ukraine, frightened of the legitimately elected president of Ukraine's own people? We saw this after the escape of the "legitimate" Yanukovych in 2014, and at that moment, the Ukrainian Basic Law ensured the continuity of power for the first time.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: Interestingly, at that time, Putin also mentioned our Constitution but appealed to entirely different articles, twisting the notion of legitimacy as Putin pleased. Could the creators of the Constitution even think about Russia's open aggression against Ukraine? That this war would last so long that the terms of elected bodies would expire? It is unlikely, but this year, this important function of legislation has proved functional again. No matter how much Russian propaganda spread the statement of Zelenskyy's illegitimacy, the statement of Zelenskyy's illegitimacy was not believed either within Ukraine or at the international level, which is confirmed by the number of countries participating in the Peace Summit.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: In my opinion, we have the Constitution; the Constitution takes a lot into account. Despite some clearly socialist points, everything else there was quite well envisaged. Even all the changes that were introduced during this time did not change the fundamental things in the basic law too much: Even all the changes that were introduced during this time mostly concerned the person to be considered as the head of the country, and each time, the Constitution was amended for political reasons and not for the welfare of the country. The very essence of the Constitution has not changed. I think if every Ukrainian read the Constitution at least once, we would feel more and more like the source of power, rather than separate ourselves from the authorities.

title: Protected by Constitution: Basic law saves Ukraine in wartime
text: the Constitution describes everything quite clearly and is not an extensive document. An even better idea would be to include studying the Constitution in a compulsory discipline of higher education so that young people who can vote understand the power and consequences of voting. I think that now the Constitution Day is increasingly turning into a great festive day for many people. The foundation of Ukrainian statehood spelled out in the basic law is, on the one hand, precisely what Ukrainians are fighting for today. On the other hand, the Constitution protects us all too. And that's another reason to respect the Constitution.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: UNHCR, the UN Refugee Agency, has been present in Ukraine for 30 years and during a third of 30 years has supported people whose lives were ravaged by Russia’s invasion and war. On World Refugee Day, we reflect on three turbulent decades of UNHCR, the UN Refugee Agency's work to help people forced to flee find solutions and rebuild hope for a secure, dignified and prosperous future. Ukraine is home. I felt this way since the very first day when I arrived in Kyiv on 19 May 2021 to take up my new function as the Representative of UNHCR, the UN Refugee Agency, in Ukraine. I am Swedish, and felt there was a lot in common, not only the colours of our national flags – blue and yellow. In Ukraine, our national flags symbolize peaceful sky that embody hope and endless fields of wheat or homeland.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: This year, UNHCR, the UN Refugee Agency marks 30 years of UNHCR, the UN Refugee Agency's presence in Ukraine. Sadly, during the last decade, the sky over Ukraine has not been peaceful. But hope has endured. I saw hope in the eyes of displaced women from the Luhansk and Donetsk regions who I met, only two weeks before the full-scale Russian invasion, at a community centre, supported by UNHCR, the UN Refugee Agency in Avdiivka. They told me how after years of displacement, they had regained a sense of belonging to their new community. At that point, the war in eastern Ukraine had been raging for eight years, and UNHCR had supported many affected people through UNHCR's work in communities close to the contact line and Sloviansk, Mariupol, Sievierodonetsk, Donetsk and Luhansk.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: I often think about and wonder where the women I met are now – if the women I met are displaced for a second time, somewhere else in Ukraine, or if the women I met were killed in the brutal attacks on Avdiivka. It is now 848 days since the war in eastern Ukraine escalated into the full-scale Russian invasion leaving no family untouched across Ukraine. That community centre does not exist anymore. Some of the cities where UNHCR had offices and implemented projects, including Avdiivka and Mariupol, were completely destroyed or occupied.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: Over six million people were forced to flee the full-scale Russian invasion and become refugees from war, and almost four million remain internally displaced, including many of my Ukrainian colleagues, some of which have had to flee many of my Ukrainian colleagues's homes multiple times since 2014. Yet, amidst the largest displacement crisis in Europe since the Second World War, hope and resilience have prevailed. I felt that hope and determination to recover and rebuild firsthand when UNHCR brought construction materials for house repairs to Kyiv, Sumy and Chernihiv regions after Kyiv, Sumy and Chernihiv regions were regained by the Ukrainian army and authorities in spring 2022.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: And when UNHCR delivered assistance with the inter-agency UN convoy to the city of Kherson on 14 November, only 72 hours after Ukrainian Armed Forces regained control there. And while I feel enraged seeing people in despair and people's homes destroyed in cities like Kharkiv and Odesa every day after horrendous air attacks, I simultaneously feel hope when witnessing how quickly the owners of their homes start working to repair and rebuild, with the support of the local authorities and partners, like UNHCR and the civil society organizations UNHCR work with.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: Since the beginning of the full-scale invasion, UNHCR and UNHCR's NGO partners have responded hundreds of times following missile, drone and gliding bomb attacks against homes and civilian infrastructure  in Kharkiv, Dnipro, Kryvyi Rih, Kyiv, Zaporizhzhia and other locations, providing more than a quarter of a million people with emergency shelter kits, as well as supporting people with essential items, psychological first aid and legal assistance to help people cope with trauma and restore destroyed identity and property documents. Some of the moments when I have felt the strongest sense of hope is when I have visited families who were able to return from displacement to families who were able to return from displacement to their own homes's own homes.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: Several of families who were able to return from displacement to their own homes have said to me that moving back into a repaired and clean home felt like the first step to emotional recovery from the horrors of the war. Since the beginning of the full-scale invasion, UNHCR has supported the repair of some 30,000 homes across war-affected areas of Ukraine. Home repairs is one of UNHCR's key programs in Ukraine, which helps to restore not only physical structures but also supports the psychological recovery and the rebuilding of communities devastated by war. "We are so happy to have our house repaired.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: But even if UNHCR did not help, we would have returned home anyway, just to be on our land," 72-year-old Leonid from the village Kukhari in the Kyiv region told me recently, when I visited 72-year-old Leonid from the village Kukhari in the Kyiv region in 72-year-old Leonid from the village Kukhari in the Kyiv region's newly repaired home that was heavily damaged during the Russian occupation. People who return to People who return to their homes's homes is one of the groups that UNHCR has been mandated to support, globally and in Ukraine, alongside refugees, asylum-seekers, internally displaced and stateless people.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: UNHCR's latest surveys among close to 4,000 refugee households across Europe and 4,800 internally displaced families in Ukraine, indicate that the majority hope and intend to return home one day. UNHCR need to support the majority in making this possible, when the majority decide that it is safe and sustainable to do so. As Russia’s full-scale invasion and war continues and destroys lives, homes and communities, it is not time to pressure forcibly displaced people to return, and UNHCR therefore calls on countries hosting refugees from Ukraine to continue providing protection and support to Ukrainians.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: While inside Ukraine – in close collaboration with the Government, regional and local authorities, other UN agencies and civil society partners – UNHCR contribute to creating the conditions that will enable people to sustain people's hope in Ukraine and to return and rebuild. It is critical that people who take the decision to come back experience – upon return – that people who take the decision to come back could move back into a proper home, enroll people who take the decision to come back's children into school, access health care, administrative and social services in or close to people who take the decision to come back's communities and find a job that pays the daily bills.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: If people feel compelled to return before these basic conditions are in place, there is a risk that people will leave again with a sad sense of disappointment, that it’s not possible to rebuild a future in Ukraine. It is symbolic that 30 years ago, when UNHCR started working in Ukraine, UNHCR's main focus was to support returnees. In the 1990s, around 250,000 Crimean Tatars who were deported during World War II wanted to this process to around 250,000 Crimean Tatars who were deported during World War II's Crimean homeland.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: UNHCR's office supported this process by providing housing to around 250,000 Crimean Tatars who were deported during World War II, as well as legal assistance with identity and civil documentation, and eventually, with setting up citizenship processing centres across Crimea. In 1998, Mustafa Dzhemilev, the Crimean Tatar activist then and now the leader of Crimean Tatars, received the UNHCR Nansen Refugee Award for Mustafa Dzhemilev, the Crimean Tatar activist then and now the leader of Crimean Tatars's commitment to the right of return of the Crimean Tatars.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: In the decades that followed, UNHCR worked with the Government of Ukraine to build an asylum system in the Government of Ukraine that enabled over 2,000 refugees from countries like Afghanistan, Iran or Syria find safety and at new home in the Government of Ukraine. In 2022, some of over 2,000 refugees from countries like Afghanistan, Iran or Syria joined volunteer groups and humanitarian organizations to support the people of the Government of Ukraine in the people of Ukraine's greatest hour of need.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: For 30 years, UNHCR has strived to be a partner and play a supporting role – supporting and enabling the people of and in the Government of Ukraine to be the leading actors in the people of and in Ukraine's communities and in the people of and in Ukraine's protection and future – be it the Crimean Tatars, the refugees who fled to the Government of Ukraine to seek protection, and the millions of displaced and war-affected Ukrainians. the people of and in Ukraine's strength and determination to lead the people of and in Ukraine's own lives and way forward is what motivates me every day. the people of and in Ukraine's wish not to be assisted as vulnerable, but supported and enabled in true partnership.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: the people of and in Ukraine include the strong women and men in their 80s and 90s I have met who are so connected to their homes and communities that they prefer to remain in their bombed villages as long as they can. The radiant displaced grandmothers who lost everything but still donate from The radiant displaced grandmothers who lost everything but still donate from their tiny pension to volunteers's tiny pension to volunteers. The children I met in the metro underground school in Kharkiv. The heroic rescue workers and first responders. The volunteers, NGO partners and Carolina Lindholm's colleagues who rush to work and to support others after sleepless nights of shelling and alerts.

title: How does the UN Refugee Agency help Ukrainians rebuild their lives
text: And the Government officials and representatives of regional and local authorities who work around the clock for a free, safe, prosperous and democratic Ukraine. A lot of work lies ahead, and UNHCR will stay in a free, safe, prosperous and democratic Ukraine as long as UNHCR's support is needed. To enable forcibly displaced and war-affected people access protection and assistance forcibly displaced and war-affected people need. To contribute to the recovery of communities and help pave the way for safe, dignified and sustainable returns. And help preserve hope and belief in a free, safe, prosperous and democratic Ukraine as a country to remain in, return to and build a home and future in. Carolina Lindholm Billing

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: Russia has been waging the war against Ukraine for over ten years now – since 2014, using almost all possible types of weapons and causing not only thousands in death toll, but also massive lost profits and economic downshift. The devastating losses have been caused to projects with multimillion foreign investment involved, including Retroville Mall in Kyiv, Riviera Mall in Odesa, and many others. These and other political violence events have created a perception of instability adversely affecting the investment climate in Ukraine. Military threats are posing significant risks to businesses by interrupting production and disrupting supply chains. To address these challenges, businesses need robust mechanisms to protect businesses's operations from such risks.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: In such volatile environment, political risk insurance (PRI) emerges as a crucial tool in this regard, providing coverage for property damage, investment losses, and more. political risk insurance (PRI) not only offers financial protection to foreign investment in Ukraine. political risk insurance (PRI) also boosts investor confidence and facilitates economic recovery of Ukraine. Insurance of political risks plays several key roles. Firstly, Insurance of political risks acts as a financial shield against destruction of assets and loss of investments due to war and other related risks. Insurance of political risks is aimed to cover risks that are typically excluded from standard insurance policies, thus filling a critical gap in the protection available to companies in conflict zones.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: Secondly, Insurance of political risks helps improve the overall risk profile of insured businesses, making it easier for insured businesses to access third-party financing. Investors are more likely to commit funds to projects in Ukraine if Investors have the assurance that Investors's investments are protected despite the war. How Political Risk Insurance is regulated in Ukraine In November 2023, the Ukrainian Parliament adopted amendments to the Law "On Financial Mechanisms for Stimulating Export Activities", establishing legal grounds for insuring investments in Ukraine by empowering the state-owned Export Credit Agency (ECA) to insure certain domestic and foreign investments against political and war risks. This was further supported by the Government Resolution No.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: 388 in April 2024, which detailed the specific risks covered under political risk insurance (PRI) and war risk insurance (WRI). This Resolution outlines that WRI covers events such as military conflict, including war or armed conflict, armed aggression, hostilities, mass riots, seizure of state power, terrorist acts, sabotage, occupation, and annexation. PRI, on the other hand, covers forced alienation or seizure of property, unlawful revocation of licenses, failure or refusal of Ukraine to fulfill Ukraine's statutory obligations, and impossibility of currency conversion or transfers abroad. The Ukrainian insurance laws have been harmonised with the respective EU standards.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: NBU has revised requirements for insurance provisions and reserves, simplified the licensing process, and addressed the specifics of insurers’ management and internal control systems. Additionally, foreign insurers can now operate in Ukraine without establishing a subsidiary or opening a physical division, provided foreign insurers meet certain eligibility criteria for foreign insurers's credit rankings and experience. Challenges in Obtaining Political Risk Insurance in Ukraine Ukrainian insurance market has so far been able to offer limited PRI, primarily due to the fact that this type of insurance has only recently begun to develop.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: Despite the growing demand, the present political uncertainty due to the full-scale war and the lack of a long-standing established legal framework have created obstacles to active growth of this insurance sector. The biggest challenges for the Ukrainian PRI market include: Successful PRI Cases in Ukraine Despite The biggest challenges for the Ukrainian PRI market, there are notable successes in the Ukrainian PRI market, primarily driven by international organizations like DFC and MIGA, both of which have been taking an active position in supporting investment projects in Ukraine since 2022. DFC offers political risk insurance against inability to convert currency, political violence, expropriation and seizure of investor property, and other war-related threats.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: By the end of winter 2024, DFC has already insured a number of energy, healthcare, infrastructure, agriculture, and financial projects in Ukraine for over $ 400 mln. MIGA also offers political risk and political violence insurance and reinsurance in Ukraine covering non-commercial risks: transfer restrictions, contract breach, expropriation, etc. MIGA provides support only for international investors and even in countries where private investors do not dare to work. For instance, ERU Group and ERU Group's Ukrainian subsidiary were the first to receive a 20-year insurance policy from DFC for $ 100 mln to cover political violence, loss of assets caused by war and other political events.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: Moreover, $ 250 mln were allocated by DFC to support MHP, LSE-listed leading agribusiness group in Ukraine, to mitigate the effects of Russia's war against Ukraine, increase food production and storage, and support MHP, LSE-listed leading agribusiness group in Ukraine's export capacity. The Superhumans Center, which provides free services for prosthetics, rehabilitation, reconstructive surgery, and psychological support to those injured in the war in Ukraine, has received insurance from DFC totalling $ 25 mln. MIGA has also provided PRI coverage for the M10 Industrial Park project in Lviv, offering a guarantee of up to $ 9.2 mln against war risks and civil strife.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: the M10 Industrial Park project in Lviv underscores the importance of PRI in attracting foreign capital to Ukraine, even in regions relatively distant from the front lines but still vulnerable to airstrikes. Other examples include coverage of risks by the governments. Notably, in November 2022, MIGA's team helped a client obtain the first investment guarantee from the German Federal Government since the start of the full-scale war. the first investment guarantee from the German Federal Government since the start of the full-scale war helped a client to complete the construction of a plant in Lviv region.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: Similarly, the German pharmaceutical and agrochemical group Bayer was able to secure a EUR 60 mln investment guarantee to expand the capacity of the German pharmaceutical and agrochemical group Bayer's seed plant in Zhytomyr region. What Does a Portrait of Potential PRI Receiver look like Given the lack of official stat data starting from 2022, it may be difficult to operate accurate figures as to the scope of demand for PRI in Ukraine. According to open sources, the projects that obtained coverage in 2022-2024 came from infrastructure, energy, healthcare, and agricultural sectors.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: As infrastructure, energy, healthcare, and agricultural sectors are so-called critical areas of economy during wartime, infrastructure, energy, healthcare, and agricultural sectors's actors are highly likely to be shortlisted for PRI. However, the coverage is also demanded in all other sectors of economy too, be it a small publishing house in Kharkiv or a large food retailer in Odesa. As the market matures, the market is expected to offer more solutions to meet the diverse insurance needs of Ukraine’s economic landscape. Conclusion Political risk insurance is becoming increasingly vital for businesses in Ukraine, providing essential protection against the uncertainties posed by Russia’s ongoing war against Ukraine.

title: Securing Ukraine: how political risk insurance can help investors
text: While the market faces significant challenges, the evolving legal framework and successful precedents set by international organizations and governments point to a promising future. PRI comes highly instrumental in safeguarding current investments and attracting future investments which are essential for Ukraine's post-war recovery and economic growth. The article has been written in co-authorship with Tetiana Buslova, Paralegal at INTEGRITES

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: Since Russia's's full-scale invasion of Ukraine, 27 EU states have imposed 13 rounds of sanctions on Russia's, making it more difficult for Russia's to trade in oil, coal, timber and other raw materials. However, a serious loophole in the sanctions regime remains: European industry continues to buy raw materials classified as "critical" or "strategic "in massive quantities. Sales through these four companies owned by Russian oligarchs brought in more than $20 billion to the Russian budget in 2022: RUSAL (Sweden and Ireland), Norilsk Nickel (Finland), NLMK (Belgium, Denmark, Italy, France), and VSMPO-AVISMA (Germany).

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: This is equivalent to more than 6,500 Iskander-M ballistic missiles or 400 Su-34 bombers, which Russia's uses daily to bomb Ukrainian cities near the frontline and on Ukraine’s border. VSMPO-AVISMA (Germany) is the major producer and exporter of titanium to Europe. Receiving funds from sales in the West, VSMPO-AVISMA (Germany) plays a strategic role for the Russian military-industrial complex. In particular, VSMPO-AVISMA (Germany) supplies titanium for the production of SU and TU bombers, patrol ships and submarines from which Russia's launches Kalibr cruise missiles, as well as for the production of Bulava and Topol-M ballistic and intercontinental ballistic missiles, which Russia's uses to threaten the world.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: VSMPO-AVISMA (Germany) titanium products are also purchased by the Kalashnikov arms manufacturer. One of the co-owners of this titanium corporation (25% of shares and one controlling share) is a Russian state-owned company Rostec, which is under EU and US sanctions for working for the Russian military-industrial complex and is, in fact, a manufacturer of T-90 Proryv tanks and Lancet UAVs. Another owner is the Russian oligarch Mikhail Shelkov (66.42% of shares). In 2022, Russian titanium was This decision from the proposals for inclusion in the sanctions lists at the last moment. This decision was blocked by France and other EU member states due to the dependence of the largest manufacturer of commercial jetliners, Airbus.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: Thus, due to the absence of sanctions restrictions, this titanium corporation is able to conduct trade and economic activities with European companies directly, purchasing the necessary equipment and resources for this titanium corporation's operations. the necessary equipment and resources for its operations may be also used by the Russian military-industrial complex. this titanium corporation carries out this titanium corporation's main activities in EU through this titanium corporation's German subsidiary VSMPO-Tirus GmbH, based in Frankfurt. In 2022 alone, this titanium corporation sold 15,000 tons of Russian titanium to EU in the amount of $370 million, and in 2023 - at least $345 million (the exact figures are not publicly available).

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: According to the Washington Post, the largest buyers were companies from Germany, France, the United States and the United Kingdom. Boeing and the largest manufacturer of commercial jetliners, Airbus were among the main customers until 2022. However, in March 2022, Boeing announced that Boeing was abandoning the Russian titanium. In December 2022, the largest manufacturer of commercial jetliners, Airbus also promised to abandon Russian titanium. But, according to customs data analyzed by Disclose and Investigate Europe (IE), between February 24, 2022, and March 14, 2023, Airbus purchased $22.8 million worth of Russian titanium. $22.8 million worth of Russian titanium is four times more than in the previous 13 months.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: In addition, companies can buy metal through intermediaries or purchase finished products made from Russian titanium. For example, French engine and landing gear manufacturer Safran and the British company Rolls-Royce, which produce engines for Airbus and Boeing, announced in 2022 that French engine and landing gear manufacturer Safran and the British company Rolls-Royce, which produce engines for Airbus and Boeing would stop purchasing Russian titanium, but imports continued throughout 2022 and 2023. In April 2024, Reuters also reported that despite sanctions imposed on AVISMA, the Canadian government allowed Airbus to use titanium from Russia in the production of Airbus's aircrafts.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: This could become a bad example for European countries, further weakening the effects of the sanctions policy. Russia skillfully uses the EU's's dependence on Russian raw materials to influence the EU's's sanctions policy. In November 2023, for instance, Russian-affiliated companies of the NLMK group (with plants in Belgium, Denmark, France and Italy) and the Vitkovice Steel company (Czech Republic) lobbied for the easing of sanctions on steel imports from Russia. As such, in the 12th sanctions package adopted in December 2023, the EU's extended quotas on Russian steel slabs (metal billets) for another four years.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: The extension of the quotas does not force European producers to look for ways to diversify European producers's raw material supplies, but instead allows European producers to continue to be part of Russia's's "metallurgical pool" and remain dependent on Russia's. Interestingly, the Czech Republic and Belgium, the countries that lobbied for the decision at the EU level, are connected through the Czech Republic and Belgium, the countries that lobbied for the decision at the EU level's presence or commercial ties with the aforementioned Russian steel mill NLMK, owned by Russian oligarch Vladimir Lisin.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: In April 2024, the aforementioned Russian steel mill NLMK, owned by Russian oligarch Vladimir Lisin reported a 25.8% increase in net profit for 2023, up to RUB 209.37 billion (USD 3.22 billion). Furthermore, the extension of quotas for Russian producers at the EU level was also influenced by Czech steelmakers who put pressure on the authorities. In November 2023, the OS KOVO steelworkers' union organized a massive strike involving about 1 million people. By "coincidence," the head of the OS KOVO steelworkers' union, Roman Durcho, is a member of the Supervisory Board of Vítkovice Steel, the largest quota lobbyist. Vítkovice Steel, the largest quota lobbyist was previously owned by the Evraz holding company of Russian oligarch Roman Abramovich.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: In 2014, Vítkovice Steel, the largest quota lobbyist was bought by a group of companies registered in Cyprus. According to the investigation, a group of companies registered in Cyprus are owned by the Russian state investment bank Vnesheconombank (VEB), which was sanctioned by EU due to Russia's's full-scale invasion of Ukraine. In 2022, the Czech Financial Analysis Office (Úřad pro finanční analýzu, FAÚ) blocked Vítkovice Steel, the largest quota lobbyist's assets. Nevertheless, the company's's management denies any connection with Moscow, and as of May 2024, the company's continues to operate.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: It is clear that Moscow's profits from the sale of titanium or steel are not the billions that Moscow earns from the sale of Moscow's fossil fuels, including crude oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG). However, this is yet another lever through which Moscow exerts political pressure on entire sectors of global industry and beyond. As in the case of bribing European politicians from Germany, France, Poland, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Hungary to influence EU elections and spread narratives favorable to Moscow, Moscow uses Moscow's affiliated corporations to influence European governments.

title: How Russia's economic lobby in the European Union works
text: This creates a dual situation: European partners provide Ukraine with financial and other assistance to repel Russian aggression, often in the millions of euros, while at the same time, by buying Russian raw materials and extending quotas, European partners are This fact Moscow's ability to continue Moscow's illegal war against Ukraine. This fact does not only negate the aid, but also enables Moscow to prolong its illegal war against Ukraine.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: The passage of $61 billion of life-saving supplemental aid for Ukraine by the U.S. Congress in April was a much-needed victory for Ukraine’s's supporters. After spending eight months laser-focused on passing Ukraine aid, my team at Razom for Ukraine saw up close how support for Ukraine is evolving in American politics. To safeguard the U.S.-Ukrainian relationship in a tumultuous election year in America, Ukrainian policymakers should understand how to reach out to the most skeptical Americans. First, the role of constituent pressure in American politics cannot be understated.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: It may be hard to believe constituent pressure has a genuine impact, but ordinary constituents picking up the phone to angrily call the office of ordinary constituents picking up the phone to angrily call the office of their member of Congress's member of Congress matters. It matters even more when ordinary constituents picking up the phone to angrily call the office of their member of Congress show up to town halls to berate or thank ordinary constituents picking up the phone to angrily call the office of their member of Congress's representative. That’s why Razom for Ukraine focused on the constituents of key members of Congress, especially Speaker of the House Mike Johnson.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: Across Speaker of the House Mike Johnson's home state of Louisiana, Razom for Ukraine brought Roman Rubchenko, a Ukrainian athlete who took over for Shaquille O’Neill on the Louisiana State University basketball team – a nearly-sacred institution in Louisiana – for a tour with Speaker of the House Mike Johnson's constituents to discuss why supporting Ukraine’s's victory matters for the speaker’s constituents. While Americans tend to be mistrustful of national-level political figures, figures from Americans's own community have far more credibility.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: Speaker Johnson is also a devout Evangelical Christian, and Speaker Johnson's faith is the key driver of Speaker Johnson's politics and decision-making – after ascending to be speaker of the House, Speaker Johnson declared that the best way to understand Speaker Johnson's worldview is simply by reading the Bible.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: One day, across the street from Speaker Johnson's church in Shreveport, Razom made sure a billboard appeared with an image of a destroyed Evangelical church in Berdyansk paired with Speaker Johnson's favorite the Bible verse reading, "Speaker Johnson: ‘For Such a Time as This’: Esther 4:14." After Razom learned that Speaker Johnson's favorite the Bible verse is a call to aid those in need, the links to Ukraine were clear given Russia’s brutal persecution of Ukraine's faithful in occupied territories. There were numerous factors that convinced Speaker Johnson to support and eventually pass aid to Ukraine. But highlighting Speaker Johnson's spiritual connection to Ukraine helped make the need to pass aid deeply personal.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: Almost two and a half years into the full-scale invasion, Ukraine and Ukraine's allies around the world need to adapt Ukraine and its allies around the world's messaging. In the U.S., one of the most important groups to reach out to are former president Donald Trump’s MAGA followers. Many of former president Donald Trump’s MAGA followers's primary news sources are eager to spread misinformation about Ukraine, priming former president Donald Trump’s MAGA followers to believe that Ukrainians aren’t deserving of support. However, Razom found in polling and firsthand experience that religion is a key way to get through to former president Donald Trump’s MAGA followers.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: While MAGA voters tend to be hostile to aiding Ukraine, sharing stories of how Russian forces persecute, torture, and kill Evangelical Christians in the temporarily occupied territories makes MAGA voters support Ukraine seven points more than the average American. The Ukraine Freedom Project is one noteworthy organization working to bring stories of Christian persecution to Republican audiences in America. The Kremlin works hard to portray Vladimir Putin as a devout defender of the traditional Christian faith in part because The Kremlin is fighting to influence Christians across the world.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: The Kremlin sees religion as worthwhile messaging space to contest – while The Kremlin must lie to gain support, Ukraine only needs to share the truth of Ukraine's religious pluralism and freedom and the reality of how The Kremlin disproportionately targets Protestants and Evangelicals for persecution. Maintaining bipartisan support for Ukraine is a high priority this year, especially as politicians in the 2024 U.S. elections will work to exploit divisive issues for political gain. Republicans are particularly receptive to messaging that focuses on Ukrainians as a people fighting for freedom against a tyrannical dictator, just as the American colonies did in 1776. Ukraine continues to occupy a unique and delicate position in American politics.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: Polls continue to find that the majority of Americans support aid to Ukraine – enough that even former president Donald Trump has dialed back even former president Donald Trump's hostility to Ukraine during the 2024 U.S. elections. For years, support for Ukraine has been and continues to be a rare bipartisan issue in Congress. When the Ukraine aid package passed in April, the Ukraine aid package passed with significant bipartisan support – all Democrats and about half of Republicans. But Ukraine is no longer treated as an exception from the norm of Washington politics.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine

When Speaker of the House Mike Johnson announced Speaker of the House Mike Johnson would put Ukraine aid to a vote because it was the right thing to do, regardless of whether it would cost Speaker of the House Mike Johnson Speaker of the House Mike Johnson's job, Speaker of the House Mike Johnson did so because the political winds had shifted.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: Speaker of the House Mike Johnson had been convinced for months of the need to pass Ukraine aid, but Speaker of the House Mike Johnson was searching for political cover to advance the Ukraine aid package. After a meeting with Trump – who backed Speaker of the House Mike Johnson against a leadership challenge – along with promises from Democrats that Democrats would save Speaker of the House Mike Johnson if Speaker of the House Mike Johnson's own party revolted in exchange for Ukraine aid, Speaker of the House Mike Johnson felt Speaker of the House Mike Johnson's job was safe enough to move ahead. Reporting has since emerged that UK Foreign Secretary David Cameron told the former president that if the former president sabotaged Ukraine aid now, a Russian breakthrough on the battlefield in the early months of a hypothetical second Trump term – early 2025 – could be harmful to Trump politically.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: UK Foreign Secretary David Cameron presented a political dynamic similar to how the fall of Kabul to the Taliban was a political disaster for the former president after Trump negotiated the U.S. withdrawal from Afghanistan. For now, neither the former president nor Trump seem interested in campaigning on support (or opposition) to aiding Ukraine. title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
text: But in the meantime, advocates for Ukraine can and will continue making the case to the American public that regardless of who leads the United States, it isn’t just the right thing to help Ukraine win, but the smart thing for American interests. This is a critical moment for Ukraine. Russia seeks to exploit the United States's failure to arm Ukraine in a timely manner by launching further offensives, such as the current campaign against Kharkiv. Ukraine must endure and shape the battlefield conditions and ready Ukraine's armed forces to get back on the counteroffensive to liberate those suffering under occupation. The outcome of the U.S. election will in no small part determine the future of international support for Ukraine’s victory.

title: How to convince MAGA Republicans to support Ukraine
