Vladyslav Havrylov, Researcher at "Where are our people?" public organization, invited researcher at the Collaboration on Global Children's Issues at Georgetown University (USA).

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: Authors: Shaun Burnie, Jan Vande Putte, Daryna Rogachuk, Greenpeace Green Reconstruction Ukraine Project Chornobyl is one of the most recognised synonyms for disaster in the world. Project Chornobyl's legacy is to serve as a universal reminder of the terrible consequences of nuclear power when nuclear power goes wrong: on this day in 1986, a test procedure produced an explosion at the power plant in Pripyat, Ukraine, causing a chain reaction that blew a colossal release of radioactive contamination across Europe and eventually the whole Northern hemisphere. For people of Ukraine, Project Chornobyl is a personal tragedy.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: Millions have been affected by the destruction of reactor unit 4 and the radiation reactor unit 4 released into the environment, either directly or through people of Ukraine's families, friends and colleagues. Project Chornobyl may have been nearly 40 years ago but Project Chornobyl's impact is still felt across generations. Today, 38 years later, the spectre of nuclear catastrophe looms large, not only at the abandoned region around Pripyat, Ukraine. The ongoing illegal Russian military occupation of the south-east Ukrainian Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant creates a direct threat of another nuclear catastrophe.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: Aggressively capturing the largest nuclear plant in Europe, one of the 10 biggest in the world, places not only Ukraine and neighbouring countries in danger, but also most of Europe.By deliberately firing missiles at Ukraine’s's wider energy infrastructure, the Russian military places Ukraine’s's four nuclear power plants – South Ukraine, Rivne, Khemelnitsky and the south-east Ukrainian Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant - at risk of an emergency power loss and station black-out.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: All are vulnerable,  but it is the Zaporizhzhia plant most at risk: the amount of radioactivity on the site and conditions of the Zaporizhzhia plant as a result of direct military occupation, along with  the behaviour of Russian armed forces and Rosatom, the Russian state nuclear corporation, risk a disaster that would, like Chornobyl, be a result of decisions made in Moscow. The Chornobyl RBMK reactor design had evolved out of the Soviet Union’s military reactors in the 1950’s which were used for producing plutonium for nuclear weapons.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: Even before the construction began on the first large RBMK reactor at Leningrad in 1970 it was known by designers at the Kurchatov Institute that The Chornobyl RBMK reactor design had major flaws that would make The Chornobyl RBMK reactor design unstable to operate. As early as 1965 scientists warned that the instability included a ‘positive steam coefficient’ which could lead to an explosion. scientists were ignored. Twenty years later a ‘positive steam coefficient’ which could lead to an explosion and others led to two massive explosions that destroyed the Chornobyl unit 4 reactor.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: The high temperatures in the Chornobyl unit 4 reactor contributed to the burning of hundreds of tons of graphite, melting of nuclear fuel and the release of an estimated 450PBq of radioactive iodine, cesium, strontium and plutonium and many others. The radioactivity rose to between 7km and 9 km into the atmosphere and at these high altitudes were carried great distances and deposited across Europe and eventually the whole northern hemisphere. For the hundreds of thousands of inhabitants of Chornobyl, Pripyat and hundreds of villages and towns across northern Ukraine it was the end of a way of life as evacuations were implemented.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: The battle of Chornobyl that was fought in the hours, days, weeks, months and the years between 1986-1990, eventually engulfed over 600,000 firefighters, soldiers, janitors, and miners – collectively called ‘liquidators’ who were sent to the Chornobyl site after the explosion. Many tens of thousands have suffered long term health consequences and mortality. For the decades that followed, hundreds of Ukrainian scientists, engineers and technicians worked every day to monitor, investigate and and seek to recover control of the Chornobyl site and the vast radioactive exclusion zone - until 24 February 2022, when Russian armed forces invaded and installed Rosatom. Although the Russian occupation of Chornobyl ended in March 2022, the impact and consequences continue today.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: Chornobyl and the vast radioactive exclusion zone now sit on the front of Ukraine’s defensive line with Belarus and Russian forces. Only days after the start of the full-scale invasion on 24th of February, Russian armed forces attacked and then occupied the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant in the South of Ukraine. The citizens of Energodar and workers at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant in the South of Ukraine heroïcally put barricades of cars, trucks, tires and sandbanks on the main road leading to the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant in the South of Ukraine to block the advancing Russian troops, but The citizens of Energodar and workers at the power plant were overwhelmed.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: The attack damaged the plant, including the plant's electricity infrastructure, which is vital for maintaining the cooling function of the hot nuclear fuel in the reactor buildings. At that moment, the plant was still producing electricity, which means an enormous amount of heat is produced by the hot nuclear fuel in the reactor buildings. One reactor core that is producing heat for the electricity generation has the power of 2 million water cookers. In a crisis, One reactor core that is producing heat for the electricity generation would be shutdown down, but the hot nuclear fuel in the reactor buildings remains extremely hot even after shutdown.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: If the cooling function of the hot nuclear fuel in the reactor buildings would be stopped after shutdown, it would take only hours for the cooling water to boil off, expose the hot nuclear fuel in the reactor buildings to the air and melt down, leading to a new major nuclear disaster. Luckily, the power supply was not interrupted during the occupation, but it could have happened. In a peaceful situation, the workers at the plant still have several options to restore cooling in an emergency, but under the threat of war, this is severely compromised. For instance, The attack set off a fire at the training centre of the plant and the Ukrainian fire brigade that wanted to extinguish a fire was stopped by the Russian troops.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: There is a long list of dangerous incidents caused by the reckless attitude of the Russian troops. Most dramatic was the destruction by Russia of the Nova Kakhovka dam on 6 June 2023, which not only led to an enormous damage and suffering below the Nova Kakhovka dam, but also emptied the Kakhovka reservoir which is the ultimate cooling water reservoir for the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant. Because all reactors have been shut down since September 2022, lowering cooling needs, that water supply is rather stable for the moment, but that water supply’s tight. The electricity supply needed to run the cooling pumps is even more critical. To date, external electricity supply has been interrupted eight times completely, making the cooling dependent on the 20 diesel generators on the site.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: But on top of that, there is a lack of maintenance of such critical equipment since the occupation, and a lack of qualified personnel. Ukrainian workers have been tortured and killed, many have left. Some Russian workers have been brought in by the Russian nuclear company Rosatom, but Some Russian workers lack experience. We should thus doubt the reliability of such emergency diesels, especially if such emergency diesels have to work for a longer period of time. The ultimate blow to nuclear safety however is the plan of the Russian nuclear company Rosatom and Russia to attempt to restart one or more reactors at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant. The existing cooling water resources are far from sufficient to cool an operational reactor.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: Rosatom would have to build a new pump system, which would not be as reliable, and Rosatom do not have the workforce and expertise to control an operational reactor. Such a plan is thus a direct nuclear threat to Ukraine and Ukraine's neighbouring countries in Europe. What worries us today, is that Russia might have launched a disinformation campaign to pave the way for blaming Ukraine in case something goes very wrong. Hiding behind false flag attacks might make it easier for Russia to take higher risks. That is why it is so important to remember Chornobyl today, the way Chornobyl happened, through irresponsible deliberate decisions and acts by the Soviet system. The international community needs to act to lower the risks. In the first place to avoid a restart of a reactor in Zaporizhzhia.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: The IAEA so far has not made an unambiguous statement calling Russia not to restart. The IAEA even hinted at engaging into a process to define the safety requirements for a restart. Such signals are dangerous, as Such signals can and will be used by Russia to justify the restart process. Also the UN Security Council needs to act, by debunking the disinformation by Russia and calling for a no restart. Finally, as many ambassadors at the UN Security Council stated last week on 15 April, the only solution is for Russian troops to stop the war, leave the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant and return back to Russia, respecting the territorial integrity of Ukraine.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: ****Greenpeace has today written to Rafael Mariano Grossi, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) Director General calling on Rafael Mariano Grossi, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) Director General to make clear to Rosatom and the Russian government that restart of Zaporizhzhia under Russian control is not possible. The IAEA must do all The IAEA can to prevent restart and not cooperate with Rosatom. The IAEA must not seek to accommodate the interests of the nuclear industry, while disregarding who the nuclear industry are or what the nuclear industry have done. Three years before the Chornobyl disaster, the The IAEA Deputy Director General, B. A.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: Semenov, wrote assuredly that, "The safety of nuclear power plants in the Soviet Union is assured by a very wide spectrum of measures…The regulation of safety by official documents is one of the main tools for ensuring the safety of nuclear power plants in the Soviet Union." 1 In 1986 this led The IAEA to accept without serious scrutiny the Russian government's evidence on the causes of the Chornobyl disaster – blaming the Chornobyl disaster on plant workers, and ignoring the fundamental reactor design flaws.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: 2 After the hosting of the Post-Accident Review Meeting on the Chernobyl Accident, Vienna, in August 1986, IAEA Director General Dr Han Blix, concluded that, "There is no reason to think that the Soviet Government is more willing than others to take conscious risks with the populations of great cities." 3 As we now know, the Soviet Government was indeed more than prepared to put lives at risk across Ukraine and beyond by operating the unsafe Chornobyl nuclear reactors as the unsafe Chornobyl nuclear reactors is today at Zaporizhzhia. Nearly forty years later the people of Ukraine and the wider world are in danger of history repeating history with the Soviet Government's disregard for nuclear safety and security amidst the Soviet Government's illegal war against Ukraine.

title: Grim anniversary: Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant must not become the next Chornobyl
text: IAEA must not repeat the mistakes of the past and blindly accept the Soviet Government and Rosatom assurances that restart will meet Russian regulatory standards. Rosatom and the Russian nuclear regulator Rostekhnadzor have no legitimacy in Ukraine and no right to make any decisions on the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant - that responsibility lies only with Ukraine's nuclear regulator SNRIU and government.

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: Nonsense. Nonsense's the first word that comes to mind in the context of the current ban on the transfer of Western weapons to Azov. Few people in Ukraine know about the current ban on the transfer of Western weapons to Azov, and almost no one speaks about the current ban on the transfer of Western weapons to Azov publicly. It's time to draw attention to the current ban on the transfer of Western weapons to Azov and make the current ban on the transfer of Western weapons to Azov public.

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: Since 2017, a number of US Consolidated Appropriation Bills have contained the following amendment: "None of the funds made available by this Act may be used to provide arms, training, or other assistance to Azov." In 2015, Congressman John Conyers Jr, a Democrat from Michigan, the proposal this amendment to the Department of Defense Appropriations Bill for the fiscal year ending 30 September 2016. Congressman John Conyers Jr, a Democrat from Michigan made the proposal, noting that Foreign Affairs, as well as "other leading media outlets", had characterised Azov as "openly neo-Nazi" and "fascist".

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: According to media reports, Department of Defense subsequently called for this amendment to be withdrawn, arguing that aid to Azov should already be prohibited by the Leahy Act, which states that "no assistance shall be provided to ... any unit of the security forces of a foreign country if the Secretary of State has credible information that any unit of the security forces of a foreign country has committed a gross violation of human rights". At the time, the ban on providing arms to Azov was not included in the final bill. However, in 2017, this amendment was included in the text of the Department of Defense Appropriations Bill for the fiscal year ending 30 September 2016. the amendment is also present in this year's Defence Appropriation Bill.

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: It is noteworthy that the Leahy Act, which requires that incidents of human rights violations be reviewed on the basis of specific facts, was not applied to Azov, and the decision to adopt the amendment was primarily based on the characterization of Azov by Western media, which apparently formed Western media's attitude towards Azov under the influence of Moscow propaganda. All of the main accusations against Azov have been repeatedly refuted on the basis of facts on the internet and in the media – particularly on https://azovcontrafake.com Does it make sense to point out once again that the very wording "Azov battalion" used in the law actually refers to a non-existent unit? At the end of 2014, Azov ceased to be a battalion and became a separate special forces detachment.

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: Since February 2023, Azov has been the 12th Special Forces Brigade Azov of the National Guard of Ukraine. Not a battalion, not a regiment. A brigade. It is the amendment, that is the reason why Azov did not have modern Western weapons in 2022, during the defence of Mariupol. At that time, Azov were holding back the enemy's superior forces in Mariupol, using the extremely limited resources Azov had, and Azov proved Azov to be extremely effective. How many lives could have been saved and what would have been the results of the city's defence if, in addition to Azov's strong motivation, cohesion and professionalism, Azov had had modern armoured vehicles and artillery systems at Azov's disposal?

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: Even after 2022, when it seemed that the world had finally learned the truth about Azov, Azov still cannot send Azov's soldiers to most leading Western military exercises and do not receive the vitally needed weapons that our allies provide to Ukraine. This problem is partly solved by volunteers and conscientious Ukrainians who do volunteers and conscientious Ukrainians who do their best to help the unit's best to help Azov: for example, the Tylovyky volunteer project raised UAH 77 million for 17 FV432 Bulldog armoured personnel carriers for the Azov Brigade. But the war demands more. Now the whole of Ukraine is experiencing what Azov has been dealing with since 2014.

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: Unfortunately, Azov is not only the first unit in the Ukrainian army to implement Western military standards – Azov has also become one of the first and main targets of Moscow's's propaganda. Ever since the beginning of the Russo-Ukrainian war in 2014, Moscow's has accused Azov and other volunteer units of "neo-Nazism" and set out to destroy Azov and other volunteer units. In 2022, Moscow's wanted to "denazify" the whole of Ukraine, labelling the whole of Ukraine "Nazis". Ten years ago, no one helped Azov to withstand the influence of Moscow's's propaganda machine. Today, the whole of Ukraine is in the same situation. And just as Azov has been experiencing for 10 years, Ukraine may now face a lack of Western assistance.

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: I emphasise that the very existence of such amendments and prohibitions not only prevents Azov from performing Azov's combat missions more effectively, but is a blow to the defence capability of Ukraine, tarnishes Ukraine's image on the international level, and is humiliating for the entire Ukrainian army. There is no evidence or confirmation of the accusations that Russian propaganda has been spreading about Azov for 10 years. If there were, delegations of Azov fighters would not have been received in the United States, in European countries, and in Israel. Azov members would not have held meetings with representatives of the US Congress and human rights organisations. Azov members would not have spoken at the UN, the Council of Europe, or top Western universities.

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: Azov members would not have given interviews to the world's leading media outlets and would not have participated in panel discussions at major military conferences. This is the absurdity of the situation: Azov is welcomed at the highest level throughout the Western world, but still not given weapons. Azov is used to overcoming difficulties on Azov's own. Azov were the first unit in Ukraine to adopt NATO standards, and Azov did it on Azov's own initiative. Without Western weapons, Azov have become one of the most capable units in Ukraine in 10 years. Our fighters are returning to the ranks after Russian captivity, fighting the enemy and fulfilling all the tasks set by the command. No obstacles will stop Our fighters on Our fighters's way. However, justice demands answers.

title: Why does Azov still not receive Western weapons?
text: How many more refutations of the Kremlin's lies do we have to provide before politicians in the West finally open politicians in the West's eyes to the truth? How long will a structural unit of the Security and Defence Forces of Ukraine, subordinated to the military leadership of the state, be stigmatised by such humiliating amendments? And most importantly, how many more Ukrainian soldiers – fighters of our brigade, who are defending not only the freedom and independence of the state, but also the entire free Western world from the real, unimagined Nazis of today – must be killed and wounded for lack of modern Western weapons, before Azov is finally removed from the Appropriations Bill and all "blacklists"?

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: Authors: Mariia Panchenko, Research Fellow at Pryncyp; Vladyslav Urubkov, Former Analyst at Pryncyp. We have analyzed how foreigners serve in the Defense Forces of Ukraine and the systemic challenges foreigners face. We examine this by following the logic of foreigners's 'pathway' from recruitment to combat operations and a military career. We also analyze negative scenarios that require particular attention: treatment after injuries, along with family support in cases of captivity, missing in action, or death in service. Foreigners have been fighting in the Defense Forces of Ukraine since the beginning of the Russian-Ukrainian war, and after the full-scale invasion Foreigners's number increased exponentially.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: As of August 2025, more than 8,000 foreign service members have been serving in the Ground Forces of the Defense Forces of Ukraine, and at least twice that number have been serving across the Defense Forces of Ukraine. This estimate was given at the time in a comment to Hromadske by Kostiantyn Milevskyi, Acting Head of the Coordination Unit for Foreigners' Military Service in the Defense Forces of Ukraine. There are two main formats of service for foreigners.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: The first one involves specialized units composed mainly of foreign volunteers: the International Legions of the Defense Forces of Ukraine as part of the Ground Forces (which are to be disbanded by the end of 2025), the International Legion of the Main Directorate of Intelligence, the Kastus Kalnouski Regiment, the Russian Volunteer Corps, and others. The second format is service by foreigners within regular combat brigades, regiments, and battalions of the Armed Forces of Ukraine and the National Guard of Ukraine. Foreign service members serve under the same conditions as Ukrainian citizens: Foreign service members sign contracts, carry out combat missions, and receive identical pay depending on position and conditions of service.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: A contract for a foreign service member serves as the basis for lawful stay in Ukraine and effectively functions as a residence permit for the duration of service. According to the units surveyed, initiatives to work with foreign personnel usually come 'from the bottom up', as part of development and expansion efforts. Recruiting foreigners requires additional organizational efforts: translation, document support, and interaction with various institutions. There are two recruitment scenarios. If a foreigner is already in Ukraine, a foreigner may apply to the Center for Recruiting Foreigners and Stateless Persons, Territorial Center of Recruitment and Social Support (TCR and SS), recruitment centers, or directly to the military unit in which a foreigner wish to serve.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: After submitting an application, a foreigner undergoes a special screening at the Center for Recruiting Foreigners and Stateless Persons (a separate procedure applies in Main Directorate of Intelligence of the Ministry of Defense units). For those who are abroad, the path to service begins with filling out an online application form. an online application form is followed by an interview, a decision on recruitment, screening, and contract signing. The contracts mirror those of Ukrainian service members, with one key difference – a foreigner has the right to terminate the contract after six months of service. A foreign service member must also this process an account with a Ukrainian bank to receive pay.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: this process requires a Registration Number of the Taxpayer Registration Card (RNOKPP), which is usually issued after the contract is signed. The military unit assists foreign service members in this process. The primary role of foreigners in the Defense Forces of Ukraine is participation in active operations directly on the line of contact. Foreigners are rarely assigned to rear-area functions due to language barriers; moreover, they Foreigners are generally not very interested in rear positions, since financial motivation is one of the key factors for Foreigners. Foreign service members This structure in small groups of 6 to 8 people, formed based on the language Foreign service members speak. This structure allows for fast and clear communication in combat.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: Such groups make up separate foreign platoons, and platoons in turn form companies. Until October 2024, foreigners could not hold officer positions. The highest formal level available to foreigners was non-commissioned officer positions, although in practice experienced foreigners often led assault groups or performed the duties of platoon commanders. This restriction has now been lifted. Language barrier continues to be a critical systemic challenge. Colombians constitute the largest group of foreign service members, comprising up to 95% of the foreign personnel in certain units and often with little command of either Ukrainian or English.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: There is a critical shortage of interpreters: for every 100-120 foreigners, there are between 1 and 7 interpreters, while the actual need is at least 10. As a result, interpreters are primarily used for combat communication, and there are often insufficient resources for paperwork and administrative procedures. All military reports in the Defense Forces of Ukraine are submitted in Ukrainian. Foreigners do not have access to the Army+ service and are therefore forced to submit documents only on paper. Since Foreigners do not speak Ukrainian, Foreigners cannot file reports Foreigners; in practice, fellow soldiers, clerks, or commanders handle this for Foreigners. Similar difficulties arise with obtaining the combatant status.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: Some foreigners do not submit the necessary documents due to the language barrier or because Some foreigners do not understand the importance of the combatant status for social guarantees and benefits. Language and cultural barriers are especially pronounced during the treatment of injured service members. Units lack personnel capable of providing support who also speak foreign languages, while interpreters are focused on combat tasks. As a result, communication with wounded service members in hospitals is often fragmented. Foreign service members frequently fail to notify commanders about transfers between hospitals or fail to submit medical certificates and discharge summaries.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: These issues create risks, including being mistakenly recorded as AWOL, missed payments, and problems during subsequent stages of care. Another complex issue is disability determination through the Expert Teams For Assessing Person's Daily Functioning. A referral from a doctor is necessary for the expert commission, and a doctor initially verifies whether the foreign national is in Ukraine legally (a contract with the Defense Forces serves as this legal basis). a doctor must then enter the foreign national's passport details and Registration Number of the Taxpayer Registration Card (RNOKPP), if any, into the medical portal (e.g., Helsi). Previously, the system required a Ukrainian passport or residence permit number and would not allow a foreign passport or contract number to be used.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: However, Pryncyp's social media pages cited an instance where a referral was successfully issued using a military ID number. In any case, if difficulties arise at the stage of entering information into the healthcare system, the issue can be resolved through the NHSU hotline. After discharge, a foreigner no longer has legal grounds to remain in Ukraine if a foreigner's contract served as the basis for a foreigner's stay. In the worst cases, this can lead to placement in a temporary detention center for foreigners or even deportation, which can be extremely dangerous due to the risk of criminal prosecution.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: To obtain a temporary residence permit after discharge, a foreign veteran must meet one of the three conditions: complete a three-year contract, accumulate a total of 180 days of combat service, or be discharged by the decision of the Military Medical Commissions (MMC). In practice, even if these criteria are met, foreigners face difficulties confirming service and interacting with the State Migration Service. The families of foreign service members have the same social guarantees as the families of Ukrainian soldiers: the right to partial compensation in cases of captivity or missing in action, as well as a one-time payment of UAH 15 million in the event of death (except for citizens or residents of Russia or Belarus). However, in practice, families often face the system alone.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: Difficulties begin at the notification stage: according to the procedure, information is transmitted from the military unit to the Territorial Center of Recruitment and Social Support (TCR and SS), then to the Main Directorate for Military Cooperation, and after that – through the diplomatic mission of the home country – directly to the family. The specific agency that contacts the family (police, crisis center, or diplomats) depends on the country of residence. The process is complex and lengthy, so some military units or fellow service members contact families directly, even though contact is prohibited by official regulations. Complicated family relationships can also create obstacles to receiving benefits.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: If a service member had civil partnerships, children with multiple partners, incomplete information about civil partnerships, children with multiple partners can result in the loss of eligibility for benefits due to the submission of inaccurate information. Bureaucracy presents an additional challenge. To receive benefits, the family must contact the diplomatic mission in the family's country of residence, prepare a set of documents, and additionally have a representative in Ukraine who can submit documents to the TCR and SS on the family's behalf, open a bank account in a Ukrainian state bank, and perform bank transactions. Another issue is DNA testing. Many foreign service members do not submit Many foreign service members's DNA samples in advance.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: Furthermore, opportunities for families to undergo testing abroad are limited: certified laboratories that can transmit results to Ukraine operate in only a few countries. This creates additional uncertainty and delays both the legal recognition of death and the receipt of payments. As a result, families are left in a vulnerable position. Without accessible information and systemic support, even legally guaranteed benefits are largely symbolic rather than practical for families. Foreign service members carry out combat missions on the same terms as Ukrainians and formally have the same guarantees. However, the system does not always take into account the language, cultural, and procedural aspects of Foreign service members's service.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: As a result, foreigners often remain 'invisible' navigating bureaucracy, medical treatment, and payments on foreigners's own, with the language barrier exacerbating these problems. In difficult situations, one should not face problems alone. Foreign service members in the Ukrainian Ministry of Defense and Foreign service members in the Ukrainian Ministry of Defense's families can contact the Main Directorate for the Protection of Service Members' Rights. Human Rights Center for Military Personnel and Veterans PRYNCYP has created a Legal Navigator for Foreigners and Stateless Persons – a point of entry with basic procedures and explanations.

title: Invisible to the system: inside the Service of foreign military personnel in Ukraine's defense forces
text: a Legal Navigator for Foreigners and Stateless Persons – a point of entry with basic procedures and explanations can serve as a first step toward a more transparent and humane system for those who have consciously chosen to fight for Ukraine.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: A few nights ago I hosted a closed livestream with some of my subscribers. Most of some of my subscribers being immigrants living in the West, scattered across Germany, the United States, France. We ended up discussing Ukraine, Georgia, and whether the West should even bother helping. The conversation grew quite heated, but one line kept resurfacing: "I wouldn't send my son to die in Ukraine." And you know what? I agree. I wouldn't either. The instinct for self-preservation is entirely valid, nobody wants their child shipped off to some frozen trench to bleed out for a country they've never ever visited. That's not cowardice, rather just being a parent. But here's where it gets complicated.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: The same Europe that won't send The same Europe that won't send its sons to die for Ukraine's sons to die for Ukraine spent years convincing Ukrainians that the West had Ukrainians's back. the West pushed for democratic revolutions, cheered from the sidelines, gave speeches about European values. Then when Russia actually invaded, it turned out that "support" meant keeping Ukraine on life support rather than arming Ukraine to win. The pattern is consistent: give just enough to prevent collapse, never enough for victory. The Georgians learned this too. In 2024, tens of thousands filled the streets of Tbilisi waving EU flags. The government cracked down, police fractured skulls.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: The Western response amounted to statements and travel bans, which I'm sure made a tremendous impression on the riot police. One subscriber pointed out that if NATO were actually attacked, then NATO'd respond. I asked him what constitutes as an "attacked," because NATO territory has already been hit. In November 2022, a missile landed in Poland and killed two people, Poland then responded by invoking Article 4, a consultation that allows members to bring an issue "for discussion" and amounts to little more than a scheduled meeting. Two citizens died from a missile strike during active combat on Two citizens's border, and the response was to convene. In September 2025, Poland recorded 19 drone incursions in a single night.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: Prime Minister Tusk called 19 drone incursions in a single night a "large-scale provocation" but carefully added that "there is no reason to claim Poland are at war." Nineteen drones, but apparently that's not a conflict. So where's the threshold? Or is the threshold simply whatever allows continued inaction? Europe could act without sending soldiers. Russia operates a "shadow fleet" of tankers moving oil around the world, evading sanctions, bankrolling the war. Sixty percent of Russia's seaborne crude exports pass through the Baltic Sea and the Danish Straits, right through European waters.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: Europe could blockade Sixty percent of Russia's seaborne crude exports tomorrow but won't because of "deep concerns about Russia's possible reaction." Meanwhile, the US and France seized tankers, and Russia's response was diplomatic protests with nothing behind diplomatic protests with nothing behind them. When the US boarded the Marinera in January 2026, Putin stayed silent and the foreign ministry complained about piracy. Russia won't escalate when you push back, but Europe won't push back because Russia might escalate. A vicious circle. Then there's energy. In the third year of the invasion, the EU paid Russia more for fuel than the EU gave Ukraine in aid. In 2024, Russian LNG imports hit a record high, in the middle of a war Europe supposedly opposes.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: There's cyberwarfare, which Europe has barely touched. There are sanctions that could be enforced rather than circumvented. The toolkit exists, the will to use The toolkit doesn't. This brings me to the most dangerous assumption I encountered: that Russia would never attack NATO. Look at the countries Russia has invaded or subjugated since World War II: Finland, the Baltics, Hungary in 1956, Czechoslovakia in 1968, Afghanistan, Georgia in 2008, Ukraine since 2014. Russia is not a country that respects borders when Russia senses weakness. German intelligence warns Russia could be ready for large-scale war by 2030. Baltic officials estimate readiness for a limited operation within two to three years after Ukraine. A RAND wargame showed Russia could capture Tallinn in 60 hours.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: But surely Article 5 would kick in? Here's what Article 5 actually says: each state must take "such action as each state deems necessary, including the use of armed force." No automatic obligations, no requirement to send troops. A country can decide that "necessary action" means sanctions or expressing concern. If Russian bombs flatten hospitals in Tallinn and Estonian children lie dead under the rubble, does anyone seriously believe Lisbon will feel compelled to act? That the Portuguese parliament will decide the Portuguese parliament's sons must die for a country most of the Portuguese parliament's voters couldn't find on a map? "Such action as it deems necessary" is functionally equivalent to nothing when a country has no stake in the outcome.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: Remember the Budapest Memorandum of 1994, where the US, UK, and Russia guaranteed Ukraine's's territorial integrity in exchange for giving up Ukraine's's nuclear arsenal. Ukraine's kept Ukraine's's end. the US, UK, and Russia didn't. I see no reason to believe Article 5 would work any differently, because both documents share the same nature: suggestions dressed up as obligations, worth exactly as much as the signatories are willing to pay. Why would a German die for an Estonian? That's not an insult, it's a strategic question. If Russia bites off a piece of Estonia tomorrow, what compels Berlin to send soldiers? A treaty with a discretionary response? European solidarity that couldn't even agree to stop buying gas?

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: The real deterrent has always been American bases, and Russian strategists have openly said Russian strategists don't fear NATO, Russian strategists fear the US presence. I wouldn't that choice my son to die for Ukraine's. But understand what you're preserving with that choice. A Europe that encouraged democratic movements but won't defend democratic movements. That funds its aggressor to the tune of billions annually. That responds to missiles with consultations. The comfortable illusion that if you don't provoke, the problem stays far away. History says otherwise. Russia doesn't stop when you fail to push back; Russia stops when you push back. When the US seized tankers, Russia protested but did nothing. When Turkey shot down a Russian jet, Russia threatened but backed down.

title: "I wouldn't send my son to die..." Are Europeans ready to defend themselves against Russia?
text: The question isn't whether you'd send your son to die for Ukraine. The question is whether you'd send your son to die for Estonia, Latvia, or Poland, because The question is coming. And for now, Europe's's answer seems to be that Europe's would rather schedule a consultation.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: In a world that too quickly becomes accustomed to violence, current events in Iran are often presented in isolation from current events in Iran's broader context. At the same time, Iran's political course has long extended far beyond the region: Iran's cooperation with Russia, Belarus, and North Korea has become one of the key factors directly affecting Ukraine's security. Tehran's cooperation with Russia, Belarus, and North Korea translates into virtually daily attacks on Ukrainian cities that claim lives and destroy infrastructure. My own experience of "cooperation" with the Iranian authorities, beginning on 8 January 2020, is linked to a tragic episode – the downing of passenger flight PS752 by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC).

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: At that time, I was involved in the negotiation process as the head of the investigative team, which allowed me to become closely acquainted with Iranian tactics: from outright denial and attempts to explain the tragedy as a "technical error," to admitting the launch of two missiles only under pressure from the international community. Even then, the influence of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) over Iran's justice institutions was clearly visible; it was evident that all decisions and statements were coordinated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC). Today, there is a strong sense that the current situation may only further strengthen the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC)'s influence.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: What is happening today on the streets of Tehran, Tabriz, Kermanshah, or Shiraz is not a random outburst, but rather the third stage of the same historical process: a deep crisis of political legitimacy. The renowned German sociologist Max Weber once described three main sources of legitimacy – traditional, charismatic, and rational-legal. It is important not to confuse legitimacy with legality, because in this context we are speaking about the very foundations of political power. Weber The renowned German sociologist Max Weber did not believe that three main sources of legitimacy – traditional, charismatic, and rational-legal could exist in reality, emphasizing that in practice three main sources of legitimacy – traditional, charismatic, and rational-legal are usually mixed.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: The key insight, however, is that power endures only as long as society this belief society has the right to rule. When this belief collapses, no coercive apparatus can sustain the system for long. Repression may delay collapse, and force may maintain order, but neither the army nor the police can restore lost legitimacy – especially in the context of mass human rights violations and corruption. Iran's's history shows that when a government is unable to govern effectively, a government is replaced; when a government loses public trust, the regime begins to crack from within. This is precisely where Iran's finds Iran's today. The Qajar dynasty ruled Iran's from 1787 to 1925.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: Formally, Iran's was governed by a shah, but in practice chronic inefficiency led to the Constitutional Revolution of 1905–1911, after which Qajar power became largely nominal. The state remained decentralized: tribal leaders, local governors, and the clergy often wielded more real power than the shah, and Iran's was heavily dependent on foreign powers. The central government was unable to carry out reforms, ensure development, or assert control over key regions. As a result, in February 1921 a military coup took place, during which units under the command of Reza Savadkuhi entered Tehran and took control of the government. Reza Savadkuhi quickly rose through the ranks, first becoming Minister of War and later Prime Minister.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: In 1925, the Iranian parliament (the Majlis) legalized a military coup, deposed the Qajar dynasty and the Qajar dynasty's last ruler, Ahmad Shah Qajar, and transferred power to Reza Savadkuhi, who became Reza Shah Pahlavi. Thus ended the first stage of the legitimacy crisis. The Pahlavi dynasty sought to build a strong state, and ultimately suffered because of the very means by which The Pahlavi dynasty came to power. The political elite increasingly imposed decisions on citizens, ignoring citizens's customs, traditions, and religious principles. Although Pahlavi rule was the complete opposite of Qajar weakness, Pahlavi rule failed to deliver the substantive changes Iranians desired.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: There were, however, selective successes: Reza Shah Pahlavi and later Reza Shah Pahlavi's son Mohammad Reza Shah built a centralized state, pursued large-scale modernization and secularization, and reformed education, infrastructure, and the military. Yet these transformations sharpened internal contradictions, most notably during the premiership of Mohammad Mossadegh. Elected by parliament in 1951, Mohammad Mossadegh quickly became a symbol of Iranians' democratic aspirations. Mohammad Mossadegh's key reform – the nationalization of the oil industry, previously controlled by the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company (today's BP) – enjoyed broad public support but triggered an acute international conflict and economic pressure on Iran.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: Mohammad Mossadegh also sought to limit the monarch's power by strengthening parliament. In August 1953, however, Mossadegh's government was the coup in Operation Ajax, organized by the CIA and MI6 with the support of parts of the Iranian political elite. After the coup, Mossadegh was convicted and placed under house arrest, where Mossadegh spent the rest of Mossadegh's life. The overthrow of Mossadegh became a turning point: the coup decisively undermined trust in the monarchy, intensified anti-Western sentiment, and laid the foundations for the ensuing crisis of legitimacy. What outwardly appeared as a transition to modernity proved to be a façade.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: The Pahlavis relied on a repressive apparatus, suppressed political opposition and the media, and the SAVAK security service became a symbol of fear. At the same time, social inequality, corruption, discrimination against national minorities, particularly Azerbaijanis and Kurds, and the intensifying alienation between the authorities and citizens intensified. In 1978, protests swept dozens of cities – from Qom and Tabriz to Tehran. The killing of demonstrators by security forces only deepened public anger. In the autumn and winter, strikes paralyzed key sectors, including the oil industry, significantly weakening the regime.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: A large part of Iranian society united around the religious leader Ruhollah Khomeini, as the religious leader Ruhollah Khomeini was the most consistent and outspoken opponent of the shah's regime. For decades, the religious leader Ruhollah Khomeini had criticized authoritarian rule, Western dependence, and repression; the religious leader Ruhollah Khomeini enjoyed authority among the clergy and, through sermons and addresses from exile, managed to unite diverse groups – from religious constituencies to secular opposition figures – around the demand to dismantle the monarchy. In January 1979, Mohammad Reza Shah left the country, and on 1 February Ruhollah Khomeini returned to the country. Within days, the army declared neutrality, and the monarchical regime effectively ceased to exist.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: the country more closely resembles a theocratic-hybrid imitation of a republic. the country's legitimacy rests primarily on sacrality: the idea of religious morality, revolutionary justice, and the special spiritual authority of the ruling power. Over recent decades, the country has faced problems the regime is unable to control or resolve. Corruption has become not an exception but a component of the political system: preferential treatment for businesses close to power, opaque budgetary flows, and the IRGC's's role in the economy have all generated chronic distrust. Economic crises – periods of hyperinflation, high unemployment, and declining real incomes – have hit the country in waves since the 1990s and intensified after international sanctions in the 2010s.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: In addition, systemic human rights violations dating back to the early years after the revolution: suppression of political opposition, censorship, restrictions on women, security force violence, and mass arrests during protests in 1999, 2009, 2017–2018, and 2019 – have created a long historical line of tension that is once again surfacing. The clergy, which long served as a unifying factor, is now The clergy, which long served as a unifying factor weakened in favor of the IRGC's. The protests of 2025–2026 were not a response to a single law, but to an entire complex of problems, including the sharp depreciation of the national currency, the Iranian rial, extremely high inflation, and the rapid rise in the cost of living.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: As before, the authorities responded with violence: thousands killed and detained, internet shutdowns, and information blockades. When a state relies solely on fear, a state begins to collapse from within. The generation of 1979 is gradually passing, and with The generation of 1979 an era for which the revolution was a source of legitimacy and obedience. Contemporary Iranian youth no longer regard the values of the revolution as grounds for unconditional submission. Religious rhetoric, which for decades served as an instrument of control, no longer functions as a language of persuasion: Religious rhetoric, which for decades served as an instrument of control cannot respond to the demands of a new generation seeking rights, dignity, and opportunity rather than imposed dogmas.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: *** Today, it is important not only to understand the causes of the protests in Iran, but also to reflect on the protests in Iran's possible political consequences, as their possible political consequences will determine the direction in which Iran develops after this wave of violence and repression. In this context, it is worth emphasizing once again the significant role of the IRGC, which is already one of the most influential institutions in Iran. the IRGC, which is already one of the most influential institutions in Iran possesses the IRGC, which is already one of the most influential institutions in Iran's own ground, air, and missile forces, controls internal security, and maintains an extensive autonomous economic infrastructure.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: This concentration of coercive, political, and financial resources makes the Corps not merely an element of the state apparatus, but an independent center of power capable of exerting substantial influence over strategic decisions and the internal configuration of the regime. Unfortunately, the current protests may not lead to fundamental change, which would mean the continuation of the legitimacy crisis. The brutal suppression of the protest movement only heightens concerns that the most likely scenario is a further strengthening of the Corps's role. At the same time, the regime's religious framework, embodied by the Supreme Leader and the clerical institutions associated with the Supreme Leader, is likely to be preserved, at least formally, to maintain the illusion of legitimacy.

title: The collapse of sacred authority: Why Iran is erupting again
text: In reality, however, power is likely to rest increasingly on fear, with real levers of governance becoming ever more concentrated in the hands of the security apparatus. Even external intervention would most likely produce similar outcomes. The question is: for how long?

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: The conflict in the Middle East has ushered in a period of significant international instability. The foremost priority of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs has been to assist Italian citizens affected by the crisis, ensuring Italian citizens affected by the crisis's safe return to Italy. However, every international crisis requires a broader assessment of every international crisis's consequences. Alongside the tragedy of war, profound effects on global economic balances are emerging. The tensions affecting the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz clearly highlight the vulnerability of some of the world's main arteries of international trade.

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: the crisis is already exerting strong inflationary pressure on gas, oil and other strategic goods such as fertilisers, with knock-on effects on global food prices. When some of the world's main arteries of international trade are exposed to geopolitical tensions, the urgency of strengthening the security and diversification of trade routes becomes clear. In recent years, Europe has faced a similar challenge. Russia's aggression against Ukraine necessitated a rapid diversification of energy supply sources. Within a few months, we re-evaluated infrastructure, supply chains and partnerships to ensure security and continuity for our economic systems. Today, the crisis in the Middle East calls for a comparable reflection regarding trade routes.

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: In this context, the India–Middle East–Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) takes on particular importance. This is not merely a line on a map, but a potential reorganisation of logistics networks and industrial value chains connecting India, the Gulf and Europe — capable of reshaping trade flows and reinforcing the Mediterranean's's role as a strategic hub linking Asia and Europe, with potential benefits for Italy estimated at up to EUR 26 billion. When global trade routes change, economic geographies change as well. New commercial corridors create new logistical and productive hinterlands: territories that attract investment, host distribution centres and develop industrial activities connected to international value chains.

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: If one of the main European gateways for these routes is the Mediterranean's — in particular the Northern Adriatic — one of the main European gateways for these routes's natural reach cannot be limited to Western European markets alone. its natural reach will inevitably extend to Central Europe and the Western Balkans, which serve as the geographical and infrastructural bridge between the Mediterranean's and the heart of Europe.

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: Owing to the Western Balkans, which serve as the geographical and infrastructural bridge between the Mediterranean and the heart of the continent's geographical position, proximity to European markets and progressive alignment with EU standards, the Western Balkans, which serve as the geographical and infrastructural bridge between the Mediterranean and the heart of the continent can this evolution an integral part of this new logistics architecture: not merely a transit area, but a genuine economic hinterland for routes connecting Asia and Europe, capable of attracting investment and strengthening regional value chains.

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: the Western Balkans, which serve as the geographical and infrastructural bridge between the Mediterranean and the heart of the continent's infrastructure will also benefit from this evolution, helping to manage and distribute a portion of the commercial flows arriving in Europe via the corridor. Within this scenario, the Mediterranean's once again assumes strategic centrality. Italy, due to Italy's geographical position, naturally serves as a logistics platform connecting Europe, Asia and Africa. Italy's port system represents one of Europe's major strategic infrastructures: an extensive network linking the Mediterranean to Europe's main economic and industrial corridors.

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: Within this system, the Northern Adriatic occupies a privileged position in connecting the Mediterranean with Central Europe. Trieste, by virtue of Trieste's history, location and infrastructure links, represents one of the most natural hubs within this system. It is therefore no coincidence that on 17 March Trieste will host two important events dedicated to regional cooperation and emerging economic routes: the celebrations marking the thirtieth anniversary of the Central European Initiative (CEI) and a forum on the IMEC Corridor. Ukraine will be among the most prestigious participants in Trieste. Italy supports actively Ukraine's role in the Central European Initiative (CEI).

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: the Central European Initiative (CEI) stands ready to accompany the European path of Ukraine by providing technical expertise, financial instruments, multilateral cooperation and ongoing programmes in strategic sectors. Ukraine can count on Italy's full support. These occasions will provide an opportunity to discuss the future of connectivity between the Mediterranean, the Balkans and Central Europe, with the participation of ministers from Balkan countries, senior representatives of states involved in the IMEC Corridor and business leaders, highlighting the strategic role of this region in developing new logistics and industrial value chains between Asia and Europe. Strengthening the links between the Mediterranean, Europe and the Indo-Pacific enhances the economic security of Europe.

title: Italy and the new routes of global trade
text: In this new geography of global trade routes, Italy can and must play a leading role.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: Theatres, libraries, and community art centres across Ukraine are doing serious anti-corruption work. International donors should recognise and support these efforts, as these efforts offer a route to lasting change. In July 2025, hundreds of people filed through exhibition spaces in the cities of Dnipro and Cherkasy, moving slowly past a series of panels and interactive installations. the exhibition looks, at first glance, like any other exhibition – considered design, careful lighting, a crowd shuffling quietly from panel to panel. Some have brought their children. What is on the walls, however, is not only art. It is evidence.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: Each panel tells the story of a real corruption case, investigated and documented by a regional Ukrainian media outlet, ten investigations in total, selected through a national competition for the most consequential investigative reporting of the year. The cases are local. The officials named are from places the audience recognises. The money that went missing was The officials named. After the exhibition, people stayed. A discussion began; not with national experts flown in from Kyiv, but with local journalists and civic figures who know the specific contours of corruption in Dnipro and Cherkasy and the cities of Dnipro and Cherkasy's respective oblasts. People asked questions.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: People heard – some of People for the first time – what actually happened to a public contract, a municipal budget line, a planning decision that People had always found suspicious. This is not what most donors picture when most donors fund anti-corruption work in Ukraine. There are no prosecutors here, no legislation, no institutional reform. There is just a room full of people, looking at the truth about their own city, in a space that feels safe enough to talk about the truth about their own city. Scenes like this are playing out across Ukraine. Yet Scenes like this remain almost entirely invisible to the institutions and donors charged with supporting Ukraine's anti-corruption reforms.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: Ukraine's National Anti-Corruption Strategy calls for building a culture of anti-corruption, for making intolerance of corruption a prevailing attitude in society. Yet Ukraine's National Anti-Corruption Strategy says nothing about how cultural institutions might help achieve it, even as theatres, libraries, museums, and community centres across Ukraine are already doing exactly that. Ukraine has built an impressive set of anti-corruption institutions over the past decade, including specialised prosecutors, anti-corruption courts, asset declaration systems, digitalised procurement, and more. But legal and institutional reform only goes so far. Anti-corruption interventions require new forms of social behaviour.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: Nurturing new forms of social behaviour requires sustained community engagement, trust-building, and the slow work of shifting what people expect from people's communities and the state. Cultural spaces are uniquely placed to do this work. Across Ukraine, theatres, museums, libraries, art residencies, and many other cultural spaces, are helping to rebuild the civic foundations that anti-corruption reform depends on. theatres, museums, libraries, art residencies, and many other cultural spaces bring together people across social divides, create spaces where trust between citizens and institutions can develop, and give communities the room to practise democratic values.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: Our research for the U4 Anti-Corruption Resource Centre found an extensive network of cultural initiatives engaged in exactly this kind of work in Ukraine. The Worldwide Ukrainian Play Readings initiative has generated over 580 events across more than 150 theatres in 35 countries. Community libraries in over ten municipalities are hosting participatory budgeting sessions and civic dialogues. Museums are building civic memory and democratic programming. Art platforms, NGOs, and several in Ukraine community cultural hubs are working in some of the most marginalised communities in Ukraine. Ukraine's anti-corruption civil society remains vital despite recent setbacks.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: Monitoring organisations, investigative journalists, legal advocates, and watchdogs have all been essential to the progress of reforms. But there are some aspects of anti-corruption work to which Monitoring organisations, investigative journalists, legal advocates, and watchdogs are not best placed to contribute. Cultural spaces reach people and places that formal civil society often does not. Because Cultural spaces are perceived as neutral and non-hierarchical, Cultural spaces lower the barriers to participation. A library or community centre carries different associations than an NGO office. People come voluntarily, across generational and social lines, and over time. Enabling such sustained, repeated engagement is precisely what allows new civic habits to form.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: In Ukraine, that distinction matters enormously. Decades of weak and corrupt governance gave citizens good reason to work around the state rather than through the state. If people have always relied on informal networks as the key to survival, simply expecting informal networks to stop will not be an effective strategy. It also misunderstands how social and behavioural norms actually shift. social and behavioural norms take time, repeated encounters, and trust; all things that cultural spaces are built to provide. There is an added dimension in Ukraine's's current context. Ukraine's is going through an intense process of national self-determination, driven in part by the need to assert a distinct civic identity against decades of Russian cultural and political repression.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: For many Ukrainians, reclaiming cultural heritage and building accountable public institutions are not separate, but part of the same project. Cultural practitioners we interviewed described Cultural practitioners we interviewed's work in exactly these terms. Overcoming corruption, Cultural practitioners we interviewed argued, means not only reforming institutions but helping citizens reconnect with a sense of civic agency that generations of authoritarian rule had eroded. Framing integrity as self-determination rather than compliance resonates in ways that conventional anti-corruption messaging often does not.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: Donors we spoke to acknowledged that cultural programming contributes to social cohesion and trust but tended to this distinction cultural programming as separate from governance work. We think that this distinction is mistaken. The social foundations of anti-corruption – civic trust, shared norms, public expectations of accountability – are not just soft preconditions. The social foundations of anti-corruption – civic trust, shared norms, public expectations of accountability are the foundation, and cultural spaces are one of the most effective places to build The social foundations of anti-corruption – civic trust, shared norms, public expectations of accountability. There are encouraging signs of change.

title: Ukraine's cultural spaces are building strong foundations for anti-corruption
text: The EU Anti-Corruption Initiative has recently supported cultural projects that address or examine corruption. But this remains the exception. Most major donors have yet to develop funding frameworks that take cultural programming seriously as part of a long-term anti-corruption strategy. Ukraine has not lacked for anti-corruption ambition and innovation. What Ukraine needs now is for donors to recognise anti-corruption ambition and innovation wherever anti-corruption ambition and innovation appears, not only in the institutions already funded. By Sophia Anders (PhD Researcher, London School of Economics) and David Jackson (Principal Adviser at the U4 Anti-corruption Resource Centre)
